messages.content:
You are a flag detection model for government RFP screening. Your job is to identify which flags, if any, are present in the provided RFP text chunk.

You will be given:
- A list of relevant flag definitions and examples (retrieved context)
- An RFP text chunk to analyze

Rules:
- Output one flag name per line, using only the exact flag names listed below
- If no flags are present, output: no_flag
- Do not output explanations, reasoning, or any other text
- Only detect a flag when the evidence in the chunk is explicit — do not infer from weak signals

Valid flags:
waterfall_methodology, off_the_shelf_software, no_custom_development, lpta_source_selection,
small_business_set_aside, 8a_set_aside, wosb_set_aside, edwosb_set_aside, sdvosb_set_aside,
hubzone_set_aside, agile_methodology, oral_presentation, design_exercise, budget_too_low,
brownfield, onsite_required, onsite_madison, large_team, marginal_short_duration, no_flag

[Retrieved context]

Example 1:
The Department of Education, Office of Federal Student Aid (FSA), announces a solicitation for continued development and operational support of the StudentAid.gov platform and its underlying data services. This contract represents continuing work on a platform that serves over 43 million borrowers and applicants annually. The selected contractor will receive a comprehensive handoff package from the incumbent contractor, including all source code repositories, infrastructure-as-code scripts, API documentation, and outstanding feature roadmap commitments. The incoming contractor must demonstrate the capacity to onboard into the existing system rapidly, with mission-critical services such as the Free Application for Federal Student Aid (FAFSA) processing pipeline remaining fully operational throughout transition. FSA's evaluation methodology includes an oral presentation component during which offerors will be asked to describe specific technical challenges encountered when taking over legacy codebases and the resolution approaches applied. Key personnel proposed for this effort must be available to begin transition activities within two weeks of contract award.

Example 2:
The Department of Education, Office of Elementary and Secondary Education (OESE), is seeking contractor support for the implementation of a grants management tracking and stakeholder communication system. The Government has completed its acquisition planning and determined that a commercial off-the-shelf solution built upon the Salesforce platform provides the optimal balance of cost, functionality, and security posture. The contractor shall configure Salesforce to support grant application intake, reviewer assignment workflows, award notification processes, and recipient reporting tracking. Integration with the Department's existing identity provider using SAML 2.0 federation is required. The contractor is explicitly prohibited from developing custom software components where configuration-based solutions exist within the platform's native capabilities. The Government will provide a licensed Salesforce environment; the contractor is responsible solely for configuration, testing, training, and go-live support. The acquisition is set aside for small businesses under NAICS 541511, though the primary deliverable remains platform configuration rather than software development.

Example 3:
The Social Security Administration (SSA) announces a competitive procurement for modernization and operations support services for the Disability Case Processing System (DCPS). Contractors responding to this solicitation must understand that this effort constitutes continuing work on a system first placed into production in 2016. The existing codebase, comprising front-end Angular components and back-end services built on IBM WebSphere, will be transferred to the incoming contractor in its current state. SSA does not anticipate a full redesign during the base period; rather, the contractor is expected to maintain, enhance, and incrementally modernize the existing system in accordance with the Technology Modernization Plan attached as Exhibit C. The Government will facilitate a structured transition from incumbent contractor Leidos, providing access to all repository assets, architectural documentation, and operations runbooks. Proposals must include a staffing plan that accounts for immediate knowledge absorption from outgoing personnel during a 120-day transition period.

---

RFP chunk to analyze:
The Department of Education, Office of the Chief Information Officer, seeks a qualified contractor to support the modernization of the Federal Student Aid (FSA) Applicant Data Services infrastructure. The scope of work encompasses the design and development of a new microservices-based processing engine to replace monolithic batch processing components within the FSA data pipeline, enabling near-real-time validation and routing of applicant financial records. The contractor shall integrate the new processing components with existing FSA enterprise systems including the Central Processing System (CPS) and Partner Participation Management System (PPMS) through documented and versioned internal APIs. Security requirements include compliance with FISMA Moderate controls, Privacy Impact Assessment (PIA) completion, and System of Records Notice (SORN) alignment for all personally identifiable information handled by the system. The contract type shall be Time and Materials with a not-to-exceed ceiling of $4.6 million over a 12-month base period and two 12-month option periods. The contractor shall hold or be able to obtain a FedRAMP Moderate authorized cloud service provider agreement within 90 days of contract award for any cloud resources introduced under this effort. Minimum required personnel include a Project Manager, Solution Architect, three Full Stack Developers, a Database Engineer, and a Security and Compliance Specialist. All key personnel resumes shall be submitted with the proposal and are subject to Government approval prior to assignment. Source code, documentation, and all work products shall be delivered as Government Purpose Rights. Evaluation criteria include Technical Approach, Staffing Plan and Key Personnel, Past Performance, and Price, with technical criteria outweighing price.
no_flag

[Retrieved context]

Example 1:
The Department of Homeland Security anticipates award of a single Indefinite Delivery Indefinite Quantity (IDIQ) contract to support cybersecurity operations at multiple field offices across the continental United States. The successful offeror shall provide no fewer than twelve (12) full-time equivalent personnel, including a Program Manager, Deputy Program Manager, four (4) Senior Cybersecurity Analysts, three (3) Network Engineers, and at least two (2) Vulnerability Assessment Specialists. All personnel must maintain active Secret clearances at time of contract award. The contractor shall ensure continuous coverage during core business hours (0700–1800 local time) and shall provide on-call support during off-hours for critical incident response. The Government anticipates that the breadth of work, spanning threat monitoring, penetration testing, compliance reporting, and stakeholder coordination, will necessitate the full complement of staff described herein throughout the base period and all option years. Offerors whose proposed staffing plans reflect fewer than twelve personnel will be deemed technically unacceptable and removed from further consideration. The offeror shall submit a staffing plan as Volume III of the technical proposal, clearly identifying each labor category, the individual proposed, and their respective roles and responsibilities in support of this requirement.

Example 2:
The Department of Energy, National Nuclear Security Administration, is issuing this solicitation for nuclear security program support services at a classified facility in the Southeast United States. The scope includes physical security assessments, force-on-force exercise planning and execution, vulnerability analysis, security technology evaluation, safeguards and security training development, and regulatory compliance support. Given the classified nature of operations, the 24-hour operational tempo of the facility, and the technical complexity of nuclear security disciplines, the contractor must provide a minimum of thirteen (13) cleared personnel at all times. The required team includes a Program Director with a minimum of twenty (20) years of combined military and nuclear security experience, a Deputy Program Director, three (3) Physical Security Specialists, two (2) Force-on-Force Exercise Specialists, two (2) Safeguards and Security Analysts, a Security Technology Engineer, a Training Specialist, and two (2) Compliance Analysts. All personnel must possess active Q clearances at time of award. Offerors shall submit a staffing plan demonstrating that all thirteen positions are filled by named individuals meeting the qualification requirements set forth in Section J, Attachment 3. Personnel proposed for this effort may be subject to Government approval prior to assignment.

Example 3:
The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, Baltimore District, is soliciting proposals for environmental remediation and site restoration services at a formerly used defense site located in the Mid-Atlantic region. Given the scale of contamination, the complexity of multi-media remediation (soil, groundwater, and sediment), and the requirement for concurrent work across three distinct operable units, the Government has determined that a minimum of fifteen (15) qualified personnel must be engaged on this contract at all times during active remediation phases. The required team shall include a licensed Professional Engineer serving as Project Manager, a Certified Industrial Hygienist, at minimum two (2) licensed Geologists, a Health and Safety Officer, four (4) field technicians, a quality control manager, and sufficient support staff to maintain documentation, community relations, and regulatory liaison functions. The period of performance is estimated at thirty-six (36) months with two (2) twelve-month option periods. Offerors shall demonstrate in their Management Plan that the proposed team possesses the depth of expertise and numerical sufficiency to execute overlapping work streams without degradation of quality or schedule. Failure to staff to the required minimum levels at any point during performance may constitute a material breach of contract.

---

RFP chunk to analyze:
The Department of Homeland Security, Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency, is soliciting proposals for the establishment and operation of a Critical Infrastructure Resilience and Technical Assistance Program serving state, local, tribal, and territorial partners across FEMA Regions IV and VI. The program encompasses vulnerability assessments, consequence-based tabletop exercises, cyber-physical risk analysis, stakeholder engagement, and the development of sector-specific resilience plans for owners and operators of lifeline infrastructure including water systems, transportation networks, energy distribution facilities, and communications infrastructure. The scale of the geographic coverage area, the diversity of infrastructure sectors within scope, and the intensity of the engagement schedule, projected to include no fewer than sixty (60) site visits and twelve (12) major exercise events annually, collectively establish a requirement for a large, multidisciplinary contractor team. The contractor shall provide a minimum of thirteen (13) full-time equivalent personnel dedicated to this program, including a Program Manager, a Deputy Program Manager with specialized cybersecurity expertise, two (2) Physical Security Specialists, two (2) Cybersecurity Advisors holding active CISSP or equivalent certifications, a Water Sector Subject Matter Expert, an Energy Sector Subject Matter Expert, a Transportation Sector Subject Matter Expert, a Communications Sector Subject Matter Expert, an Exercise Design and Facilitation Lead, and at least one (1) Report and Deliverable Production Specialist. The offeror shall describe in its Management Plan how the proposed team of thirteen or more personnel will be organized, tasked, and geographically positioned to ensure responsiveness to partner requests across both FEMA regions without undue delay or resource conflict.

[Retrieved context]

Example 1:
The contractor shall implement Agile development methodologies throughout the period of performance. All software development activities shall be conducted using Scrum framework, with two-week sprint cycles. The contractor shall conduct daily stand-up meetings, sprint planning sessions, sprint reviews, and sprint retrospectives in accordance with Scrum ceremonies. A certified Scrum Master shall be designated and responsible for facilitating all Agile ceremonies and removing impediments to team progress. The Government Product Owner shall participate in sprint planning and backlog grooming sessions to prioritize user stories and acceptance criteria. The contractor shall maintain a product backlog in a Government-approved project management tool and provide sprint velocity metrics as part of monthly progress reporting. All deliverables shall be produced incrementally, with a potentially shippable product increment demonstrated at the conclusion of each sprint. The contractor shall adhere to the Agile Manifesto principles and demonstrate continuous improvement through retrospective action items documented and tracked across sprints.

Example 2:
Pursuant to the Department of Defense's software acquisition pathway as defined in DoDI 5000.87, the contractor shall deliver capabilities using Agile software development methodology with continuous integration and continuous delivery pipelines. The contractor shall organize personnel into Agile squads operating in two-week sprints, with each squad responsible for a defined set of product backlog items. All Agile ceremonies, including sprint planning, daily standups, sprint reviews, and retrospectives, shall be conducted in person or via Government-approved collaboration tools. The contractor shall employ a certified Scrum Master for each squad who is accountable for coaching team members on Agile principles and ensuring adherence to Scrum practices. A product roadmap updated at minimum quarterly shall be maintained and shared with Government stakeholders to provide visibility into planned increments of capability. The use of Kanban boards or equivalent visual management tools is required to provide real-time transparency into work-in-progress, blocked items, and completed work. Sprint velocity shall be tracked and used to inform release planning and capacity projections.

Example 3:
The General Services Administration Office of Information Technology requires offerors to demonstrate proven experience delivering projects using Agile development practices. The period of performance shall be executed through iterative Agile sprints of two-week duration. The contractor shall implement all five Scrum events: Sprint Planning, Daily Scrum, Sprint Review, Sprint Retrospective, and the Sprint itself, with Government personnel invited and encouraged to participate in Sprint Reviews and Retrospectives. The contractor's Agile Coach shall work with the Government Product Owner to build and maintain a prioritized product backlog, ensuring that the highest-value features are delivered earliest. Acceptance testing shall be performed within the sprint in which development occurs, consistent with Agile testing best practices and shift-left quality principles. The contractor shall track and report on Agile health metrics including team happiness index, sprint goal achievement rate, and escaped defect rate. All tools used to manage the Agile process, including the backlog and sprint boards, shall be accessible to designated Government personnel at all times.

---

RFP chunk to analyze:
Background: The Department has observed significant interest across industry in adaptive project management concepts, including but not limited to iterative delivery, cross-functional teaming, and customer collaboration. The Government acknowledges the extensive body of literature supporting such approaches and notes their adoption across both commercial and public sector organizations. Notwithstanding this broader context, this acquisition is structured as a Firm-Fixed-Price (FFP) contract for the production of a defined set of technical documentation deliverables with fully specified content requirements, formats, and submission deadlines enumerated in Section F. Because deliverables are fixed in scope, schedule, and format, the rationale for prescribing any particular internal development or project management methodology does not apply to this effort. Contractors are free to organize their internal teams, workflows, and review processes as they see fit, including through the use of collaborative tools, digital kanban boards, or daily coordination meetings, provided that no additional cost is incurred by the Government and that all work products conform to the Documentation Standards Handbook, Version 4.2. No retrospectives, sprint reviews, or backlog grooming sessions shall be scheduled with Government personnel as a contract requirement. Contractor internal practices are solely the responsibility of the contractor and shall not be interpreted as Government-endorsed methodologies.
no_flag

Example 1:
The General Services Administration, Federal Acquisition Service, Region 6, issues this combined synopsis and solicitation for janitorial and custodial services at a single federal office building in Kansas City, Missouri, containing approximately 8,400 rentable square feet. The contract will be structured as a firm-fixed-price arrangement for a base year with no options. The maximum contract value, inclusive of all labor, supplies, equipment, and supervision for the twelve-month base period, is $38,500. This figure reflects the agency's available appropriations for facility maintenance services at this location. Prospective vendors must be capable of providing five-day-per-week service within this funding constraint. The GSA contracting officer has documented that market research supports the feasibility of fulfilling the described services within the $38,500 ceiling based on comparable awards at similar-sized facilities. Quoters whose proposed annual cost exceeds thirty-eight thousand five hundred dollars ($38,500) will be eliminated from consideration. Award will be made on a lowest-price, technically acceptable basis.

Example 2:
The General Services Administration, Public Buildings Service, Region 6, issues this solicitation as a COMPLETE SMALL BUSINESS SET-ASIDE. All offers must come from small business concerns as defined in FAR 2.101 and 13 C.F.R. Part 121. This procurement is for janitorial and custodial services at federally owned and leased buildings throughout the Kansas City metropolitan area. NAICS 561720 (Janitorial Services) applies with a $22.0 million average annual receipts size standard. In accordance with FAR 19.502-2, the Contracting Officer has determined that award can be made at fair market prices with adequate competition from small businesses. Offerors shall complete FAR 52.219-6 and ensure their small business certification in SAM.gov reflects the appropriate NAICS code at the time of offer and at the time of contract award. The Government will verify small business size representations and reserves the right to refer any suspected misrepresentation to the SBA for a formal size determination prior to award.

Example 3:
The General Services Administration, Public Buildings Service, hereby solicits proposals for facilities management support services at the Byron G. Rogers Federal Building, 1961 Stout Street, Denver, Colorado 80294. The contractor shall provide all services on-site at the Byron G. Rogers Federal Building. This includes, but is not limited to, janitorial services, building systems monitoring, visitor management, and physical security support. All contractor employees must maintain a continuous physical presence during assigned shifts within the building. The building operates on a 24-hour, 7-day-per-week schedule, and contractor coverage must meet this requirement accordingly. Personnel files and time and attendance records will be reviewed by the COR on a biweekly basis at the building management office. Contractors are expressly forbidden from performing any contract tasks from locations other than the specified federal building. Equipment used in performance of this contract shall remain on-site at all times unless written approval for removal is obtained from the Contracting Officer. Proposals must include a site-specific staffing plan demonstrating the offeror's capacity to maintain full on-site coverage throughout the entire base period and all option years.

---

RFP chunk to analyze:
The General Services Administration, Public Buildings Service, Rocky Mountain Region, issues this Request for Quotations for janitorial and custodial services at a single-tenant federal office facility in Cheyenne, Wyoming. The Government intends to award a firm-fixed-price contract for a base period of twelve (12) months, with no option periods included in this acquisition. The total not-to-exceed contract value for the base period is $91,750. This amount represents the full funding available for all cleaning services, supplies, equipment, supervision, and any incidental costs necessary to maintain the facility in accordance with the performance work statement attached hereto as Attachment A. GSA's contracting office has conducted independent market research confirming that the services described can be procured within this funding level in the current regional labor market. Offerors are cautioned that the Government has no mechanism to authorize expenditures beyond the stated ceiling of $91,750 and will not award a contract to any vendor whose total proposed price exceeds this amount. Quotes must include a complete unit price schedule and a summary total that clearly demonstrates compliance with the ninety-one thousand seven hundred fifty dollar ($91,750) maximum. This solicitation is a total small business set-aside pursuant to FAR Subpart 19.5.

Example 1:
The Department of Homeland Security, Office of the Chief Information Officer, hereby solicits proposals for an enterprise identity and access management solution. This requirement is for a COTS solution required to meet the agency's zero-trust architecture objectives. The Government will not fund, authorize, or accept delivery of any custom-developed software components. Offerors are advised that the scope of this engagement is configuration only, encompassing identity provider setup, multi-factor authentication policy definition, role-based access control mapping, and directory synchronization. All proposed software must be commercially available and actively maintained by the Offeror as a standard product offering. Licensing terms must reflect commercial market rates, and the product must receive regular security patches and version updates as part of the vendor's standard release cycle. Integration connectors used to link the platform with DHS legacy systems must be pre-built and commercially supported. Engineering of new connectors or middleware specifically for this contract is outside the permissible scope of work.

Example 2:
The Department of Homeland Security anticipates award of a single Indefinite Delivery Indefinite Quantity (IDIQ) contract to support cybersecurity operations at multiple field offices across the continental United States. The successful offeror shall provide no fewer than twelve (12) full-time equivalent personnel, including a Program Manager, Deputy Program Manager, four (4) Senior Cybersecurity Analysts, three (3) Network Engineers, and at least two (2) Vulnerability Assessment Specialists. All personnel must maintain active Secret clearances at time of contract award. The contractor shall ensure continuous coverage during core business hours (0700–1800 local time) and shall provide on-call support during off-hours for critical incident response. The Government anticipates that the breadth of work, spanning threat monitoring, penetration testing, compliance reporting, and stakeholder coordination, will necessitate the full complement of staff described herein throughout the base period and all option years. Offerors whose proposed staffing plans reflect fewer than twelve personnel will be deemed technically unacceptable and removed from further consideration. The offeror shall submit a staffing plan as Volume III of the technical proposal, clearly identifying each labor category, the individual proposed, and their respective roles and responsibilities in support of this requirement.

Example 3:
The General Services Administration (GSA) Federal Acquisition Service seeks a contractor to provide software development and maintenance support for the System for Award Management (SAM.gov). This effort requires the contractor to work directly within the existing system environment, which has been under continuous development since 2012. The incoming contractor will execute a transition from incumbent operations currently held by IBM Federal, taking full responsibility for all active development sprints, defect backlogs, and release management activities. The legacy codebase includes multiple subsystems developed across several prior contract periods, resulting in heterogeneous technology stacks that the contractor must be prepared to support concurrently. GSA anticipates that the first six months of the contract will be heavily weighted toward stabilization and documentation of the existing system before new feature development proceeds at full velocity. The Government's Quality Assurance Surveillance Plan includes specific metrics for transition risk during this stabilization phase.

---

RFP chunk to analyze:
The Department of Homeland Security, Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA), announces a solicitation for software engineering services in support of the Automated Indicator Sharing (AIS) Program Enhancement Project. The contractor shall design and develop enhancements to CISA's existing threat intelligence sharing platform, extending its capability to support STIX 2.1 and TAXII 2.1 protocol standards, improving participant onboarding workflows, and adding machine-learning-assisted indicator triage functionality. The Government envisions a hybrid team of contractor and Government personnel collaborating throughout the development lifecycle, with contractor personnel primarily working from their own facilities. The contract vehicle shall be a Cost-Plus-Incentive-Fee (CPIF) structure with a base period of 18 months and two 12-month option periods. Estimated total contract value across all periods is $5.1 million. The contractor shall maintain a software development plan, a risk register, and a cybersecurity test and evaluation plan updated throughout performance. All systems developed under this contract are subject to DHS 4300A Sensitive Systems Policy and must achieve ATO prior to production deployment. The contractor shall provide a minimum staffing complement consisting of a Program Manager, a Cybersecurity Architect, two Senior Software Engineers, a Data Scientist, and a Systems Integration Engineer. Proposals will be evaluated on Technical Approach and Understanding of the Requirement, Key Personnel, Past Performance, and Price, with technical factors combined being more important than price.

Example 1:
SOLICITATION: DEPARTMENT OF ENERGY — OFFICE OF ENVIRONMENTAL MANAGEMENT — SAVANNAH RIVER OPERATIONS OFFICE. This Request for Proposals is issued for specialized radioactive waste characterization support services at the Savannah River Site in Aiken, South Carolina. The period of performance is limited to eight (8) months from date of contract award, with no option periods contemplated. This requirement is being procured as a short-duration task to support accelerated characterization activities required for near-term regulatory compliance milestones. The contractor must provide a qualified radiological engineer, a health physics technician, and appropriate monitoring equipment on-site within fifteen (15) calendar days of award. All work shall conform to applicable DOE Orders, Nuclear Regulatory Commission standards, and site-specific radiological control procedures. The contractor shall deliver a final waste characterization report summarizing sampling results, dose assessments, and waste classification recommendations no later than the penultimate month of performance. Proposals shall be evaluated using best value criteria with technical and management approach equally weighted against price.

Example 2:
The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, Savannah District, is seeking qualified contractors to provide environmental remediation services at the Savannah District Hazardous Waste Site located at Fort Stewart, Georgia. All remediation activities shall be conducted at the designated project site on Fort Stewart, 1020 Liberator Drive, Hinesville, GA 31314. Contractor personnel, including all subcontractors and specialty technicians, must perform all work physically at the site. There is no provision for off-site data processing, laboratory coordination, or administrative functions to be conducted outside of the government-established project office trailer located at the remediation site. Soil sampling, groundwater monitoring, waste characterization, and reporting activities must all be carried out by personnel who are on-site and badged into the restricted work zone daily. The Army retains the right to conduct unannounced inspections to verify contractor presence and compliance with site safety plans. All field notes, chain-of-custody forms, and preliminary findings must be documented using paper logs maintained in the on-site project office. Digital submissions of final deliverables shall originate from the government network terminal provided within the on-site trailer.

Example 3:
The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, Baltimore District, is soliciting proposals for environmental remediation and site restoration services at a formerly used defense site located in the Mid-Atlantic region. Given the scale of contamination, the complexity of multi-media remediation (soil, groundwater, and sediment), and the requirement for concurrent work across three distinct operable units, the Government has determined that a minimum of fifteen (15) qualified personnel must be engaged on this contract at all times during active remediation phases. The required team shall include a licensed Professional Engineer serving as Project Manager, a Certified Industrial Hygienist, at minimum two (2) licensed Geologists, a Health and Safety Officer, four (4) field technicians, a quality control manager, and sufficient support staff to maintain documentation, community relations, and regulatory liaison functions. The period of performance is estimated at thirty-six (36) months with two (2) twelve-month option periods. Offerors shall demonstrate in their Management Plan that the proposed team possesses the depth of expertise and numerical sufficiency to execute overlapping work streams without degradation of quality or schedule. Failure to staff to the required minimum levels at any point during performance may constitute a material breach of contract.

---

RFP chunk to analyze:
COMBINED SYNOPSIS/SOLICITATION: The United States Army Corps of Engineers, South Atlantic Division, is issuing this solicitation for environmental site assessment and sampling services at a formerly used defense site located in Chatham County, Georgia. The period of performance is seven (7) months from the date of contract execution. This contract does not contain option periods and will not be extended beyond the established performance end date. The contractor shall conduct Phase II Environmental Site Assessments in accordance with ASTM Standard E1903, collect soil and groundwater samples at no fewer than forty designated locations, and submit a final Remedial Investigation report to the Contracting Officer's Representative within thirty (30) days of completing all field activities. Sampling and analysis protocols must comply with EPA Region 4 guidance and applicable Georgia Environmental Protection Division standards. The contractor shall maintain all required health and safety certifications for personnel conducting intrusive fieldwork. A project kickoff meeting shall be held within five (5) business days of award. Progress reports are required on a bi-weekly basis. Offerors shall submit a detailed project schedule demonstrating the ability to complete all required deliverables within the constrained seven-month performance window. Proposals will be evaluated using a best value tradeoff methodology considering technical approach, past performance, key personnel qualifications, and price.

Example 1:
The Department of Veterans Affairs seeks a qualified contractor to assume responsibility for the continuing work on the Veterans Benefits Management System (VBMS). The selected vendor will inherit the existing system architecture, currently maintained by the incumbent contractor whose period of performance concludes on September 30, 2024. The incoming contractor must demonstrate proven experience onboarding into a legacy codebase without service disruption. Transition activities shall include a mandatory 90-day knowledge transfer period during which the outgoing and incoming contractors will operate concurrently. The Government requires that the new contractor review all existing technical documentation, data dictionaries, and system design records prior to assuming full operational responsibility. Offerors must describe in their technical approach how they will manage risk during transition from incumbent operations, including how they will handle undocumented components of the existing system. Continuity of service to Veterans during the transition period is a non-negotiable requirement, and the Government will evaluate all proposals on the basis of transition risk mitigation strategies.

Example 2:
The Social Security Administration (SSA) announces a competitive procurement for modernization and operations support services for the Disability Case Processing System (DCPS). Contractors responding to this solicitation must understand that this effort constitutes continuing work on a system first placed into production in 2016. The existing codebase, comprising front-end Angular components and back-end services built on IBM WebSphere, will be transferred to the incoming contractor in its current state. SSA does not anticipate a full redesign during the base period; rather, the contractor is expected to maintain, enhance, and incrementally modernize the existing system in accordance with the Technology Modernization Plan attached as Exhibit C. The Government will facilitate a structured transition from incumbent contractor Leidos, providing access to all repository assets, architectural documentation, and operations runbooks. Proposals must include a staffing plan that accounts for immediate knowledge absorption from outgoing personnel during a 120-day transition period.

Example 3:
The General Services Administration (GSA) Federal Acquisition Service seeks a contractor to provide software development and maintenance support for the System for Award Management (SAM.gov). This effort requires the contractor to work directly within the existing system environment, which has been under continuous development since 2012. The incoming contractor will execute a transition from incumbent operations currently held by IBM Federal, taking full responsibility for all active development sprints, defect backlogs, and release management activities. The legacy codebase includes multiple subsystems developed across several prior contract periods, resulting in heterogeneous technology stacks that the contractor must be prepared to support concurrently. GSA anticipates that the first six months of the contract will be heavily weighted toward stabilization and documentation of the existing system before new feature development proceeds at full velocity. The Government's Quality Assurance Surveillance Plan includes specific metrics for transition risk during this stabilization phase.

The Department of Justice, Justice Management Division, is releasing this solicitation for information technology development and sustainment support for the Unified Financial Management System (UFMS). This procurement is structured as a recompete following the current contract term, and the selected vendor will be responsible for taking over an existing system that serves as the Department's primary financial management platform, processing in excess of $35 billion in annual transactions across 40 component bureaus and offices. The existing system is built on Oracle Federal Financials and has been customized extensively over multiple contract generations, resulting in a complex legacy codebase with several vendor-specific configurations that are not fully reflected in current system documentation. The Government requires that the incoming contractor lead a formal transition from incumbent operations, including the assumption of all active development work items, open defect backlogs, and pending change requests documented in the Jira project management environment. A mandatory Transition Readiness Review will be conducted by the Government at Day 30, Day 60, and Day 90 of the base period to assess the contractor's progress in achieving full operational independence. Offerors must include in their proposal a detailed Transition Plan that addresses staffing continuity, risk identification, and the methodology for validating completeness of knowledge transfer from the departing contractor. The Department will not authorize the outgoing contractor's release from transition obligations until the incoming contractor has certified full assumption of all operational responsibilities and the Government's Contracting Officer has issued a written acceptance of transition completion.

Example 1:
The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, Savannah District, is seeking qualified contractors to provide environmental remediation services at the Savannah District Hazardous Waste Site located at Fort Stewart, Georgia. All remediation activities shall be conducted at the designated project site on Fort Stewart, 1020 Liberator Drive, Hinesville, GA 31314. Contractor personnel, including all subcontractors and specialty technicians, must perform all work physically at the site. There is no provision for off-site data processing, laboratory coordination, or administrative functions to be conducted outside of the government-established project office trailer located at the remediation site. Soil sampling, groundwater monitoring, waste characterization, and reporting activities must all be carried out by personnel who are on-site and badged into the restricted work zone daily. The Army retains the right to conduct unannounced inspections to verify contractor presence and compliance with site safety plans. All field notes, chain-of-custody forms, and preliminary findings must be documented using paper logs maintained in the on-site project office. Digital submissions of final deliverables shall originate from the government network terminal provided within the on-site trailer.

Example 2:
The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, Baltimore District, is soliciting proposals for environmental remediation and site restoration services at a formerly used defense site located in the Mid-Atlantic region. Given the scale of contamination, the complexity of multi-media remediation (soil, groundwater, and sediment), and the requirement for concurrent work across three distinct operable units, the Government has determined that a minimum of fifteen (15) qualified personnel must be engaged on this contract at all times during active remediation phases. The required team shall include a licensed Professional Engineer serving as Project Manager, a Certified Industrial Hygienist, at minimum two (2) licensed Geologists, a Health and Safety Officer, four (4) field technicians, a quality control manager, and sufficient support staff to maintain documentation, community relations, and regulatory liaison functions. The period of performance is estimated at thirty-six (36) months with two (2) twelve-month option periods. Offerors shall demonstrate in their Management Plan that the proposed team possesses the depth of expertise and numerical sufficiency to execute overlapping work streams without degradation of quality or schedule. Failure to staff to the required minimum levels at any point during performance may constitute a material breach of contract.

Example 3:
The Department of Energy, Savannah River Site, Office of Environmental Management, requires nuclear facility deactivation and decommissioning support services at the Savannah River Site in Aiken, South Carolina. All contractor work shall be conducted at the Savannah River Site, located in Aiken, SC 29808. The radiological, nuclear safety, and national security considerations associated with this facility categorically preclude the performance of any work activities from off-site locations. All contractor personnel must complete site-specific radiological worker training, obtain a DOE security clearance as applicable, and physically badge into the controlled area assigned to their scope of work each day. Operations including equipment decontamination, building characterization surveys, waste packaging, and safety documentation must be carried out by personnel who are physically present within the facility boundaries. DOE Facility Representatives will conduct daily walk-throughs to verify contractor presence and work progress. All controlled documents, radiological survey records, and waste manifests must be maintained in the on-site document control center located within the project's designated trailer complex at the Savannah River Site.

The offeror's technical proposal must include a site mobilization plan that describes how the contractor will maintain a full complement of qualified personnel at the Savannah, Georgia location throughout each field sampling event and throughout all reporting periods.

Example 1:
DEPARTMENT OF DEFENSE
DEFENSE LOGISTICS AGENCY
SOLICITATION: SPE600-24-R-0412
FOOD SERVICE MANAGEMENT

This solicitation is issued as a Women-Owned Small Business (WOSB) set-aside. In accordance with FAR 19.1505 and the authority granted under the Small Business Act, the Defense Logistics Agency Troop Support has restricted this acquisition for food service management and dining facility operations to WOSB concerns only. Market research confirmed that a reasonable expectation exists that at least two responsible WOSB firms will submit offers and that award will be made at a fair and reasonable price within the meaning of FAR 19.1505(b). The NAICS code for this procurement is 722310 (Food Service Contractors) and the applicable size standard is $47.0 million in average annual receipts. Firms must be certified as Women-Owned Small Businesses through the SBA's certify.sba.gov portal and maintain that certification throughout the contract period. The contract will cover food service operations at two military installations in the Mid-Atlantic region, with a base period of performance of one year and four one-year option periods. The Government reserves the right to cancel this set-aside if it determines that insufficient competition exists among WOSB concerns. See Section L for complete proposal preparation instructions.

Example 2:
SOLICITATION NUMBER: HHS-2024-WOSB-0142

This acquisition is a Total Small Business Set-Aside restricted to Women-Owned Small Business (WOSB) concerns pursuant to 15 U.S.C. 637(m) and FAR Subpart 19.15. Only offers submitted by firms certified as Women-Owned Small Businesses under the SBA WOSB Federal Contract Program will be considered for award. The Government will evaluate proposals on a tradeoff basis, with technical approach and past performance weighted more heavily than price. NAICS 541511 applies with a corresponding small business size standard of $34 million. All responsible sources may submit a proposal.

Example 1:
The Department of Transportation, Federal Aviation Administration, solicits technical and price proposals for development of the Aeronautical Information Management Replacement System. Given the safety-critical nature of the system, the FAA mandates compliance with DO-178C and requires the use of traditional SDLC sequential phases throughout the program lifecycle. The contractor shall complete and obtain Government approval of all systems engineering artifacts associated with each development phase before proceeding to subsequent phases. The development sequence shall include: System Requirements Review, Software Requirements Review, Preliminary Design Review, Critical Design Review, Software Build and Integration, Software Qualification Testing, System Acceptance Testing, and Operational Readiness Review. A fixed requirements baseline shall be established following Government approval of the System and Software Requirements Specifications. The contractor shall employ rigorous phase gate controls, and no phase transition shall occur without documented Government authorization. Traceability matrices shall be maintained from requirements through test.

Example 2:
The Department of Veterans Affairs requires the contractor to employ a waterfall methodology for the development of the Veterans Benefits Management System Enhancement. All work shall proceed through sequential phases including requirements analysis, system design, implementation, testing, deployment, and maintenance. Each phase must be fully completed and formally accepted by the Contracting Officer's Representative prior to initiation of the subsequent phase. The contractor shall maintain a fixed requirements baseline established during the requirements phase, which shall not be altered without formal change control board approval and contract modification. Deliverables associated with each phase gate must be submitted, reviewed, and approved before any downstream development activities commence. The contractor shall provide a detailed Waterfall Project Plan within 30 days of contract award, identifying all phase entry and exit criteria, milestone dates, and associated deliverables. Monthly status reports shall track progress against the approved baseline schedule and document any variances exceeding five percent of planned effort.

Example 3:
The Department of Energy, National Nuclear Security Administration, requires that the contractor employ a waterfall methodology for the development of the Nuclear Material Accountability and Control Information System. This requirement reflects NNSA policy mandating structured, auditable development processes for systems handling Safeguards and Security information. The contractor shall execute all software development under clearly defined sequential phases: Requirements Definition, Functional Design, Detailed Design, Software Construction, Software Testing, and System Deployment. Phase gate reviews shall be scheduled and completed at the conclusion of each phase, with a formal Government Decision Authority review required before transition to the next phase. All requirements identified during the Requirements Definition phase shall be placed under configuration control as the fixed requirements baseline. Changes to this baseline require submission of a formal Request for Change, independent cost and schedule impact assessment, and written approval from the NNSA Contracting Officer's Representative and Configuration Control Board.

The contractor shall execute all development activities in strict accordance with a waterfall methodology, as mandated by FAA Order 1370.82A governing safety-critical system development. Work shall proceed through the following sequential phases in the order specified: Mission Need Analysis, System Requirements Definition, Preliminary System Design, Detailed Design, Software Implementation, Integration Testing, Operational Test and Evaluation, and Deployment. The contractor shall not commence any phase until the preceding phase has been formally closed, all required deliverables have been accepted in writing by the Contracting Officer's Representative, and a written Authorization to Proceed has been issued by the Government. This plan shall include all phase entry and exit criteria, planned milestone dates, key deliverable submission schedules, and resource loading by phase. Progress shall be assessed at monthly program reviews against the approved baseline schedule, with written explanation required for any schedule variance exceeding ten percent of planned phase duration.

Oral presentations are designed to enable the Government to evaluate the offeror's technical capabilities, proposed methodologies, and the experience of key personnel in a direct interactive format. The FBI will schedule oral presentations at Headquarters in Washington, D.C., and travel costs shall be borne by the offeror. Each presentation shall be strictly limited to sixty (60) minutes, followed by a thirty (30) minute question-and-answer period facilitated by the Source Selection Advisory Council. Evaluation results from the oral presentation will be documented and retained as part of the official source selection record.

Presentations shall be limited to sixty (60) minutes with an additional twenty (20) minutes reserved for Government questions. The lead proposed Program Manager and at least two key technical personnel identified in the proposal must participate in person. Scores assigned during the oral presentation phase will be integrated with written proposal scores to produce a final technical rating for each offeror.

The contractor shall employ a certified Scrum Master for each squad who is accountable for coaching team members on Agile principles and ensuring adherence to Scrum practices. The use of Kanban boards or equivalent visual management tools is required to provide real-time transparency into work-in-progress, blocked items, and completed work. Sprint velocity shall be tracked and used to inform release planning and capacity projections.

Example 2:
The Department of Veterans Affairs requires the selected vendor to employ Agile/Scrum methodology for all development efforts under this task order. The contractor shall organize development personnel into cross-functional Agile teams, each consisting of a Scrum Master, developers, testers, and business analysts. Sprint duration shall be fixed at two weeks, and the contractor shall deliver working software at the end of each sprint for Government review and acceptance. The contractor shall conduct sprint reviews with VA stakeholders to demonstrate completed user stories against agreed-upon Definition of Done criteria. Backlog refinement sessions shall occur at minimum once per sprint to ensure the product backlog reflects current priorities and is sufficiently groomed for upcoming sprints. The contractor shall provide release planning documentation outlining the expected delivery of features across program increments. Agile metrics including sprint burndown charts, velocity, and cumulative flow diagrams shall be reported to the Contracting Officer's Representative on a bi-weekly basis.

Example 3:
The General Services Administration Office of Information Technology requires offerors to demonstrate proven experience delivering projects using Agile development practices. The period of performance shall be executed through iterative Agile sprints of two-week duration. The contractor shall implement all five Scrum events: Sprint Planning, Daily Scrum, Sprint Review, Sprint Retrospective, and the Sprint itself, with Government personnel invited and encouraged to participate in Sprint Reviews and Retrospectives. The contractor's Agile Coach shall work with the Government Product Owner to build and maintain a prioritized product backlog, ensuring that the highest-value features are delivered earliest. Acceptance testing shall be performed within the sprint in which development occurs, consistent with Agile testing best practices and shift-left quality principles. The contractor shall track and report on Agile health metrics including team happiness index, sprint goal achievement rate, and escaped defect rate. All tools used to manage the Agile process, including the backlog and sprint boards, shall be accessible to designated Government personnel at all times.

The anticipated period of performance is three years from the date of award, inclusive of options.

The contractor shall implement Agile development methodologies throughout the period of performance. All software development activities shall be conducted using Scrum framework, with two-week sprint cycles. The contractor shall conduct daily stand-up meetings, sprint planning sessions, sprint reviews, and sprint retrospectives in accordance with Scrum ceremonies. A certified Scrum Master shall be designated and responsible for facilitating all Agile ceremonies and removing impediments to team progress. The Government Product Owner shall participate in sprint planning and backlog grooming sessions to prioritize user stories and acceptance criteria. The contractor shall maintain a product backlog in a Government-approved project management tool and provide sprint velocity metrics as part of monthly progress reporting. All deliverables shall be produced incrementally, with a potentially shippable product increment demonstrated at the conclusion of each sprint. The contractor shall adhere to the Agile Manifesto principles and demonstrate continuous improvement through retrospective action items documented and tracked across sprints.

Example 2:
The Department of Veterans Affairs requires the selected vendor to employ Agile/Scrum methodology for all development efforts under this task order. The contractor shall organize development personnel into cross-functional Agile teams, each consisting of a Scrum Master, developers, testers, and business analysts. Sprint duration shall be fixed at two weeks, and the contractor shall deliver working software at the end of each sprint for Government review and acceptance. The contractor shall conduct sprint reviews with VA stakeholders to demonstrate completed user stories against agreed-upon Definition of Done criteria. Backlog refinement sessions shall occur at minimum once per sprint to ensure the product backlog reflects current priorities and is sufficiently groomed for upcoming sprints. The contractor shall provide release planning documentation outlining the expected delivery of features across program increments. Agile metrics including sprint burndown charts, velocity, and cumulative flow diagrams shall be reported to the Contracting Officer's Representative on a bi-weekly basis.

Example 3:
The Social Security Administration requires all IT development work performed under this Blanket Purchase Agreement to comply with SSA's Agile Development Standards. Offerors shall describe their Agile delivery model in the technical approach and demonstrate how their proposed methodology aligns with Scrum or Kanban frameworks. Contractors shall conduct sprint planning meetings with SSA's Product Owner to decompose epics into sprint-ready user stories with acceptance criteria no later than the iteration planning session preceding each sprint. The contractor shall update the Government's enterprise project management tool daily with sprint board status, impediments, and burndown data. Demonstration of working software to SSA stakeholders shall occur at the end of every sprint, and the Government reserves the right to redirect backlog priorities following each sprint review based on mission needs. The contractor shall implement Definition of Ready criteria to ensure user stories are sufficiently detailed prior to sprint commitment. Quarterly business reviews shall include Agile performance metrics, including average sprint velocity, defect density, and sprint goal completion percentage.

The contractor shall not substitute waterfall, modified waterfall, or other sequential development approaches without prior written approval from the Contracting Officer. Scrum teams shall be sized between five and nine members, inclusive of the Scrum Master, and shall maintain dedicated team membership for the duration of each program increment to preserve team velocity and minimize onboarding disruption. The contractor shall conduct sprint planning ceremonies at the start of each two-week sprint, decomposing prioritized product backlog items into sprint backlog tasks with associated time estimates. Daily standup meetings shall occur at a consistent time and location agreed upon with the Government, and the Scrum Master shall document and actively work to resolve all identified impediments. The TSA Product Owner, or an authorized delegate, shall participate in backlog refinement sessions held at minimum biweekly to maintain a groomed and prioritized backlog extending at least two sprints ahead of the current iteration. Sprint reviews shall include live demonstrations of all completed user stories against their acceptance criteria, and Government stakeholders shall formally accept or reject each story during the review session. The contractor shall deliver cumulative flow diagrams, sprint burndown charts, and release burnup charts to the program office on a biweekly basis to maintain transparency into delivery progress and forecast remaining work.

Each offeror will be given access to a standardized environmental monitoring dataset and tasked with producing a data quality analysis report and visualization dashboard within a three-hour period. The hands-on exercise will be conducted at EPA's Research Triangle Park facility. Offerors must bring all necessary hardware and licensed software; EPA will provide only network connectivity and access to the standardized dataset via a secure file transfer mechanism. Evaluators will assess the quality, accuracy, and clarity of the analysis report and dashboard outputs using criteria specified in Section M.4. Offerors are encouraged to review the Technical Evaluation Criteria in Section M prior to initiating prototype development. All prototype submissions must be delivered via secure government portal no later than 5:00 PM Eastern Time on the date specified in the solicitation timeline.

Example 1:
The Department of Defense, Defense Logistics Agency, requires development of the Materiel Tracking and Accountability System under a waterfall development model consistent with MIL-STD-498 and DoD 5000.02 acquisition guidelines. The contractor shall conduct all software development activities in strict sequential phases including Software Requirements Analysis, Preliminary Design, Detailed Design, Coding and Unit Testing, Computer Software Component Integration and Testing, Computer Software Configuration Item Testing, and System Integration and Testing. The V-model verification and validation framework shall be applied throughout, with each left-side development phase paired with a corresponding right-side testing activity. Phase gate reviews, including the System Requirements Review, Preliminary Design Review, and Critical Design Review, shall be conducted at prescribed milestones. Entrance and exit criteria for each phase gate are defined in Attachment J-3. No phase may commence without written Government approval documenting satisfaction of all exit criteria from the preceding phase.

Example 2:
Under this task order issued against the NASA Solutions for Enterprise-Wide Procurement contract, the contractor shall manage and execute all software development activities in accordance with Agile methodology. The contractor shall implement a Scrum-based framework and shall operate all development teams on a uniform two-week sprint cadence synchronized with the program's release train schedule. Sprint planning shall be completed within four hours for a two-week sprint, and the contractor shall ensure that sprint goals are clearly articulated and agreed upon by the Government Product Owner prior to sprint commencement. The contractor shall maintain a Definition of Done that includes code review completion, unit test coverage thresholds, security scanning, and Product Owner acceptance. Working software shall be deployed to a staging environment at the end of each sprint for Government validation. The contractor shall produce and maintain an Agile project charter documenting team norms, Agile ceremony schedules, tooling agreements, and escalation procedures. Release planning artifacts including feature roadmaps and program increment objectives shall be reviewed and approved by the NASA Contracting Officer's Representative on a quarterly basis.

Example 3:
The Department of Veterans Affairs seeks a qualified contractor to assume responsibility for the continuing work on the Veterans Benefits Management System (VBMS). The selected vendor will inherit the existing system architecture, currently maintained by the incumbent contractor whose period of performance concludes on September 30, 2024. The incoming contractor must demonstrate proven experience onboarding into a legacy codebase without service disruption. Transition activities shall include a mandatory 90-day knowledge transfer period during which the outgoing and incoming contractors will operate concurrently. The Government requires that the new contractor review all existing technical documentation, data dictionaries, and system design records prior to assuming full operational responsibility. Offerors must describe in their technical approach how they will manage risk during transition from incumbent operations, including how they will handle undocumented components of the existing system. Continuity of service to Veterans during the transition period is a non-negotiable requirement, and the Government will evaluate all proposals on the basis of transition risk mitigation strategies.

The Department of Education, Office of the Chief Information Officer, requires the contractor to lead and execute a modernization initiative using Agile Scrum practices in alignment with the agency's Technology Modernization Playbook. The contractor shall staff an Agile delivery team that includes at minimum a Scrum Master, Product Manager, UX Researcher, Front-End and Back-End Developers, and a Quality Assurance Engineer. All work shall be organized into a continuously refined product backlog with epics, features, and user stories sized and prioritized by the Government Product Owner in collaboration with the contractor. Sprint cycles shall be two weeks in duration, and the contractor shall present a sprint demo to agency stakeholders at each sprint review, with attendance tracked and documented. The Scrum Master shall facilitate sprint retrospectives and shall maintain a retrospective action log accessible to the Government. Agile metrics shall be reported in the contractor's monthly status report, including planned versus completed story points, sprint goal achievement rate, and the number of impediments raised and resolved. The contractor shall provide Agile coaching support to Government personnel as a secondary deliverable to build internal agency capacity.

Example 2:
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services requires the contractor to apply Agile methodology across all workstreams supporting the modernization of legacy claims processing systems. The contractor shall organize work into user stories, epics, and themes within a hierarchical backlog structure and shall demonstrate traceability from individual user stories to program-level epics and strategic objectives. Sprints shall be conducted on a two-week cadence with sprint planning occurring on the first day of each sprint and sprint review and retrospective conducted on the final day. The Government's designated Product Owner shall have authority to accept or reject sprint deliverables based on the agreed Definition of Done. The contractor shall not carry incomplete user stories across sprint boundaries without documented justification and re-estimation. The contractor shall facilitate quarterly Agile maturity assessments using a Government-approved framework and shall present findings and improvement recommendations to the CMS program office. Test-Driven Development (TDD) and Continuous Integration practices shall be employed as integral components of the Agile engineering workflow.

Example 3:
The Department of Veterans Affairs requires the selected vendor to employ Agile/Scrum methodology for all development efforts under this task order. The contractor shall organize development personnel into cross-functional Agile teams, each consisting of a Scrum Master, developers, testers, and business analysts. Sprint duration shall be fixed at two weeks, and the contractor shall deliver working software at the end of each sprint for Government review and acceptance. The contractor shall conduct sprint reviews with VA stakeholders to demonstrate completed user stories against agreed-upon Definition of Done criteria. Backlog refinement sessions shall occur at minimum once per sprint to ensure the product backlog reflects current priorities and is sufficiently groomed for upcoming sprints. The contractor shall provide release planning documentation outlining the expected delivery of features across program increments. Agile metrics including sprint burndown charts, velocity, and cumulative flow diagrams shall be reported to the Contracting Officer's Representative on a bi-weekly basis.

The Department of Housing and Urban Development, Office of the Chief Information Officer, mandates that the contractor employ Agile development methodology for the modernization of the HUD Integrated Data System described in this solicitation. FOOD SERVICE MANAGEMENT

Offerors must ensure that the proposed Project Manager, Lead Systems Architect, and at least one subject matter expert in clinical data integration are physically present at the VA facility designated by the Contracting Officer. Requests for remote participation will not be considered except under extraordinary circumstances as determined solely by the Contracting Officer. Offerors shall submit all presentation materials, including slide decks and supporting documentation, to the Contracting Officer no later than seventy-two (72) hours prior to the scheduled session. Materials submitted after this deadline will not be permitted during the presentation. All information conveyed during the oral presentation, including verbal statements and demonstrated materials, will be considered part of the offeror's formal proposal submission and evaluated under Factor 1 – Technical Approach and Factor 3 – Key Personnel. The VA reserves the right to record oral presentations for documentation purposes. Scoring derived from the oral presentation phase will be combined with written proposal scores to generate a composite technical rating used in the best value determination.

The contractor shall configure and deploy the selected commercial platform, ensuring full compliance with NOAA digital branding standards, Section 508 accessibility requirements, and applicable federal web policy. Deliverables include site architecture documentation, configured and populated site with a minimum of 50 content pages, staff training for designated content editors, and a 12-month support retainer for platform administration. Example 2:
The Department of Homeland Security (DHS) Science and Technology Directorate is soliciting proposals for the implementation of a cloud-based project portfolio management capability to support research program tracking across the Directorate. Following a formal analysis of alternatives, the Government has selected a commercial solution based on Salesforce Project Management modules as the technical baseline. The primary work under this contract is configuring and deploying the selected commercial off-the-shelf platform to meet DHS-specific reporting, resource allocation, and milestone tracking requirements. The contractor shall configure dashboards, automated notifications, role-based access controls, and integration connectors to existing financial management systems. Offerors shall describe their approach to Salesforce configuration without reliance on custom Apex code development except where configuration-only approaches are technically infeasible and documented as such. The Government anticipates a total level of effort of approximately 2,800 hours across the base year, with options for ongoing platform administration and enhancement.

Example 3:
The Department of Energy Office of Environmental Management hereby solicits proposals for a project controls and earned value management system to support cleanup program oversight at multiple national laboratory sites. This acquisition is structured as a commercial item purchase under FAR Subpart 12.3 and requires a COTS solution required to be delivered without modification to core application logic. No software development is permitted under this contract. Offerors who propose to build custom modules, develop proprietary integrations requiring new code, or enhance existing product functionality through engineering effort will be found non-compliant. The permissible scope of work is configuration only and includes definition of work breakdown structures, cost account mapping, schedule baseline setup, earned value calculation method selection using product-native options, and report layout customization within the platform's standard reporting environment. The DOE contracting officer's representative will serve as the primary configuration authority and must approve all system settings prior to go-live. A configuration management plan shall be submitted within thirty days of contract award.

The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, National Weather Service, is soliciting proposals for the development of a custom Environmental Data Ingestion and Visualization Platform to support operational meteorologists across regional forecast offices. The Government seeks a contractor capable of architecting and implementing a scalable data pipeline that ingests heterogeneous sensor telemetry from buoy networks, upper-air observation stations, and satellite-derived datasets, normalizing records into a unified geospatial data schema. The platform shall provide an interactive browser-based visualization interface enabling forecasters to overlay multiple data layers, configure custom alert thresholds, and export formatted reports for public dissemination. The contractor shall leverage cloud-native infrastructure hosted within a FedRAMP-authorized environment and implement role-based access controls consistent with NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 requirements. The acquisition is structured as a cost-plus-fixed-fee contract with a twelve-month base period and two twelve-month option periods. Source selection will be conducted using a best value tradeoff approach considering technical approach, past performance, and cost, with technical factors weighted more heavily than cost in the evaluation. Offerors must demonstrate prior experience developing data ingestion architectures handling ingestion rates exceeding one million records per hour, as well as experience with geospatial data formats including NetCDF, GeoJSON, and GeoTIFF. The contractor team shall include personnel with domain familiarity in atmospheric science or environmental monitoring to ensure data schema designs meet operational requirements. All software deliverables shall be government-owned upon completion, and the contractor shall provide complete technical documentation, version-controlled source code, and knowledge transfer sessions prior to closeout.

The Social Security Administration, Office of Systems, issues this Request for Proposal for the Disability Determination Services Workflow Automation Project. The contractor shall manage all development activities in accordance with SSA's Enterprise Systems Development Methodology, which prescribes a waterfall approach to system delivery. Project execution shall follow sequential phases as follows: Business Requirements Definition, Logical Design, Physical Design, Construction, System Integration Testing, User Acceptance Testing, and Production Deployment. Each phase produces mandatory deliverables that must receive formal written acceptance from the SSA Project Manager before work on the subsequent phase is authorized to begin. The contractor shall establish and maintain a fixed requirements baseline upon Government acceptance of the Business Requirements Specification. All proposed modifications to baselined requirements shall be submitted via the SSA Change Request process and are subject to impact assessment, Configuration Control Board review, and written approval prior to incorporation.

Example 2:
The Department of Homeland Security, Office of the Chief Information Officer, solicits proposals for the modernization of the Personnel Security Adjudication Platform. The Government mandates use of a traditional SDLC approach consistent with DHS policy directives governing enterprise system development. The contractor shall execute the project through clearly delineated sequential phases: Concept of Operations, Requirements Definition, Preliminary Design, Critical Design, Code and Unit Test, Integration and Test, System Acceptance Test, and Operational Deployment. A formal phase gate review shall be conducted at the conclusion of each phase, with Government approval required before work proceeds. Requirements established at the conclusion of the Requirements Definition phase shall constitute the fixed requirements baseline and shall be placed under configuration management control. No development work shall begin prior to Government approval of the System Requirements Specification document.

Example 3:
The Department of Veterans Affairs requires the contractor to employ a waterfall methodology for the development of the Veterans Benefits Management System Enhancement. All work shall proceed through sequential phases including requirements analysis, system design, implementation, testing, deployment, and maintenance. Each phase must be fully completed and formally accepted by the Contracting Officer's Representative prior to initiation of the subsequent phase. The contractor shall maintain a fixed requirements baseline established during the requirements phase, which shall not be altered without formal change control board approval and contract modification. Deliverables associated with each phase gate must be submitted, reviewed, and approved before any downstream development activities commence. The contractor shall provide a detailed Waterfall Project Plan within 30 days of contract award, identifying all phase entry and exit criteria, milestone dates, and associated deliverables. Monthly status reports shall track progress against the approved baseline schedule and document any variances exceeding five percent of planned effort.

Example 1:
The Department of Veterans Affairs intends to award a contract for janitorial and custodial services at the Phoenix VA Medical Center. The Government will evaluate offers using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process. Technical acceptability will be determined on a pass/fail basis. Offerors must demonstrate compliance with all technical requirements outlined in the Performance Work Statement. Price will be evaluated for reasonableness and balance. The Government will award a contract to the responsible offeror whose offer conforms to the solicitation requirements and represents the lowest price technically acceptable. No tradeoffs will be made between technical merit and price. Offerors are advised that the Government does not intend to pay a premium for technical proposals that exceed the minimum requirements. Only proposals determined to be technically acceptable will be considered for award. Offerors should not propose capabilities beyond those required, as such additions will not enhance the probability of award under this LPTA evaluation methodology.

Example 2:
DEPARTMENT OF VETERANS AFFAIRS
VETERANS HEALTH ADMINISTRATION
SOLICITATION: 36C10X24R0221

PURCHASE TYPE: This requirement is a TOTAL SERVICE-DISABLED VETERAN-OWNED SMALL BUSINESS (SDVOSB) SET-ASIDE pursuant to 38 U.S.C. 8127 and 38 CFR Part 74, as implemented by VAAR 819.7005. The Veterans Health Administration (VHA), Network Contracting Office 20, is procuring laundry and linen management services for the VA Puget Sound Health Care System, Seattle Division and American Lake Division. Services include collection, laundering, pressing, folding, and return delivery of patient linens, surgical textiles, and staff uniforms on a recurring basis. NAICS code 812320 (Drycleaning and Laundry Services, except Coin-Operated) applies with a size standard of $8 million. Pursuant to 38 U.S.C. 8127(d), a contract may be awarded only to a verified Service-Disabled Veteran-Owned Small Business that is listed in the VA Vendor Information Pages (VIP) database as verified. Offerors must maintain VIP verification status through the date of award. Award will be on a best-value tradeoff basis considering technical approach, past performance, and price. Estimated contract value is between $500,000 and $2,000,000 over the full period of performance.

Example 3:
The Naval Facilities Engineering Systems Command, Southwest, hereby solicits offers for refuse collection, recycling, and solid waste management services at Naval Base San Diego. This procurement is conducted using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection methodology. Technical evaluation criteria are defined as Acceptable or Unacceptable and include: compliance with all federal, state, and local environmental regulations; demonstration of required equipment and staffing levels; and proof of required licenses and permits. Price evaluation will consider the total evaluated price across the base period and all option years. Award shall be made to the responsible offeror whose proposal is technically acceptable and offers the lowest price. The Government is not seeking best value tradeoffs and will not award to a higher-priced technically acceptable offeror under any circumstances. Offerors must thoroughly address all technical criteria to avoid an Unacceptable rating, which would preclude award regardless of pricing submitted.

The physical and operational security demands of this mission-critical federal facility require that all personnel engaged under this contract be present at the port of entry during all hours of contract performance. No work may be conducted from off-site locations. Personnel must complete CBP-specific security training and receive a facility access badge before performing any duties. The contractor shall maintain a dedicated on-site supervisor during all operational shifts. Maintenance logs, inspection records, and service reports shall be completed on-site and submitted to the COR through the government's facility management system, which is accessible only from terminals located within the port of entry.
