Specifically, each offeror must submit a prototype submission demonstrating their proposed citizen-facing web portal solution. The prototype submission shall be a fully navigable, hosted demonstration environment accessible via government-provided credentials. The prototype must include functional user registration, document upload, and status tracking modules. GSA evaluators will engage directly with the prototype during a structured two-hour evaluation session and will simulate end-user workflows as described in Attachment D, User Scenario Matrix. Prototype submissions are due no later than fourteen (14) days prior to the scheduled oral presentations. Offerors are advised that the prototype submission will be evaluated independently from the written technical volume and carries a weight of thirty-five percent (35%) of the total technical score. Any prototype that is unavailable, incomplete, or non-functional during the evaluation session will receive a rating of Unacceptable for that factor.

Example 2:
In accordance with the evaluation methodology described herein, the Department of Veterans Affairs requires each offeror to deliver a prototype submission of their proposed electronic health record integration module. The prototype submission shall demonstrate bidirectional data exchange between the offeror's platform and a VA-supplied test instance of the existing legacy system. Offerors must configure and host the prototype in a government-authorized cloud environment prior to the evaluation period. VA technical evaluators will conduct a structured review session lasting no more than three (3) hours, during which they will execute pre-defined integration test scripts and evaluate system responses. The prototype submission will be used to validate claims made in the written technical proposal; any discrepancies between the written proposal and demonstrated prototype functionality may result in a downward adjustment to the technical rating. Offerors must submit prototype access credentials to the Contracting Officer no fewer than five (5) business days before the scheduled evaluation session. Detailed instructions for the VA test environment configuration are provided in Section J, Attachment 7.

Example 3:
The Department of Defense, Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency, hereby solicits proposals under this Broad Agency Announcement and requires all offerors selected for Phase II negotiations to complete a mandatory prototype submission. The prototype submission shall constitute a functional hardware or software artifact demonstrating feasibility of the proposed technical approach as described in Section 3.2 of this solicitation. DARPA program managers will evaluate each prototype submission during a dedicated review session, which may include independent laboratory testing, performance benchmarking, and direct technical questioning of the offeror team. The prototype submission evaluation will assess innovation, technical merit, and readiness level advancement relative to the baseline technology described in the solicitation background. Offerors must ship hardware prototypes to the designated government laboratory no fewer than ten (10) business days prior to the evaluation session. Software prototypes must be delivered via approved media and must execute without modification in the government-provided test environment. DARPA evaluators retain the right to attempt to replicate offeror-reported performance results using independent test procedures.

The contractor shall implement a Scrum-based framework and shall operate all development teams on a uniform two-week sprint cadence synchronized with the program's release train schedule. Sprint planning shall be completed within four hours for a two-week sprint, and the contractor shall ensure that sprint goals are clearly articulated and agreed upon by the Government Product Owner prior to sprint commencement. The contractor shall maintain a Definition of Done that includes code review completion, unit test coverage thresholds, security scanning, and Product Owner acceptance. Working software shall be deployed to a staging environment at the end of each sprint for Government validation. The contractor shall produce and maintain an Agile project charter documenting team norms, Agile ceremony schedules, tooling agreements, and escalation procedures. Release planning artifacts including feature roadmaps and program increment objectives shall be reviewed and approved by the NASA Contracting Officer's Representative on a quarterly basis.

This solicitation is issued to procure comprehensive moving and relocation services for federal agency personnel and office equipment associated with a consolidation effort at multiple facilities in the Washington, D.C. metropolitan area. The period of performance shall be five (5) months from the date of award. This procurement does not include option periods, and the Government does not anticipate any need for extension given the defined scope of the relocation project. The contractor shall develop and submit a detailed move management plan within ten (10) days of award, coordinate all logistics with agency POCs, provide all necessary packing materials and specialized handling equipment, and complete all relocations in accordance with the approved schedule. All moves involving classified materials or sensitive equipment shall be conducted under escort and in strict compliance with applicable GSA and agency security protocols. Proposals must include a technical approach narrative, a staffing and resources plan, and a price schedule reflecting all anticipated labor, equipment, and materials costs.

Example 2:
The General Services Administration, Public Buildings Service, announces this solicitation for facility operations and maintenance services for a portfolio of seventeen federally owned buildings located in the National Capital Region. The combined gross square footage of the portfolio exceeds 4.2 million square feet, encompassing office buildings, courthouses, and a federal laboratory campus. To ensure adequate coverage and rapid response to maintenance needs across this geographically dispersed portfolio, the contractor shall provide a minimum staffing complement of thirty (30) full-time personnel. Required positions include a Contract Manager, an Operations Manager, six (6) licensed journeyman electricians, four (4) HVAC mechanics holding EPA 608 Universal certification, three (3) plumbers, two (2) elevator mechanics, two (2) fire suppression system technicians, a locksmith, a roofing specialist, and sufficient general maintenance workers to support all preventive and corrective maintenance tasks. The contractor shall also provide administrative staff to support work order management and reporting. All personnel shall be physically located within the National Capital Region and available to respond to emergency calls within two (2) hours of notification. Staffing plans must demonstrate no gaps in coverage for any building within the portfolio.

Example 3:
The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, Baltimore District, is soliciting proposals for environmental remediation and site restoration services at a formerly used defense site located in the Mid-Atlantic region. Given the scale of contamination, the complexity of multi-media remediation (soil, groundwater, and sediment), and the requirement for concurrent work across three distinct operable units, the Government has determined that a minimum of fifteen (15) qualified personnel must be engaged on this contract at all times during active remediation phases. The required team shall include a licensed Professional Engineer serving as Project Manager, a Certified Industrial Hygienist, at minimum two (2) licensed Geologists, a Health and Safety Officer, four (4) field technicians, a quality control manager, and sufficient support staff to maintain documentation, community relations, and regulatory liaison functions. The period of performance is estimated at thirty-six (36) months with two (2) twelve-month option periods. Offerors shall demonstrate in their Management Plan that the proposed team possesses the depth of expertise and numerical sufficiency to execute overlapping work streams without degradation of quality or schedule. Failure to staff to the required minimum levels at any point during performance may constitute a material breach of contract.

The Department of Transportation, Federal Aviation Administration, solicits technical and price proposals for development of the Aeronautical Information Management Replacement System. Given the safety-critical nature of the system, the FAA mandates compliance with DO-178C and requires the use of traditional SDLC sequential phases throughout the program lifecycle. The contractor shall complete and obtain Government approval of all systems engineering artifacts associated with each development phase before proceeding to subsequent phases. The development sequence shall include: System Requirements Review, Software Requirements Review, Preliminary Design Review, Critical Design Review, Software Build and Integration, Software Qualification Testing, System Acceptance Testing, and Operational Readiness Review. A fixed requirements baseline shall be established following Government approval of the System and Software Requirements Specifications. The contractor shall employ rigorous phase gate controls, and no phase transition shall occur without documented Government authorization. Traceability matrices shall be maintained from requirements through test.

This effort requires the contractor to work directly within the existing system environment, which has been under continuous development since 2012. The incoming contractor will execute a transition from incumbent operations currently held by IBM Federal, taking full responsibility for all active development sprints, defect backlogs, and release management activities. The legacy codebase includes multiple subsystems developed across several prior contract periods, resulting in heterogeneous technology stacks that the contractor must be prepared to support concurrently. GSA anticipates that the first six months of the contract will be heavily weighted toward stabilization and documentation of the existing system before new feature development proceeds at full velocity. The Government's Quality Assurance Surveillance Plan includes specific metrics for transition risk during this stabilization phase.

Customs and Border Protection
Office of Acquisition
Solicitation Number: 70B04C24R00000021
Subject: Uniform Apparel Procurement

PROCUREMENT NOTICE — HUBZONE SET-ASIDE:

This requirement is designated as a HUBZone small business set-aside. Pursuant to 15 U.S.C. § 657a and implementing regulations at 13 C.F.R. Part 126, this solicitation is restricted to offerors that are certified HUBZone small business concerns. The Contracting Officer has conducted market research and concluded that the HUBZone set-aside is appropriate given the availability of qualified HUBZone firms in the uniform manufacturing industry. FAR clause 52.219-3, Notice of HUBZone Set-Aside or Sole Source Award, is incorporated into this solicitation. The applicable NAICS code is 315990 with a small business size standard of 500 employees. The required staffing configuration shall include a Program Manager with PMP certification, a Deputy Program Manager, a Help Desk Manager overseeing a staff of at least four (4) Tier I and Tier II support specialists, three (3) application developers, two (2) database administrators, a network engineer, a cybersecurity analyst, a data warehouse specialist, and a configuration management specialist. The contractor shall maintain this minimum staffing level throughout all base and option periods. Proposed personnel must successfully complete a DOJ background investigation prior to being granted access to DOJ networks and systems. The staffing plan, including résumés for all eighteen key and non-key personnel, shall be submitted with Volume II of the technical proposal.

The Social Security Administration (SSA) announces a competitive procurement for modernization and operations support services for the Disability Case Processing System (DCPS). Contractors responding to this solicitation must understand that this effort constitutes continuing work on a system first placed into production in 2016. The existing codebase, comprising front-end Angular components and back-end services built on IBM WebSphere, will be transferred to the incoming contractor in its current state. SSA does not anticipate a full redesign during the base period; rather, the contractor is expected to maintain, enhance, and incrementally modernize the existing system in accordance with the Technology Modernization Plan attached as Exhibit C. The Government will facilitate a structured transition from incumbent contractor Leidos, providing access to all repository assets, architectural documentation, and operations runbooks. Proposals must include a staffing plan that accounts for immediate knowledge absorption from outgoing personnel during a 120-day transition period.

Example 3:
The Department of Veterans Affairs seeks a qualified contractor to assume responsibility for the continuing work on the Veterans Benefits Management System (VBMS). The selected vendor will inherit the existing system architecture, currently maintained by the incumbent contractor whose period of performance concludes on September 30, 2024. Transition activities shall include a mandatory 90-day knowledge transfer period during which the outgoing and incoming contractors will operate concurrently. The Government requires that the new contractor review all existing technical documentation, data dictionaries, and system design records prior to assuming full operational responsibility. Offerors must describe in their technical approach how they will manage risk during transition from incumbent operations, including how they will handle undocumented components of the existing system. Continuity of service to Veterans during the transition period is a non-negotiable requirement, and the Government will evaluate all proposals on the basis of transition risk mitigation strategies.

The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, Omaha District, is seeking proposals for facility maintenance and repair services at Camp Ashland, Nebraska. The source selection methodology for this procurement is Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA). Offerors must submit a Technical Volume and a Price Volume. The Technical Volume will be evaluated to determine whether the offeror's proposed approach meets the minimum requirements of the Performance Work Statement. Ratings will be limited to Acceptable or Unacceptable. An offeror receiving an Unacceptable technical rating will not be eligible for award. Price will be evaluated for completeness, reasonableness, and balance. Award will be made to the offeror with the lowest evaluated price whose technical proposal has been rated Acceptable. The use of LPTA in this acquisition is consistent with the determination that the requirement is well-defined and risk of unsuccessful performance is minimal. The Government does not anticipate the need for discussions.

Example 2:
The Environmental Protection Agency, Office of Acquisition Solutions, is issuing this Request for Proposals for technical support services related to water quality monitoring and sampling at Superfund sites in EPA Region 5. This acquisition will be evaluated using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process in accordance with FAR 15.101-2. Technical acceptability will be evaluated under three pass/fail criteria: Key Personnel Qualifications, Laboratory Accreditation Compliance, and Quality Assurance Plan Adequacy. Proposals must be rated Acceptable on all three criteria to be eligible for award. Technical proposals that exceed minimum requirements will receive no additional consideration. Following determination of technical acceptability, award will be made to the responsible offeror submitting the lowest evaluated total price. The Government has made a written determination that LPTA is the appropriate evaluation approach given the well-defined nature of the work and the availability of qualified contractors capable of performing at minimum standards. Questions regarding the LPTA evaluation criteria must be submitted by the deadline specified in Section L.

Example 3:
The Department of Veterans Affairs intends to award a contract for janitorial and custodial services at the Phoenix VA Medical Center. The Government will evaluate offers using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process. Technical acceptability will be determined on a pass/fail basis. Offerors must demonstrate compliance with all technical requirements outlined in the Performance Work Statement. Price will be evaluated for reasonableness and balance. The Government will award a contract to the responsible offeror whose offer conforms to the solicitation requirements and represents the lowest price technically acceptable. No tradeoffs will be made between technical merit and price. Offerors are advised that the Government does not intend to pay a premium for technical proposals that exceed the minimum requirements. Only proposals determined to be technically acceptable will be considered for award. Offerors should not propose capabilities beyond those required, as such additions will not enhance the probability of award under this LPTA evaluation methodology.

All software development activities shall be conducted using Scrum framework, with two-week sprint cycles. A certified Scrum Master shall be designated and responsible for facilitating all Agile ceremonies and removing impediments to team progress. The Government Product Owner shall participate in sprint planning and backlog grooming sessions to prioritize user stories and acceptance criteria. The contractor shall maintain a product backlog in a Government-approved project management tool and provide sprint velocity metrics as part of monthly progress reporting. All deliverables shall be produced incrementally, with a potentially shippable product increment demonstrated at the conclusion of each sprint. The contractor shall adhere to the Agile Manifesto principles and demonstrate continuous improvement through retrospective action items documented and tracked across sprints.

The period of performance shall be executed through iterative Agile sprints of two-week duration. The contractor's Agile Coach shall work with the Government Product Owner to build and maintain a prioritized product backlog, ensuring that the highest-value features are delivered earliest. Acceptance testing shall be performed within the sprint in which development occurs, consistent with Agile testing best practices and shift-left quality principles. The contractor shall track and report on Agile health metrics including team happiness index, sprint goal achievement rate, and escaped defect rate. All tools used to manage the Agile process, including the backlog and sprint boards, shall be accessible to designated Government personnel at all times.

The contractor shall conduct sprint reviews with VA stakeholders to demonstrate completed user stories against agreed-upon Definition of Done criteria. Backlog refinement sessions shall occur at minimum once per sprint to ensure the product backlog reflects current priorities and is sufficiently groomed for upcoming sprints. The General Services Administration Office of Information Technology requires the contractor to utilize Agile methodology as the primary software development approach for all work performed under this Blanket Purchase Agreement. The contractor shall establish and maintain cross-functional Scrum teams composed of a certified Scrum Master, Product Owner proxy, software developers, quality assurance engineers, and DevSecOps personnel. All development activities shall be executed in two-week sprint cycles, with sprint ceremonies including sprint planning, daily stand-ups, sprint reviews, and sprint retrospectives conducted in accordance with the Scrum Guide. The Government-designated Product Owner shall participate in backlog grooming and sprint planning sessions and shall hold authority to accept or reject completed user stories based on pre-established acceptance criteria. The contractor shall maintain a prioritized product backlog in JIRA or another GSA-approved Agile lifecycle management tool accessible to all Government stakeholders. Sprint velocity, sprint burndown charts, and release burnup charts shall be submitted to the Contracting Officer's Representative as part of bi-weekly status reporting. Working, tested software increments shall be demonstrated to Government stakeholders at each sprint review, and no deliverable shall be considered complete unless it satisfies the team's documented Definition of Done. The contractor shall foster a culture of continuous improvement, with retrospective action items tracked and reported across consecutive sprints to demonstrate measurable process enhancement.

EXHIBIT A – TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS SUMMARY

Background: The Department is seeking vendor support for a multi-year longitudinal public health survey effort. Previous rounds of this survey were administered through a hybrid model involving in-person data collection teams deployed to designated metropolitan statistical areas, including the Madison, WI MSA, which served as one of twelve sentinel sites during the prior contract period. Survey interviewers under the previous contract were required to maintain physical presence within their assigned MSA and conduct face-to-face household interviews.

Current Scope Modification: Following a comprehensive program evaluation, the agency has restructured the survey methodology to eliminate in-person interviewing requirements entirely. The current procurement covers telephone-based and web-assisted survey administration only. Contractor personnel conducting outreach and interviews may be located anywhere within the United States and are not required to reside in, travel to, or maintain any physical presence within any of the previously designated sentinel cities, including but not limited to Madison, Wisconsin; Columbus, Ohio; or Portland, Oregon.

This change reflects updated epidemiological guidance and lessons learned regarding remote data collection validity. Offerors shall demonstrate experience managing distributed, remote interviewer workforces and should not propose staffing models predicated on geographic co-location with survey target populations. Proposals describing onsite regional office infrastructure within specific cities will not receive additional technical credit and may be considered non-responsive to the streamlined delivery model described herein.

Example 1:
Proposals submitted in response to this solicitation will undergo a multi-phase evaluation process. Phase I consists of written technical and price proposal review. Offerors achieving a combined written evaluation score that places them within the competitive range will advance to Phase II, which requires an oral presentation before the Department of Transportation Technical Review Board. The oral presentation phase is designed to supplement written proposals by allowing offerors to demonstrate technical depth, address evaluator questions regarding proposed approaches, and introduce key personnel who will execute the contract. Oral presentations will be evaluated under Factor 1 – Technical Approach and Factor 3 – Key Personnel, and scores derived from the oral presentation will be weighted equally with written proposal scores for those factors. Each presentation is allocated ninety (90) minutes, with no more than sixty (60) minutes reserved for the offeror's presentation and the remainder designated for Government questions. The offeror's Project Manager and at least two senior technical staff members named in the proposal must be physically present.

Example 2:
Offerors in the competitive range shall be required to participate in an oral presentation as part of the evaluation process. The oral presentation will be conducted at the Department of Veterans Affairs Network Contracting Office and will be scheduled at the Government's discretion. The purpose of the oral presentation is to assess each offeror's understanding of the Performance Work Statement requirements, proposed staffing model, and quality control methodology. Presentations shall be a maximum of seventy-five (75) minutes in duration, which includes a mandatory fifteen (15) minute question-and-answer session conducted by the Technical Evaluation Panel. The proposed Contract Program Manager must attend and lead the presentation. Supporting staff may participate but shall not exceed four (4) individuals total. All slide materials or visual aids must be provided to the Contracting Officer no later than three (3) business days prior to the presentation. The Government may record oral presentations for evaluation documentation purposes, and offerors will be notified accordingly.

Example 3:
As part of the evaluation methodology for this Information Technology Services procurement, the General Services Administration (GSA) will incorporate oral presentations into the source selection process. After an initial review of written proposals, offerors receiving a rating of Acceptable or higher under the Technical Factor will be invited to participate in an oral presentation before the Technical Evaluation Team. The oral presentation allows the Government to assess offeror capabilities beyond what is conveyed in written form and to evaluate proposed personnel directly. Each offeror will be allotted ninety (90) minutes total: sixty (60) minutes for the presentation and thirty (30) minutes for Government-directed questions. Key personnel named in Section H must be present and actively participate. Substitutions of key personnel for the oral presentation will not be permitted without prior written approval and may negatively impact the evaluation. Offerors should note that the oral presentation is evaluated separately under Factor 2 – Management Approach and scored on an Adjectival basis consistent with the criteria described in Section M.

RFP chunk to analyze:
3.4 Oral Presentation Component. The U.S. Department of Transportation (DOT), Federal Transit Administration, has incorporated an oral presentation requirement into the evaluation methodology for this procurement. Following the completion of initial written proposal evaluations, offerors whose submissions are deemed competitive will be invited to deliver a structured oral presentation to a panel of Government evaluators. The oral presentation is designed to evaluate the offeror's understanding of multimodal transit systems integration requirements and the qualifications of personnel proposed for critical roles. Each presentation shall be limited to fifty (50) minutes of offeror-led content, followed by a twenty (20) minute period for evaluator questions. The proposed Program Manager, Lead Systems Engineer, and at least one other key technical staff member named in the written proposal must participate in person. Participation by telephone or audio-only conferencing will not be permitted under any circumstances. Offerors may employ visual aids, live demonstrations, or prototype displays during the presentation, provided that all such materials are pre-approved by the Contracting Officer and submitted for review no fewer than five (5) business days in advance. Scores derived from the oral presentation evaluation will be integrated with written proposal scores under the Technical Merit factor as outlined in Section M.3 of this solicitation. The Government will document oral presentation proceedings and may record sessions for internal evaluation purposes only. Offerors will not receive copies of recordings or evaluator notes.

Example 1:
The Department of Homeland Security, Customs and Border Protection (CBP), solicits proposals for the sustainment and modernization of the Automated Commercial Environment (ACE), the primary trade processing system for all import and export activity at U.S. ports of entry. This acquisition requires the selected contractor to assume full responsibility for an existing system that processes more than $3 trillion in trade annually and interfaces with more than 48 Partner Government Agencies. The transition from the incumbent contractor must be executed without interrupting trade processing operations. The legacy codebase spans over 15 years of accumulated development and includes components written in COBOL, Java EE, and more recent cloud-native services. Offerors must submit a Transition Risk Management Plan as a separate volume of their proposal, describing in detail how they will manage knowledge transfer from the current team, maintain the existing system during transition, and begin prioritized modernization activities in the base period.

Example 2:
The Department of Veterans Affairs seeks a qualified contractor to assume responsibility for the continuing work on the Veterans Benefits Management System (VBMS). The selected vendor will inherit the existing system architecture, currently maintained by the incumbent contractor whose period of performance concludes on September 30, 2024. The incoming contractor must demonstrate proven experience onboarding into a legacy codebase without service disruption. Transition activities shall include a mandatory 90-day knowledge transfer period during which the outgoing and incoming contractors will operate concurrently. The Government requires that the new contractor review all existing technical documentation, data dictionaries, and system design records prior to assuming full operational responsibility. Offerors must describe in their technical approach how they will manage risk during transition from incumbent operations, including how they will handle undocumented components of the existing system. Continuity of service to Veterans during the transition period is a non-negotiable requirement, and the Government will evaluate all proposals on the basis of transition risk mitigation strategies.

Example 3:
The Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) hereby solicits proposals for the continued development and sustainment of the National Flood Insurance Program's Policy Management Platform. This acquisition represents a transition from incumbent contractor DXC Technology, whose base period of performance expires in the fourth quarter of Fiscal Year 2025. The incoming contractor will be responsible for taking over an existing system that processes in excess of five million policyholder records annually. The Government has prepared a Transition Plan framework that the selected contractor must execute, including the migration of all active development branches, issue tracking records, and CI/CD pipeline configurations. Offerors are advised that the existing system was built on a microservices architecture deployed on Amazon Web Services GovCloud and that familiarity with this environment is expected. The technical evaluation criteria will weight heavily the offeror's demonstrated experience assuming operational control of complex legacy systems under compressed timelines.

The Department of Homeland Security, Customs and Border Protection (CBP) is soliciting proposals for the sustained development, operations, and maintenance of the Automated Targeting System (ATS). The selected contractor will assume full responsibility for continuing work previously performed under Task Order HSBP1019F00123, with the incumbent contractor's period of performance concluding no later than March 31, 2025. The incoming contractor must be prepared to take ownership of the existing system, which encompasses a distributed architecture spanning multiple classified and unclassified network enclaves. CBP will provide Government-Furnished Information at contract award, including all available technical documentation, architecture diagrams, and operational runbooks compiled by the outgoing team. Offerors should be aware that portions of the legacy codebase predate current agency coding standards and may contain undocumented business logic critical to mission operations. The Government anticipates a 120-day transition period during which the incumbent and incoming contractors will operate concurrently to ensure continuity of border security screening functions. Proposals must include a detailed Transition-In Plan describing how the offeror will systematically inventory, assess, and document all components of the existing system within the first 45 days of performance. The Government will evaluate technical proposals on the demonstrated ability of the offeror to absorb a complex, multi-tiered legacy codebase while maintaining uninterrupted operational availability. Any degradation in system performance attributable to transition activities will be considered adversely in past performance assessments.

Example 1:
This solicitation is issued by the General Services Administration, Public Buildings Service, for grounds maintenance and landscaping services at federal facilities in the National Capital Region. Source selection will be conducted using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) methodology as prescribed in FAR 15.101-2. Evaluation factors include Technical Capability and Price. Technical Capability will be rated either Acceptable or Unacceptable based on the offeror's demonstrated ability to meet the requirements of the Statement of Work. Price will not be evaluated for technical merit. Award will be made to the offeror submitting the lowest price among all technically acceptable proposals. Proposals rated Unacceptable under the Technical Capability factor will be eliminated from consideration regardless of price. The Government reserves the right to conduct discussions if necessary but intends to make award without discussions. All offerors must be registered in SAM.gov at time of offer submission.

Example 2:
The Department of Agriculture, Forest Service, issues this solicitation for tree marking, timber cruising, and silvicultural services in the Tongass National Forest, Alaska. Source selection will be accomplished using the lowest price technically acceptable methodology. The Technical Factor will assess whether the offeror has demonstrated the minimum qualifications, past relevant experience, and equipment necessary to perform the work as described in the Statement of Work. Technical acceptability determinations will be made on a pass/fail basis only. Price will be evaluated as the total price for all contract line items over the ordering period. Among all offerors determined to be technically acceptable, the award will be issued to the offeror presenting the lowest price. The Government has determined that LPTA is appropriate for this acquisition because the work is routine in nature, performance requirements are clearly defined, and successful performance risk is low. Offerors are cautioned that elaborate technical proposals will not improve their competitive standing.

Example 3:
The Department of Veterans Affairs intends to award a contract for janitorial and custodial services at the Phoenix VA Medical Center. The Government will evaluate offers using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process. Technical acceptability will be determined on a pass/fail basis. Offerors must demonstrate compliance with all technical requirements outlined in the Performance Work Statement. Price will be evaluated for reasonableness and balance. The Government will award a contract to the responsible offeror whose offer conforms to the solicitation requirements and represents the lowest price technically acceptable. No tradeoffs will be made between technical merit and price. Offerors are advised that the Government does not intend to pay a premium for technical proposals that exceed the minimum requirements. Only proposals determined to be technically acceptable will be considered for award. Offerors should not propose capabilities beyond those required, as such additions will not enhance the probability of award under this LPTA evaluation methodology.

---

RFP chunk to analyze:
SECTION M – EVALUATION FACTORS FOR AWARD

Award will be made to the offeror whose proposal represents the Best Value to the Government considering the following factors listed in descending order of importance: (1) Technical Approach, (2) Management Approach and Staffing Plan, (3) Past Performance, and (4) Price/Cost.

M.4 PAST PERFORMANCE EVALUATION. The Government will evaluate the relevance and quality of each offeror's past performance record. Relevant experience is defined as contracts or task orders of similar scope, complexity, and dollar magnitude to this requirement. The Government notes for informational purposes that contracts or task orders valued below $100,000 will generally be considered of limited relevance given the scale of the current requirement, valued at approximately $6,300,000 over the full period of performance. Such references are not disqualifying but will receive lower relevance ratings compared to examples that more closely approximate the scope and budget of this acquisition. Offerors should prioritize submitting past performance references for work valued at $500,000 or greater. The Contracting Officer's Representative will review CPARS records, contact provided references, and may consider any other credible information available regarding offeror performance history. Price will not be evaluated for technical acceptability but must be fair, reasonable, and realistic as determined through the cost/price analysis conducted by the Contracting Officer.

Example 1:
REQUEST FOR PROPOSALS — DEPARTMENT OF THE INTERIOR, BUREAU OF RECLAMATION, LOWER COLORADO REGION. The Bureau of Reclamation requires hydrological survey and water quality assessment services for a series of monitoring stations located along the Colorado River corridor in Nevada and Arizona. The period of performance for this contract is seven (7) months, beginning on or about the anticipated award date identified in this solicitation. This acquisition does not include any option periods or provisions for extension beyond the stated seven-month duration. The contractor shall conduct bi-weekly field assessments, collect water samples in accordance with EPA-approved protocols, and submit laboratory analysis results to the Contracting Officer's Representative within five (5) business days of collection. A final comprehensive technical report summarizing all findings, trend analyses, and recommendations shall be delivered no later than two weeks before contract expiration. The Government will evaluate proposals based on technical approach, key personnel qualifications, and price. Proposals are limited to fifty pages excluding resumes and certifications.

Example 2:
SOLICITATION NOTICE: The National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA), Goddard Space Flight Center, Office of Procurement, is seeking a qualified contractor to support the development and delivery of prototype sensor calibration hardware for the Earth Science Division. The period of performance for this effort is limited to eight (8) months from date of award. This is a firm-fixed-price, short-term developmental contract and does not include option periods. The contractor shall deliver a minimum of three functional prototype units, all associated technical documentation, test reports, and a lessons-learned summary. All deliverables must be submitted to the NASA Contracting Officer's Representative no later than the final month of performance. Milestone reviews are scheduled at months two, five, and seven. Due to the compressed schedule, offerors must demonstrate immediate availability of key personnel including a Systems Engineer and Hardware Integration Lead. Proposals shall be evaluated on technical capability, schedule feasibility, and price.

Example 3:
The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, Baltimore District, is soliciting proposals for environmental remediation and site restoration services at a formerly used defense site located in the Mid-Atlantic region. Given the scale of contamination, the complexity of multi-media remediation (soil, groundwater, and sediment), and the requirement for concurrent work across three distinct operable units, the Government has determined that a minimum of fifteen (15) qualified personnel must be engaged on this contract at all times during active remediation phases. The required team shall include a licensed Professional Engineer serving as Project Manager, a Certified Industrial Hygienist, at minimum two (2) licensed Geologists, a Health and Safety Officer, four (4) field technicians, a quality control manager, and sufficient support staff to maintain documentation, community relations, and regulatory liaison functions. The period of performance is estimated at thirty-six (36) months with two (2) twelve-month option periods. Offerors shall demonstrate in their Management Plan that the proposed team possesses the depth of expertise and numerical sufficiency to execute overlapping work streams without degradation of quality or schedule. Failure to staff to the required minimum levels at any point during performance may constitute a material breach of contract.

RFP chunk to analyze:
The Department of the Interior, Bureau of Reclamation, Upper Colorado Basin Region, is issuing this Request for Proposals for professional hydrological modeling and drought impact assessment services in support of the Colorado River Basin Water Supply and Demand Study. The period of performance for this contract shall be nine (9) months from the date of contract execution. The Government has determined that no option periods are warranted given the scope and deliverable structure of this requirement. Contractors shall develop and calibrate hydrologic simulation models for three sub-basin areas, conduct scenario-based drought stress testing under projected climate conditions, and produce a final technical report with policy-relevant recommendations for water resource managers. The contractor must demonstrate prior experience with Variable Infiltration Capacity (VIC) or equivalent hydrologic modeling platforms and familiarity with Bureau of Reclamation data standards. Monthly progress reports and a mid-performance technical briefing to Bureau leadership are required. All data, models, and documentation produced under this contract shall be delivered as government-owned work product in formats specified by the Contracting Officer's Representative. This solicitation is open to all responsible sources. Proposals will be evaluated using a technically acceptable, lowest-price methodology. Questions regarding this solicitation must be submitted in writing to the designated Contract Specialist no later than ten (10) calendar days prior to the proposal due date.

DISTRICT OPERATIONS SUPPORT

SET-ASIDE DETERMINATION: Pursuant to Federal Acquisition Regulation Part 19.1405 and FAR clause 52.219-27, this solicitation is designated as a 100% Service-Disabled Veteran-Owned Small Business (SDVOSB) set-aside. The U.S. Small Business Administration, Office of Administrative Services, is seeking administrative and clerical support staffing services for SBA district offices in the Mid-Atlantic region, including offices located in Maryland, Virginia, Delaware, and the District of Columbia. The applicable NAICS code is 561110 (Office Administrative Services) with a size standard of $12 million. Only offerors that certify their status as a Service-Disabled Veteran-Owned Small Business in SAM.gov and comply with the ownership and control requirements of 13 CFR Part 128 are eligible to receive an award under this solicitation. In evaluating offers, the contracting officer will verify SDVOSB certifications prior to any award decision. Concerns that cannot demonstrate compliance with the definition of a Service-Disabled Veteran-Owned Small Business as stated in FAR 2.101 will be found ineligible for award. The government anticipates awarding a single-award time-and-materials task order contract with a one-year base period and up to three one-year option periods.

DEPARTMENT OF ENERGY
NATIONAL NUCLEAR SECURITY ADMINISTRATION
SOLICITATION: DE-SOL-0015872

THIS ACQUISITION IS A TOTAL SET-ASIDE FOR SERVICE-DISABLED VETERAN-OWNED SMALL BUSINESS (SDVOSB) FIRMS. Pursuant to FAR 52.219-27 and FAR Subpart 19.14, the National Nuclear Security Administration (NNSA) is soliciting offers from qualified SDVOSB concerns for cybersecurity assessment and penetration testing services supporting NNSA facilities. The period of performance consists of a base year and four option years. NAICS code 541519 (Other Computer Related Services) applies, with a small business size standard of $34 million. To qualify under this set-aside, firms must represent that they are a Service-Disabled Veteran-Owned Small Business as defined in FAR 2.101, meaning the concern is at least 51 percent owned and controlled by one or more service-disabled veterans. Contracting Officer's representative will verify SDVOSB status through SAM.gov prior to award. Offerors are advised that misrepresentation of SDVOSB status may result in criminal penalties, suspension, or debarment proceedings. Security clearance requirements at the SECRET level will apply to contract personnel performing on-site work.

The General Services Administration, Federal Acquisition Service, Region 4, is seeking professional facilities management and operations and maintenance services for federal buildings located throughout the southeastern United States. Offerors must also be registered and active in the System for Award Management (SAM.gov) with a valid Unique Entity Identifier (UEI). Joint ventures must demonstrate compliance with the WOSB joint venture requirements set forth at 13 CFR 127.506 and must include their joint venture agreement with the proposal submission.

The contractor shall implement Agile development methodologies throughout the period of performance. All software development activities shall be conducted using Scrum framework, with two-week sprint cycles. The contractor shall conduct daily stand-up meetings, sprint planning sessions, sprint reviews, and sprint retrospectives in accordance with Scrum ceremonies. A certified Scrum Master shall be designated and responsible for facilitating all Agile ceremonies and removing impediments to team progress. The Government Product Owner shall participate in sprint planning and backlog grooming sessions to prioritize user stories and acceptance criteria. The contractor shall maintain a product backlog in a Government-approved project management tool and provide sprint velocity metrics as part of monthly progress reporting. All deliverables shall be produced incrementally, with a potentially shippable product increment demonstrated at the conclusion of each sprint. The contractor shall adhere to the Agile Manifesto principles and demonstrate continuous improvement through retrospective action items documented and tracked across sprints.

Example 2:
The Department of Veterans Affairs requires the selected vendor to employ Agile/Scrum methodology for all development efforts under this task order. The contractor shall organize development personnel into cross-functional Agile teams, each consisting of a Scrum Master, developers, testers, and business analysts. Sprint duration shall be fixed at two weeks, and the contractor shall deliver working software at the end of each sprint for Government review and acceptance. The contractor shall conduct sprint reviews with VA stakeholders to demonstrate completed user stories against agreed-upon Definition of Done criteria. Backlog refinement sessions shall occur at minimum once per sprint to ensure the product backlog reflects current priorities and is sufficiently groomed for upcoming sprints. The contractor shall provide release planning documentation outlining the expected delivery of features across program increments. Agile metrics including sprint burndown charts, velocity, and cumulative flow diagrams shall be reported to the Contracting Officer's Representative on a bi-weekly basis.

Example 3:
Under this task order issued against the NASA Solutions for Enterprise-Wide Procurement contract, the contractor shall manage and execute all software development activities in accordance with Agile methodology. The contractor shall implement a Scrum-based framework and shall operate all development teams on a uniform two-week sprint cadence synchronized with the program's release train schedule. Sprint planning shall be completed within four hours for a two-week sprint, and the contractor shall ensure that sprint goals are clearly articulated and agreed upon by the Government Product Owner prior to sprint commencement. The contractor shall maintain a Definition of Done that includes code review completion, unit test coverage thresholds, security scanning, and Product Owner acceptance. Working software shall be deployed to a staging environment at the end of each sprint for Government validation. The contractor shall produce and maintain an Agile project charter documenting team norms, Agile ceremony schedules, tooling agreements, and escalation procedures. Release planning artifacts including feature roadmaps and program increment objectives shall be reviewed and approved by the NASA Contracting Officer's Representative on a quarterly basis.

Example 1:
The Department of Homeland Security (DHS) Science and Technology Directorate is soliciting proposals for the implementation of a cloud-based project portfolio management capability to support research program tracking across the Directorate. Following a formal analysis of alternatives, the Government has selected a commercial solution based on Salesforce Project Management modules as the technical baseline. The primary work under this contract is configuring and deploying the selected commercial off-the-shelf platform to meet DHS-specific reporting, resource allocation, and milestone tracking requirements. The contractor shall configure dashboards, automated notifications, role-based access controls, and integration connectors to existing financial management systems. Offerors shall describe their approach to Salesforce configuration without reliance on custom Apex code development except where configuration-only approaches are technically infeasible and documented as such. The Government anticipates a total level of effort of approximately 2,800 hours across the base year, with options for ongoing platform administration and enhancement.

Example 2:
The Department of Homeland Security, Office of the Chief Information Officer, hereby solicits proposals for an enterprise identity and access management solution. This requirement is for a COTS solution required to meet the agency's zero-trust architecture objectives. The Government will not fund, authorize, or accept delivery of any custom-developed software components. Offerors are advised that the scope of this engagement is configuration only, encompassing identity provider setup, multi-factor authentication policy definition, role-based access control mapping, and directory synchronization. All proposed software must be commercially available and actively maintained by the Offeror as a standard product offering. Licensing terms must reflect commercial market rates, and the product must receive regular security patches and version updates as part of the vendor's standard release cycle. Integration connectors used to link the platform with DHS legacy systems must be pre-built and commercially supported. Engineering of new connectors or middleware specifically for this contract is outside the permissible scope of work.

Example 3:
The Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) is seeking a qualified contractor to configure and deploy a commercial off-the-shelf customer relationship management solution to support the Veterans Benefits Administration's case intake and tracking functions. The Government has determined that Salesforce Government Cloud meets the functional requirements identified in the market research phase and represents the most cost-effective approach. The contractor shall be responsible for configuring Salesforce workflows, custom objects, permission sets, and approval processes in alignment with VA business rules. No custom software development is anticipated under this effort. The contractor shall also be responsible for data migration from legacy Access databases, user acceptance testing coordination, and end-user training for approximately 400 VBA personnel across regional offices. The period of performance is a twelve-month base period with two twelve-month option periods. All work shall comply with VA Handbook 6500, FISMA requirements, and FedRAMP authorization standards applicable to the selected commercial solution.

The Department of Homeland Security (DHS), Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA), requires a qualified contractor to design and build a custom Vulnerability Coordination Workflow System (VCWS) to streamline the intake, triage, assignment, and resolution tracking of vulnerability disclosures received through CISA's Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure (CVD) program. The system shall replace manual processes currently managed through spreadsheets and email chains and shall provide a unified case management interface for CISA analysts, external researchers, and affected vendor stakeholders. Core functional requirements include automated case creation via structured web submission forms and email parsing, configurable triage rules, secure stakeholder communication portals with role-based permissions, audit logging of all case actions, and integration with the National Vulnerability Database (NVD) API for CVE lookup and enrichment. The contractor shall develop the system as a cloud-hosted application on a FedRAMP High authorized environment and shall meet DHS 4300A security requirements. The period of performance is a twenty-four-month base period inclusive of development, testing, pilot deployment, and full operational deployment, followed by two twelve-month option periods covering ongoing maintenance and capability enhancements. Contract type is Firm-Fixed-Price. Offerors shall submit a technical volume, past performance volume, and price volume. Past performance submissions shall reference at least two contracts involving development of case management or workflow automation systems for federal civilian or law enforcement agencies.

The Department of Justice Office of Justice Programs is issuing this solicitation for a performance measurement and reporting platform to support grant recipient monitoring activities. OJP requires a commercially available product with demonstrated use in federal grant management contexts. This is not a software development contract. No custom development of any application component is within scope, and proposals containing such elements will be rejected. The Government's intent is to configure an existing platform to capture grantee performance data, generate compliance reports, and provide dashboard visualizations using tools native to the commercial product. Configuration-only work is authorized, including form design using the platform's built-in form builder, workflow definition using the platform's process configuration module, and user access management. The Contractor shall provide a configuration workbook documenting all settings applied during implementation. OJP will retain the ability to perform ongoing configuration adjustments using administrative tools provided within the product. No source code access, compiled code deliverables, or software release packages are expected or permitted under this contract.

Example 2:
The Department of the Interior Bureau of Indian Affairs is soliciting offers for a grants management information system to replace legacy tracking tools currently in use across twelve regional offices. The Government's acquisition strategy for this requirement is based on the procurement of a commercially available, pre-built platform. No custom software development is authorized under this contract. The BIA recognizes that a COTS solution required approach may necessitate adjustment of internal grant lifecycle processes to conform to the system's standard workflows. This trade-off is acceptable to the Government and has been incorporated into the acquisition planning assumptions. The Contractor's role is limited to system configuration, user acceptance testing support, training delivery, and help desk services during a twelve-month transition period. Any proposal that includes line items for software engineers, developers, architects engaged in coding activity, or application development environments will be evaluated as non-compliant with the stated scope. Questions regarding permissible configuration activities should be submitted through the official solicitation question-and-answer process.

Example 3:
The Department of Justice, Executive Office for Immigration Review, requires comprehensive information technology support services to sustain and enhance court management systems used by immigration judges across more than seventy (70) immigration courts nationwide. The contractor must deploy a standing team of not fewer than eighteen (18) IT professionals to execute the full scope of work, which includes help desk operations, application maintenance and development, network administration, data center management, cybersecurity monitoring, and business intelligence reporting. The required staffing configuration shall include a Program Manager with PMP certification, a Deputy Program Manager, a Help Desk Manager overseeing a staff of at least four (4) Tier I and Tier II support specialists, three (3) application developers, two (2) database administrators, a network engineer, a cybersecurity analyst, a data warehouse specialist, and a configuration management specialist. The contractor shall maintain this minimum staffing level throughout all base and option periods. Proposed personnel must successfully complete a DOJ background investigation prior to being granted access to DOJ networks and systems. The staffing plan, including résumés for all eighteen key and non-key personnel, shall be submitted with Volume II of the technical proposal.

Example 1:
The Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) intends to conduct oral presentations with offerors as part of its evaluation of proposals submitted in response to this solicitation for Environmental Monitoring and Assessment Support Services. Oral presentations will be conducted following the initial screening of written proposals. Only those offerors determined to be technically acceptable after written review will be scheduled for an oral presentation. The oral presentation will focus primarily on the offeror's technical methodology, data management approach, and the qualifications of proposed scientific personnel. Each session is limited to one hundred and twenty (120) minutes, including a thirty (30) minute question period. The proposed Principal Investigator must personally attend and lead the oral presentation. Additional team members may participate at the offeror's discretion but are subject to a five-person maximum. Materials presented must not introduce new technical approaches not previously described in the written proposal, and any inconsistencies identified will be evaluated unfavorably.

Example 2:
Section M – Evaluation Criteria: The Department of Homeland Security will evaluate all responsive proposals using a Best Value Tradeoff methodology. Following written proposal evaluation, offerors deemed within the competitive range will be invited to deliver an oral presentation to the Source Selection Evaluation Board (SSEB). The oral presentation is intended to allow offerors to clarify and elaborate upon their written technical submissions and to demonstrate the depth of understanding of the program requirements. Presentations shall be limited to sixty (60) minutes with an additional twenty (20) minutes reserved for Government questions. The lead proposed Program Manager and at least two key technical personnel identified in the proposal must participate in person. Remote participation via video teleconference will be permitted only upon written approval by the Contracting Officer. Scores assigned during the oral presentation phase will be integrated with written proposal scores to produce a final technical rating for each offeror.

Example 3:
In accordance with FAR 15.102, this solicitation incorporates oral presentations as a substitute for, or supplement to, written information for the Technical Approach evaluation factor. Offerors selected for oral presentations will receive written notification from the Contracting Officer specifying the date, time, location, and format requirements. The Department of Energy's Office of Environmental Management will evaluate the oral presentation under Subfactor 1A – Technical Understanding and Subfactor 1B – Staffing and Organization. Each offeror's presentation team must include the proposed Project Director, Deputy Project Director, and at least one subject matter expert for each major technical area described in the Statement of Work. Presentations shall not exceed one hundred and fifty (150) minutes inclusive of Government questions. Offerors are encouraged to use visual aids, technical demonstrations, or mock deliverable examples to enhance their presentations. The evaluation panel will score the oral presentation independently from written submissions, and both scores will be combined to produce the final Technical Approach rating used in the Best Value determination.

The Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), Office of Acquisition Solutions, intends to conduct oral presentations as an integral part of the source selection process for this environmental monitoring and data analytics services acquisition. Offerors selected for the competitive range following written proposal evaluation will receive written notification specifying the date, time, location, and logistics for their respective oral presentation session. Presentations shall not exceed ninety (90) minutes in total duration, with the first sixty (60) minutes dedicated to the offeror's structured presentation and the remaining thirty (30) minutes reserved exclusively for questions posed by the Source Selection Evaluation Board (SSEB). Offerors must ensure the attendance of the proposed Program Manager, Lead Data Scientist, and at least one subject matter expert relevant to environmental compliance monitoring. Evaluators will score the oral presentation under the Technical Approach factor using the same adjectival rating scale applied to written submissions. Any material inconsistencies identified between the written proposal and statements made during the oral presentation may result in a downward adjustment of the offeror's technical score or a request for clarification. All slide materials and supporting documents to be used during the presentation must be submitted in advance and will become part of the official proposal record.

The period of performance under this contract shall be nine (9) months from the date of award. There are no option years associated with this procurement. Services shall be provided on-site five days per week, with on-call availability for psychiatric emergencies. All contractor personnel must successfully complete Bureau of Prisons background investigations and security orientation prior to commencing work. Monthly performance reports are required and must be submitted to the COR no later than the fifth business day of each succeeding month. Award will be made to the responsible offeror whose proposal represents the best value.

Example 2:
NOTICE OF INTENT TO AWARD / REQUEST FOR PROPOSALS – DEPARTMENT OF JUSTICE, BUREAU OF PRISONS. The Federal Bureau of Prisons, Facilities Management Branch, solicits proposals for the repair and restoration of exterior masonry at one medium-security correctional facility in the Southeast Region. The scope includes inspection, joint repointing, surface cleaning, and application of waterproof sealant across approximately 4,000 square feet of affected surface area. This procurement will result in a single award firm-fixed-price contract. Available funding for this effort totals $88,000, which constitutes the full and complete contract ceiling. The BOP has determined that this project must be executed within that amount based on current capital improvement budget allocations for the facility. No modifications adding additional scope or funding will be entertained after award. Contractors must include a complete material and labor cost breakdown with their proposals and certify that the total proposed price, inclusive of all overhead, profit, bonding, and insurance, does not exceed eighty-eight thousand dollars ($88,000). Proposals above this ceiling will be rejected.

The total ceiling for this contract shall not exceed $85,000 for a base period of performance of twelve (12) months. No option periods are anticipated at this time. Offerors are advised that all technical deliverables, including requirements documentation, system design, development, testing, training, and post-deployment support, must be accomplished within the stated ceiling amount. The Government does not intend to award a contract exceeding this threshold under any circumstances. This acquisition is set aside exclusively for small businesses under NAICS code 541511. Interested parties should note that the contracting officer has determined that $85,000 represents a fair and reasonable price for the described scope of work based on independent government cost estimates. Proposals that exceed this maximum budget will be deemed non-responsive and will not be evaluated further. Offerors must clearly demonstrate how all required tasks can be completed within the $85,000 total contract value.

Example 1:
The Naval Facilities Engineering Systems Command, Southwest, hereby solicits offers for refuse collection, recycling, and solid waste management services at Naval Base San Diego. This procurement is conducted using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection methodology. Technical evaluation criteria are defined as Acceptable or Unacceptable and include: compliance with all federal, state, and local environmental regulations; demonstration of required equipment and staffing levels; and proof of required licenses and permits. Price evaluation will consider the total evaluated price across the base period and all option years. Award shall be made to the responsible offeror whose proposal is technically acceptable and offers the lowest price. The Government is not seeking best value tradeoffs and will not award to a higher-priced technically acceptable offeror under any circumstances. Offerors must thoroughly address all technical criteria to avoid an Unacceptable rating, which would preclude award regardless of pricing submitted.

Example 2:
The Environmental Protection Agency, Office of Acquisition Solutions, is issuing this Request for Proposals for technical support services related to water quality monitoring and sampling at Superfund sites in EPA Region 5. This acquisition will be evaluated using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process in accordance with FAR 15.101-2. Technical acceptability will be evaluated under three pass/fail criteria: Key Personnel Qualifications, Laboratory Accreditation Compliance, and Quality Assurance Plan Adequacy. Proposals must be rated Acceptable on all three criteria to be eligible for award. Technical proposals that exceed minimum requirements will receive no additional consideration. Following determination of technical acceptability, award will be made to the responsible offeror submitting the lowest evaluated total price. The Government has made a written determination that LPTA is the appropriate evaluation approach given the well-defined nature of the work and the availability of qualified contractors capable of performing at minimum standards. Questions regarding the LPTA evaluation criteria must be submitted by the deadline specified in Section L.

Example 3:
The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, Omaha District, is seeking proposals for facility maintenance and repair services at Camp Ashland, Nebraska. The source selection methodology for this procurement is Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA). Offerors must submit a Technical Volume and a Price Volume. The Technical Volume will be evaluated to determine whether the offeror's proposed approach meets the minimum requirements of the Performance Work Statement. Ratings will be limited to Acceptable or Unacceptable. An offeror receiving an Unacceptable technical rating will not be eligible for award. Price will be evaluated for completeness, reasonableness, and balance. Award will be made to the offeror with the lowest evaluated price whose technical proposal has been rated Acceptable. The use of LPTA in this acquisition is consistent with the determination that the requirement is well-defined and risk of unsuccessful performance is minimal. The Government does not anticipate the need for discussions.

The United States Coast Guard, Surface Forces Logistics Center, hereby solicits offers for depot-level maintenance and repair services for 25-foot Response Boat-Small (RB-S) hulls and associated mechanical systems. Source selection will be conducted using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable methodology as set forth in FAR 15.101-2. The Government has made this determination based on the availability of clear, detailed technical specifications and the low risk of performance variation among qualified marine repair vendors. Technical Acceptability will be evaluated on a pass/fail basis using three criteria: (1) demonstration of facility certifications and qualified marine repair workforce as specified in Section L; (2) submission of an acceptable Quality Control Plan meeting the requirements of Section C; and (3) evidence of required environmental compliance permits for hull painting and hazardous material handling operations. Failure to satisfy any single criterion will result in an overall Technical rating of Unacceptable, rendering the proposal ineligible for further evaluation. Offerors are encouraged to verify compliance with all technical criteria prior to submission. The Government does not anticipate conducting discussions and intends to award based on initial proposals. Price will be evaluated for completeness and reasonableness. Among all proposals rated Technically Acceptable, the contract will be awarded to the offeror submitting the lowest price. Superior technical approaches or enhanced capabilities beyond stated minimums will not influence award under this LPTA process.

Example 1:
The Department of Veterans Affairs requires the contractor to employ a waterfall methodology for the development of the Veterans Benefits Management System Enhancement. All work shall proceed through sequential phases including requirements analysis, system design, implementation, testing, deployment, and maintenance. Each phase must be fully completed and formally accepted by the Contracting Officer's Representative prior to initiation of the subsequent phase. The contractor shall maintain a fixed requirements baseline established during the requirements phase, which shall not be altered without formal change control board approval and contract modification. Deliverables associated with each phase gate must be submitted, reviewed, and approved before any downstream development activities commence. The contractor shall provide a detailed Waterfall Project Plan within 30 days of contract award, identifying all phase entry and exit criteria, milestone dates, and associated deliverables. Monthly status reports shall track progress against the approved baseline schedule and document any variances exceeding five percent of planned effort.

Example 2:
The Department of Transportation, Federal Aviation Administration, solicits technical and price proposals for development of the Aeronautical Information Management Replacement System. Given the safety-critical nature of the system, the FAA mandates compliance with DO-178C and requires the use of traditional SDLC sequential phases throughout the program lifecycle. The contractor shall complete and obtain Government approval of all systems engineering artifacts associated with each development phase before proceeding to subsequent phases. The development sequence shall include: System Requirements Review, Software Requirements Review, Preliminary Design Review, Critical Design Review, Software Build and Integration, Software Qualification Testing, System Acceptance Testing, and Operational Readiness Review. A fixed requirements baseline shall be established following Government approval of the System and Software Requirements Specifications. The contractor shall employ rigorous phase gate controls, and no phase transition shall occur without documented Government authorization. Traceability matrices shall be maintained from requirements through test.

Example 3:
The Department of Energy, National Nuclear Security Administration, requires that the contractor employ a waterfall methodology for the development of the Nuclear Material Accountability and Control Information System. This requirement reflects NNSA policy mandating structured, auditable development processes for systems handling Safeguards and Security information. The contractor shall execute all software development under clearly defined sequential phases: Requirements Definition, Functional Design, Detailed Design, Software Construction, Software Testing, and System Deployment. Phase gate reviews shall be scheduled and completed at the conclusion of each phase, with a formal Government Decision Authority review required before transition to the next phase. All requirements identified during the Requirements Definition phase shall be placed under configuration control as the fixed requirements baseline. Changes to this baseline require submission of a formal Request for Change, independent cost and schedule impact assessment, and written approval from the NNSA Contracting Officer's Representative and Configuration Control Board.

The Department of Transportation, Federal Aviation Administration, issues this solicitation for the development of the Airspace Management Coordination System. The contractor shall employ a waterfall methodology as the mandated development framework for all system engineering activities under this contract. Project execution shall proceed through the following sequential phases: Mission Needs Analysis, System Requirements Definition, High-Level Design, Detailed Design, Software Implementation, Integration Testing, System Verification and Validation, and Operational Acceptance. Each phase shall culminate in a formal phase gate review conducted by the Government Technical Review Board. Written authorization to proceed to the subsequent phase must be obtained from the Contracting Officer's Representative before any work associated with that phase may commence. The contractor shall establish a fixed requirements baseline upon Government approval of the System Requirements Specification, anticipated no later than 45 calendar days following contract award. All baselined requirements shall be placed under formal configuration management control in accordance with EIA-649. Any proposed modification to the approved requirements baseline must be submitted as a formal Request for Change, reviewed by the Configuration Control Board, and approved via contract modification prior to implementation. The contractor shall deliver a comprehensive Waterfall Project Management Plan within 15 business days of contract award, detailing phase entry and exit criteria, key milestone dates, resource allocations, and a master integrated schedule. Progress will be assessed monthly against the Government-approved baseline schedule, with written variance reports required for any deviation exceeding ten percent of planned effort or schedule.

All work under this contract must be performed at designated project locations within the Bridger-Teton National Forest, as specified in the individual task orders issued by the Contracting Officer's Representative. Contractor crews must be physically present at each designated trail segment during all active work days. Field personnel may not perform any portion of this work from remote administrative locations; all ground disturbance, drainage installation, tread construction, and revegetation activities are inherently field-based and require continuous on-site presence. Daily field logs, photographic documentation, and resource protection monitoring forms must be completed by crew personnel while physically located at the work site. Personnel files and time and attendance records will be reviewed by the COR on a biweekly basis at the building management office. Contractors are expressly forbidden from performing any contract tasks from locations other than the specified federal building. Equipment used in performance of this contract shall remain on-site at all times unless written approval for removal is obtained from the Contracting Officer.
