PART IV – EVALUATION CRITERIA

Section M.3 – Basis for Award

Award will be made to the offeror whose proposal represents the Best Value to the Government considering the following factors listed in descending order of importance: (1) Technical/Management Approach, inclusive of oral presentation; (2) Past Performance; and (3) Price/Cost. Technical/Management Approach and Past Performance, when combined, are significantly more important than Price/Cost.

Section M.4 – Technical/Management Approach

This factor evaluates the offeror's understanding of the requirement and the soundness of their proposed solution. A minimum of fifty percent (50%) of the evaluation score under this factor will be derived from the oral presentation conducted after written proposal review. The remaining fifty percent (50%) will derive from the written technical narrative. Offerors must explicitly address their Agile/Scrum implementation plan within both components. The written narrative shall describe sprint structure, definition of done criteria, backlog grooming procedures, and integration of user stories with system requirements traceability. During the oral presentation, offerors will be evaluated on their ability to articulate how Agile ceremonies will be conducted within a classified government environment, how they will manage dependencies across multiple Scrum teams, and how they will ensure continuous delivery while meeting federal compliance obligations. The oral presentation will be evaluated by a panel of no fewer than three (3) technical evaluators using standardized scoring rubrics. Offerors shall provide a roster of oral presentation participants to the Contracting Officer no later than seventy-two (72) hours in advance. All individuals presenting must be identified as key personnel in the offeror's proposal.

The consolidated regional approach reflected in this solicitation is the result of an approved Acquisition Strategy that emphasizes best value and operational integration. The contractor shall maintain sufficient management infrastructure to handle demand variability across the full ordering range. Performance will be measured against Quality Assurance Surveillance Plan metrics defined in Section E. The Contracting Officer's Representative will conduct monthly performance reviews and quarterly program management meetings throughout the period of performance.

RFP chunk to analyze:
MARKET RESEARCH SUMMARY AND ACQUISITION STRATEGY RATIONALE

In preparation for this acquisition, the Contracting Office conducted a comprehensive market survey in accordance with FAR Part 10 to assess the availability of qualified small business sources. A Sources Sought Notice (SSN) was posted to SAM.gov on a prior date, and the agency received seventeen responses from various business categories. Of the responding firms, four self-identified as Service-Disabled Veteran-Owned Small Businesses. Upon reviewing the capability statements submitted, the Contracting Officer, in coordination with the cognizant Small Business Specialist and the Program Manager, determined that the responses from SDVOSB firms, while demonstrating general industry familiarity, did not collectively evidence the specific nuclear safety compliance certifications and specialized containment engineering expertise required to fulfill this requirement at the performance levels demanded. As a result, the Rule of Two threshold under FAR 19.502-2 was not met for an SDVOSB set-aside determination, meaning the evidence did not support a reasonable expectation that award at a fair market price could be made to two or more SDVOSB concerns. This acquisition therefore proceeds as an Unrestricted competition. This determination does not reflect negatively on the capabilities of SDVOSB firms in general; rather, it reflects the highly specialized and credentialed nature of the specific technical performance requirements associated with this particular statement of work.

Consistent with FHWA's technology modernization policy, this acquisition mandates a COTS solution and explicitly prohibits any custom application development. The work under this contract is scoped as configuration only, covering asset taxonomy definition, inspection workflow setup, geospatial layer integration using the platform's native mapping connectors, and report template customization through the product's built-in report designer. The Contractor must demonstrate that all proposed capabilities are available in the current production release of the software and do not depend on future product updates or vendor commitments. Proposals referencing software development lifecycles, code repositories, or testing environments associated with new feature creation will be considered outside the scope of this solicitation and may result in the proposal being found technically unacceptable.

Example 2:
The Department of the Interior Bureau of Indian Affairs is soliciting offers for a grants management information system to replace legacy tracking tools currently in use across twelve regional offices. The Government's acquisition strategy for this requirement is based on the procurement of a commercially available, pre-built platform. No custom software development is authorized under this contract. The BIA recognizes that a COTS solution required approach may necessitate adjustment of internal grant lifecycle processes to conform to the system's standard workflows. This trade-off is acceptable to the Government and has been incorporated into the acquisition planning assumptions. The Contractor's role is limited to system configuration, user acceptance testing support, training delivery, and help desk services during a twelve-month transition period. Any proposal that includes line items for software engineers, developers, architects engaged in coding activity, or application development environments will be evaluated as non-compliant with the stated scope. Questions regarding permissible configuration activities should be submitted through the official solicitation question-and-answer process.

Example 3:
The Department of Transportation, Federal Aviation Administration, solicits technical and price proposals for development of the Aeronautical Information Management Replacement System. Given the safety-critical nature of the system, the FAA mandates compliance with DO-178C and requires the use of traditional SDLC sequential phases throughout the program lifecycle. The contractor shall complete and obtain Government approval of all systems engineering artifacts associated with each development phase before proceeding to subsequent phases. The development sequence shall include: System Requirements Review, Software Requirements Review, Preliminary Design Review, Critical Design Review, Software Build and Integration, Software Qualification Testing, System Acceptance Testing, and Operational Readiness Review. A fixed requirements baseline shall be established following Government approval of the System and Software Requirements Specifications. The contractor shall employ rigorous phase gate controls, and no phase transition shall occur without documented Government authorization. Traceability matrices shall be maintained from requirements through test.

Example 1:
SOLICITATION NUMBER: HHS-2024-EDWOSB-0042

This acquisition is a 100% set-aside for Economically Disadvantaged Women-Owned Small Business (EDWOSB) concerns under the authority of 15 U.S.C. 637(m). The Department of Health and Human Services, Office of Acquisition Services, intends to award a firm-fixed-price contract for the provision of professional health information technology consulting services in support of the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services modernization initiative. Only offerors who are certified Economically Disadvantaged Women-Owned Small Business concerns at the time of offer submission and at the time of award are eligible to receive this contract. Offerors must be registered in the System for Award Management (SAM.gov) and must have a current EDWOSB certification through the SBA certification program or a third-party certifier approved by the Small Business Administration. The applicable NAICS code is 541512 with a small business size standard of $34 million in average annual receipts. Any offer received from a concern that does not qualify as an EDWOSB at the time of offer will be considered nonresponsive and will not be evaluated. Questions regarding EDWOSB eligibility requirements should be directed to the contracting officer prior to the closing date of this solicitation.

Example 2:
DEPARTMENT OF EDUCATION
OFFICE OF ACQUISITIONS
Solicitation Number: ED-ODS-24-C-0087

The U.S. Department of Education, Office of the Deputy Secretary, is issuing this Request for Proposals as an Economically Disadvantaged Women-Owned Small Business (EDWOSB) set-aside pursuant to FAR 19.1505 and the SBA's regulations at 13 CFR Part 127. The Department requires program evaluation and performance measurement services to assess the effectiveness of Title I grant programs across selected local educational agencies. This acquisition falls under NAICS code 541611 – Administrative Management and General Management Consulting Services, with a size standard of $24.5 million. To be eligible for this award, an offeror must qualify as an Economically Disadvantaged Women-Owned Small Business concern and must hold a current, valid EDWOSB certification issued by the SBA or a third-party certifier recognized by the SBA. Mere self-certification as a WOSB or EDWOSB is no longer sufficient; offerors must provide a third-party or SBA-issued certification as required under the SBA's final rule effective October 15, 2020. Proposals received from firms that do not meet the EDWOSB eligibility criteria will be rejected without evaluation. The anticipated period of performance is three years from the date of award, inclusive of options.

Example 3:
DEPARTMENT OF AGRICULTURE
AGRICULTURAL RESEARCH SERVICE
Solicitation Number: 12-25-B-0034

This solicitation is being conducted as an Economically Disadvantaged Women-Owned Small Business (EDWOSB) set-aside in accordance with FAR Subpart 19.15 and 13 CFR Part 127. The USDA Agricultural Research Service (ARS) requires logistics, warehousing, and distribution services in support of research specimen and laboratory supply chain operations at the National Centers for Animal Health complex in Ames, Iowa. The applicable NAICS code for this requirement is 493110 – General Warehousing and Storage Facilities, and the applicable size standard is $34 million in average annual receipts. Pursuant to the EDWOSB set-aside designation, only offers submitted by firms that are currently certified as Economically Disadvantaged Women-Owned Small Business concerns will be considered. To be considered economically disadvantaged, the woman or women who own and control the firm must meet the personal financial criteria established at 13 CFR 127.200, including limitations on personal net worth, adjusted gross income, and total assets. Offerors should include their SBA-issued EDWOSB certification number or third-party certifier documentation in Volume I of their offer. Awards made under this solicitation are subject to the limitations on subcontracting requirements applicable to EDWOSB set-asides as specified in FAR 52.219-30.

---

RFP chunk to analyze:
SECTION L – INSTRUCTIONS TO OFFERORS

L.4 SOCIOECONOMIC PROGRAM ACKNOWLEDGMENT

The Department is committed to maximizing opportunities for small and disadvantaged businesses across all acquisitions. Offerors are encouraged to review SBA's guidelines regarding the Women-Owned Small Business Federal Contract Program, which includes provisions for both WOSB and Economically Disadvantaged Women-Owned Small Business (EDWOSB) participants. These programs have been specifically designed to level the playing field in industries where women-owned firms have historically been underrepresented.

For the purposes of this Request for Proposals, however, the agency is not implementing an EDWOSB set-aside, a WOSB set-aside, or any other socioeconomic set-aside designation. This solicitation is open to all offerors meeting the stated minimum qualifications. Past performance evaluations, technical approach assessments, and price/cost analyses will be conducted without regard to the socioeconomic status of the offeror.

Subcontracting Considerations: Large business prime contractors are advised that an acceptable subcontracting plan will be required as a condition of award per FAR 52.219-9. Such plans should demonstrate good-faith outreach to EDWOSB, WOSB, HUBZone, SDVOSB, and other small business categories. Subcontracting goals will be negotiated prior to award. Failure to demonstrate meaningful small business inclusion in the subcontracting plan may render an offeror's proposal unacceptable.

This solicitation for Healthcare Information Technology Modernization Support Services includes an oral presentation requirement as a formal element of the evaluation process. The Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) will invite offerors in the competitive range to participate in structured oral presentations following the initial written proposal review. Oral presentations will be assessed under Technical Factor 2 – Implementation Methodology and Technical Factor 4 – Relevant Experience. Each offeror will have seventy-five (75) minutes to present, followed by a twenty-five (25) minute question-and-answer session conducted by the HHS Evaluation Committee. The offeror's proposed Program Manager, Lead Systems Architect, and at least one clinical informatics specialist must be present in person. Remote participation is not authorized except under emergency circumstances approved in advance by the Contracting Officer. Offerors should structure their oral presentation to directly address the evaluation criteria outlined in Section M and are advised that new information introduced during the oral presentation that was not contained in the written proposal will be evaluated as a clarification and not as a revision or amendment to the offer.

Example 2:
Offerors in the competitive range shall be required to participate in an oral presentation as part of the evaluation process. The oral presentation will be conducted at the Department of Veterans Affairs Network Contracting Office and will be scheduled at the Government's discretion. The purpose of the oral presentation is to assess each offeror's understanding of the Performance Work Statement requirements, proposed staffing model, and quality control methodology. Presentations shall be a maximum of seventy-five (75) minutes in duration, which includes a mandatory fifteen (15) minute question-and-answer session conducted by the Technical Evaluation Panel. The proposed Contract Program Manager must attend and lead the presentation. Supporting staff may participate but shall not exceed four (4) individuals total. All slide materials or visual aids must be provided to the Contracting Officer no later than three (3) business days prior to the presentation. The Government may record oral presentations for evaluation documentation purposes, and offerors will be notified accordingly.

Example 3:
As part of the evaluation methodology for this Information Technology Services procurement, the General Services Administration (GSA) will incorporate oral presentations into the source selection process. After an initial review of written proposals, offerors receiving a rating of Acceptable or higher under the Technical Factor will be invited to participate in an oral presentation before the Technical Evaluation Team. The oral presentation allows the Government to assess offeror capabilities beyond what is conveyed in written form and to evaluate proposed personnel directly. Each offeror will be allotted ninety (90) minutes total: sixty (60) minutes for the presentation and thirty (30) minutes for Government-directed questions. Key personnel named in Section H must be present and actively participate. Substitutions of key personnel for the oral presentation will not be permitted without prior written approval and may negatively impact the evaluation. Offerors should note that the oral presentation is evaluated separately under Factor 2 – Management Approach and scored on an Adjectival basis consistent with the criteria described in Section M.

Subpart 15.4 Supplement – Agency Oral Presentation Requirements: U.S. Agency for International Development. The U.S. Agency for International Development (USAID) has determined that oral presentations will be conducted as a formal evaluation component for this global health supply chain and logistics services acquisition. Following the initial technical evaluation of written proposals, USAID's Bureau for Management, Office of Acquisition and Assistance, will identify offerors within the competitive range and schedule individual oral presentation sessions. Each session will be allocated a total of ninety (90) minutes: sixty (60) minutes for the offeror's presentation followed by a thirty (30)-minute structured question period conducted by the Technical Evaluation Committee (TEC). The proposed Chief of Party and a minimum of two technical leads named in the proposal must participate in person at USAID's Ronald Reagan Building offices in Washington, D.C. Requests for remote participation must be submitted in writing and will be evaluated on a case-by-case basis by the Agreement Officer. Oral presentations will be assessed under the Technical Approach and Management Plan evaluation factors and will be scored using the same merit-based rating methodology applied to written technical submissions. Scores derived from oral presentations will be weighted equally with the corresponding written technical scores within each factor to produce a final composite technical rating. Offerors are advised that all statements, data, representations, and commitments made during the oral presentation are considered part of the official proposal and may be incorporated into the resulting award instrument. Slide materials and visual aids must be submitted to the Agreement Officer at least forty-eight (48) hours in advance of the scheduled session.

U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) seeks to acquire translation and interpretation services in more than 40 languages to support field operations at U.S. ports of entry along the southern and northern borders. SOLICITATION NUMBER: HHS-2024-WOSB-0142

This acquisition is a Total Small Business Set-Aside restricted to Women-Owned Small Business (WOSB) concerns pursuant to 15 U.S.C. 637(m) and FAR Subpart 19.15. Only offers submitted by firms certified as Women-Owned Small Businesses under the SBA WOSB Federal Contract Program will be considered for award. The North American Industry Classification System (NAICS) code for this requirement is 541611 (Administrative Management and General Management Consulting Services) with a size standard of $21.5 million. The Department of Health and Human Services, Office of the Assistant Secretary for Administration, requires management consulting support services to assist in organizational transformation initiatives across regional offices. Offerors must be registered in the SBA certify.sba.gov repository as a WOSB prior to proposal submission. Failure to maintain active WOSB certification at time of award will result in disqualification. The Government intends to award a single Firm-Fixed-Price task order contract with a base period of 12 months and four one-year option periods. All interested WOSB firms must submit capability statements no later than the date specified in Section L of this solicitation.

DEPARTMENT OF HOMELAND SECURITY
U.S. CUSTOMS AND BORDER PROTECTION
SOLICITATION: 70B04C24R00000025

This requirement is set aside exclusively for Service-Disabled Veteran-Owned Small Business (SDVOSB) concerns in accordance with Federal Acquisition Regulation (FAR) 19.1405. U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) intends to procure professional training and curriculum development services to support officer training programs at the CBP Academy, Glynco, Georgia. SECTION B — SUPPLIES OR SERVICES AND PRICES

This procurement is for Multilingual Translation and Interpretation Services in support of U.S. Customs and Border Protection field operations at ports of entry along the Southwest Border region. All task orders will be competed among awardees in accordance with FAR 16.505. Offerors are advised that misrepresentation of WOSB status constitutes a violation of federal law and may result in suspension, debarment, and referral for criminal prosecution. Capability statements and past performance narratives must be submitted in accordance with the instructions provided in Section L of this solicitation.

Example 1:
The contractor shall implement Agile development methodologies throughout the period of performance. All software development activities shall be conducted using Scrum framework, with two-week sprint cycles. The contractor shall conduct daily stand-up meetings, sprint planning sessions, sprint reviews, and sprint retrospectives in accordance with Scrum ceremonies. A certified Scrum Master shall be designated and responsible for facilitating all Agile ceremonies and removing impediments to team progress. The Government Product Owner shall participate in sprint planning and backlog grooming sessions to prioritize user stories and acceptance criteria. The contractor shall maintain a product backlog in a Government-approved project management tool and provide sprint velocity metrics as part of monthly progress reporting. All deliverables shall be produced incrementally, with a potentially shippable product increment demonstrated at the conclusion of each sprint. The contractor shall adhere to the Agile Manifesto principles and demonstrate continuous improvement through retrospective action items documented and tracked across sprints.

Example 2:
Pursuant to the Department of Defense's software acquisition pathway as defined in DoDI 5000.87, the contractor shall deliver capabilities using Agile software development methodology with continuous integration and continuous delivery pipelines. The contractor shall organize personnel into Agile squads operating in two-week sprints, with each squad responsible for a defined set of product backlog items. All Agile ceremonies, including sprint planning, daily standups, sprint reviews, and retrospectives, shall be conducted in person or via Government-approved collaboration tools. The contractor shall employ a certified Scrum Master for each squad who is accountable for coaching team members on Agile principles and ensuring adherence to Scrum practices. A product roadmap updated at minimum quarterly shall be maintained and shared with Government stakeholders to provide visibility into planned increments of capability. The use of Kanban boards or equivalent visual management tools is required to provide real-time transparency into work-in-progress, blocked items, and completed work. Sprint velocity shall be tracked and used to inform release planning and capacity projections.

Example 3:
The Department of Defense, Defense Logistics Agency, requires development of the Materiel Tracking and Accountability System under a waterfall development model consistent with MIL-STD-498 and DoD 5000.02 acquisition guidelines. The contractor shall conduct all software development activities in strict sequential phases including Software Requirements Analysis, Preliminary Design, Detailed Design, Coding and Unit Testing, Computer Software Component Integration and Testing, Computer Software Configuration Item Testing, and System Integration and Testing. The V-model verification and validation framework shall be applied throughout, with each left-side development phase paired with a corresponding right-side testing activity. Phase gate reviews, including the System Requirements Review, Preliminary Design Review, and Critical Design Review, shall be conducted at prescribed milestones. Entrance and exit criteria for each phase gate are defined in Attachment J-3. No phase may commence without written Government approval documenting satisfaction of all exit criteria from the preceding phase.

---

RFP chunk to analyze:
The Government anticipates that development activities will proceed in an iterative and incremental fashion consistent with modern software delivery practices. Offerors are explicitly discouraged from proposing rigid sequential phases or waterfall-style execution models that would delay working software delivery. The contractor shall employ agile frameworks such as Scrum or Kanban, delivering potentially shippable product increments at the conclusion of each sprint. While the program office acknowledges that some legacy documentation artifacts reference traditional phase gate language inherited from earlier acquisition planning documents, such references are superseded by this solicitation's requirement for continuous integration and continuous delivery (CI/CD) pipeline implementation. Requirements will be managed as a living backlog subject to reprioritization by the Product Owner in coordination with agency stakeholders. The contractor shall not interpret any contract line item numbering structure as implying a waterfall sequence of effort; rather, CLINs reflect funding categories and not development stages. Monthly progress reviews will assess velocity, defect density, and sprint goal attainment rather than phase completion milestones. The Government reserves the right to re-sequence, add, or retire backlog items at any sprint boundary without requiring a formal contract modification, provided total level of effort remains within obligated funding ceilings.

The General Services Administration Office of Technology Policy is issuing this Request for Proposal for a cloud-based contract writing and acquisition management platform. This procurement is strictly limited to the acquisition of an existing commercial solution. No software development is authorized under any circumstances. Offerors shall not propose to build, code, or engineer new application modules in response to agency-specific requirements. The Government's intent is to leverage a proven, market-available product and adapt internal business processes to align with the tool's existing capabilities. Configuration of user permissions, workflow routing, document templates, and integration with existing financial systems via standard APIs is permissible and expected. Offerors must demonstrate that the proposed platform has been deployed in at least three federal agency environments. Proposals that include statements of work referencing sprint cycles, agile development backlogs, or custom feature engineering will be evaluated unfavorably and may be deemed non-compliant with this solicitation's scope restrictions.

RFP chunk to analyze:
SECTION L.4 – TECHNICAL APPROACH EVALUATION CRITERIA

Offerors shall describe their approach to providing the specialized advisory and assistance services contemplated under this solicitation. The agency recognizes that engagements of this nature, focused primarily on policy analysis and written deliverable production, do not necessitate the deployment of large numbers of personnel. Indeed, analogous task orders awarded under the parent Indefinite Delivery Indefinite Quantity vehicle have historically been executed by teams of five to eight individuals spanning analytical, editorial, and project management functions. Offerors should resist the tendency to propose inflated teams as a proxy for capability; the Source Selection Evaluation Board will evaluate staffing proposals for realism and alignment with scope. While the Contractor may employ a broader internal workforce across its enterprise, only those individuals directly assigned to and billable under this task order are relevant to the staffing evaluation. The Government is expressly seeking a focused, expert team rather than a high-headcount delivery model. Offerors shall submit an organizational chart limited to directly assigned personnel and shall certify that all proposed individuals are currently employed by or hold valid consultant agreements with the Offeror's firm.

FEDERAL BUREAU OF PRISONS — REQUEST FOR PROPOSALS
Contracting Activity: Federal Bureau of Prisons, Acquisitions Branch, Washington, D.C.

The Federal Bureau of Prisons (BOP) hereby issues this solicitation for inmate vocational training program development and curriculum delivery services at the Federal Correctional Institution located in Terre Haute, Indiana. The total period of performance under this contract is seven (7) months, commencing on the first day of the month following contract award. SECTION M – EVALUATION FACTORS FOR AWARD

M.3 TECHNICAL EVALUATION CRITERIA

Factor 1 – Technical Approach (Most Important)

The Government will evaluate the extent to which the offeror demonstrates a thorough and credible approach to delivering solutions using Agile methodologies. Submissions that do not include an interactive or clickable prototype element will receive a rating no higher than Acceptable under this factor.

The Social Security Administration is seeking a vendor to implement a COTS document management and workflow automation solution within the Office of Disability Determinations. This is a firm-fixed-price acquisition for commercial software licensing, implementation, and ongoing support. The scope of this contract is expressly limited to the deployment and configuration of an existing commercial product. No custom software development will be permitted. Offerors may not propose to write new code, develop new application interfaces, or engineer bespoke processing logic to satisfy SSA-specific business rules. Where the commercial product includes configurable rules engines, form builders, or workflow designers, the Contractor may use those tools to tailor the solution to SSA requirements. Such activities constitute configuration and are within scope. However, any effort requiring software compilation, version branching, or source code modification is strictly prohibited and shall not be included in any proposal. SSA reserves the right to reject any proposal that conflates configuration services with software development activity.

Example 2:
The Department of Homeland Security, Office of the Chief Information Officer, hereby solicits proposals for an enterprise identity and access management solution. This requirement is for a COTS solution required to meet the agency's zero-trust architecture objectives. The Government will not fund, authorize, or accept delivery of any custom-developed software components. Offerors are advised that the scope of this engagement is configuration only, encompassing identity provider setup, multi-factor authentication policy definition, role-based access control mapping, and directory synchronization. All proposed software must be commercially available and actively maintained by the Offeror as a standard product offering. Licensing terms must reflect commercial market rates, and the product must receive regular security patches and version updates as part of the vendor's standard release cycle. Integration connectors used to link the platform with DHS legacy systems must be pre-built and commercially supported. Engineering of new connectors or middleware specifically for this contract is outside the permissible scope of work.

Example 3:

Proposals submitted in response to this solicitation will undergo a multi-phase evaluation process. Phase I consists of written technical and price proposal review. Offerors achieving a combined written evaluation score that places them within the competitive range will advance to Phase II, which requires an oral presentation before the Department of Transportation Technical Review Board. The oral presentation phase is designed to supplement written proposals by allowing offerors to demonstrate technical depth, address evaluator questions regarding proposed approaches, and introduce key personnel who will execute the contract. Oral presentations will be evaluated under Factor 1 – Technical Approach and Factor 3 – Key Personnel, and scores derived from the oral presentation will be weighted equally with written proposal scores for those factors. Each presentation is allocated ninety (90) minutes, with no more than sixty (60) minutes reserved for the offeror's presentation and the remainder designated for Government questions. The offeror's Project Manager and at least two senior technical staff members named in the proposal must be physically present.

DARPA program managers will evaluate each prototype submission during a dedicated review session, which may include independent laboratory testing, performance benchmarking, and direct technical questioning of the offeror team. The prototype submission evaluation will assess innovation, technical merit, and readiness level advancement relative to the baseline technology described in the solicitation background. Offerors must ship hardware prototypes to the designated government laboratory no fewer than ten (10) business days prior to the evaluation session. As part of the Phase 1 technical submission, offerors shall provide a Concept of Operations narrative not to exceed fifteen pages describing how the proposed solution will support the agency's mission requirements during the base period of performance. This narrative serves an analytical and planning function and should not be construed as a request for a working prototype or functional demonstration model. The Department of Transportation, Federal Aviation Administration, solicits technical and price proposals for development of the Aeronautical Information Management Replacement System. Given the safety-critical nature of the system, the FAA mandates compliance with DO-178C and requires the use of traditional SDLC sequential phases throughout the program lifecycle. The contractor shall complete and obtain Government approval of all systems engineering artifacts associated with each development phase before proceeding to subsequent phases. The development sequence shall include: System Requirements Review, Software Requirements Review, Preliminary Design Review, Critical Design Review, Software Build and Integration, Software Qualification Testing, System Acceptance Testing, and Operational Readiness Review. A fixed requirements baseline shall be established following Government approval of the System and Software Requirements Specifications. The contractor shall employ rigorous phase gate controls, and no phase transition shall occur without documented Government authorization. Traceability matrices shall be maintained from requirements through test.

Example 2:
The Department of Energy, National Nuclear Security Administration, requires that the contractor employ a waterfall methodology for the development of the Nuclear Material Accountability and Control Information System. This requirement reflects NNSA policy mandating structured, auditable development processes for systems handling Safeguards and Security information. The contractor shall execute all software development under clearly defined sequential phases: Requirements Definition, Functional Design, Detailed Design, Software Construction, Software Testing, and System Deployment. Phase gate reviews shall be scheduled and completed at the conclusion of each phase, with a formal Government Decision Authority review required before transition to the next phase. All requirements identified during the Requirements Definition phase shall be placed under configuration control as the fixed requirements baseline. Changes to this baseline require submission of a formal Request for Change, independent cost and schedule impact assessment, and written approval from the NNSA Contracting Officer's Representative and Configuration Control Board.

Example 3:
The Department of Defense, Defense Logistics Agency, requires development of the Materiel Tracking and Accountability System under a waterfall development model consistent with MIL-STD-498 and DoD 5000.02 acquisition guidelines. The contractor shall conduct all software development activities in strict sequential phases including Software Requirements Analysis, Preliminary Design, Detailed Design, Coding and Unit Testing, Computer Software Component Integration and Testing, Computer Software Configuration Item Testing, and System Integration and Testing. The V-model verification and validation framework shall be applied throughout, with each left-side development phase paired with a corresponding right-side testing activity. Phase gate reviews, including the System Requirements Review, Preliminary Design Review, and Critical Design Review, shall be conducted at prescribed milestones. Entrance and exit criteria for each phase gate are defined in Attachment J-3. No phase may commence without written Government approval documenting satisfaction of all exit criteria from the preceding phase.

SET-ASIDE INFORMATION: This acquisition is a 100% HUBZone Small Business Set-Aside. Only offers from Historically Underutilized Business Zone (HUBZone) small business concerns are eligible for award. Offerors must be certified by the Small Business Administration as a qualified HUBZone small business concern at the time of offer submission and at the time of award. This requirement is being set aside for HUBZone small businesses pursuant to 13 C.F.R. Part 126 and FAR 19.1305. The North American Industry Classification System (NAICS) code for this procurement is 561730, with a corresponding small business size standard of $9.0 million. Contractors must maintain their HUBZone certification throughout the performance period. Proof of current SBA HUBZone certification must be included with the offer package. Awards made under this solicitation will count toward the agency's HUBZone contracting goals as established under the Small Business Act. Any offeror that is not a certified HUBZone small business concern will be considered non-responsive and will be rejected without further evaluation. Questions regarding HUBZone eligibility should be directed to the SBA at hubzone@sba.gov prior to submission.

Example 2:
DEPARTMENT OF TRANSPORTATION
Federal Highway Administration
Solicitation Number: DTFH6124R00034
Program: Transportation Planning and Data Analysis

AGENCY SET-ASIDE DETERMINATION:

After conducting market research in accordance with FAR Part 10, the Contracting Officer has determined that this requirement shall be set aside exclusively for HUBZone small business concerns. This HUBZone set-aside is established under the authority of FAR 19.1305 and the Small Business Act, 15 U.S.C. § 657a. To be eligible to receive award under this solicitation, offerors must (1) be certified as a HUBZone small business concern by the SBA, (2) be registered in SAM with an active HUBZone designation, and (3) continue to qualify as a HUBZone small business through the duration of contract performance. The NAICS code for this acquisition is 541490, with a size standard of $19.0 million. Offerors must include their SBA-issued HUBZone certification number in their proposal cover letter. The Government will not consider offers from firms that do not hold an active HUBZone certification. Estimated contract value is $2.4 million over a base year plus four option years.

Example 3:
DEPARTMENT OF ENERGY
National Energy Technology Laboratory
Solicitation Reference: DE-SOL-0010988
Scope: Mechanical Engineering and Technical Support Services

NOTICE OF HUBZone SET-ASIDE: In accordance with FAR Subpart 19.13 and the Small Business Act, 15 U.S.C. § 657a, this solicitation is being conducted as a competitive HUBZone small business set-aside. The Department of Energy has determined that this requirement is appropriate for the HUBZone program based on current market research indicating the availability of multiple qualified HUBZone small business concerns capable of fulfilling the requirement at fair and reasonable prices. To qualify for this set-aside, firms must be certified as HUBZone small business concerns by the U.S. Small Business Administration. The HUBZone certification must be active and in good standing at the time of proposal submission. NAICS code 541330 (Engineering Services) applies with a size standard of $25.5 million. The Government will independently verify HUBZone certification status through SBA records. FAR 52.219-3 is included in this solicitation. Offerors must also comply with the HUBZone performance of work requirement, ensuring that at least 50% of the contract's labor cost is performed by HUBZone employees. The contract is anticipated to be a cost-plus-fixed-fee type with a period of performance of five years.

Nevada State Office

L.4 INSTRUCTIONS TO OFFERORS — ELIGIBILITY AND SET-ASIDE REQUIREMENTS

This solicitation is issued as a 100% HUBZone Small Business Set-Aside. In accordance with Federal Acquisition Regulation 19.1305 and the Small Business Act, only Historically Underutilized Business Zone (HUBZone) certified small business concerns are eligible to receive award under this solicitation. The Bureau of Land Management has reviewed available market data and concluded that a reasonable expectation exists of receiving offers from not fewer than two HUBZone small business concerns at prices that will be fair and reasonable to the Government.

The requirement encompasses geospatial data collection, aerial survey support, and land boundary mapping services across BLM-administered public lands in Nevada and eastern California. NAICS code 541370 (Surveying and Mapping Services) applies, with a size standard of $19.0 million in average annual receipts. The Government contemplates a single award, firm-fixed-price contract with a one-year base period and up to four one-year option periods, subject to the availability of appropriations.

All offerors responding to this solicitation must submit with their proposal package: (1) a copy of their current SBA HUBZone certification letter or a screenshot from the SBA HUBZone portal confirming active certified status; and (2) a signed certification that the offeror will maintain HUBZone eligibility throughout performance. The contracting officer will independently verify HUBZone status in SAM.gov and the SBA certification database. Offerors found to lack current, valid HUBZone certification at any point during the evaluation or award process will be excluded from further consideration.

The clause FAR 52.219-3, Notice of HUBZone Set-Aside or Sole Source Award, is incorporated in full text in Section I of this solicitation. Awards made under this contract will be applied toward BLM's HUBZone small business contracting goals as reported annually to the SBA.

Example 1:
The Department of Veterans Affairs seeks a qualified contractor to assume responsibility for the continuing work on the Veterans Benefits Management System (VBMS). The selected vendor will inherit the existing system architecture, currently maintained by the incumbent contractor whose period of performance concludes on September 30, 2024. The incoming contractor must demonstrate proven experience onboarding into a legacy codebase without service disruption. Transition activities shall include a mandatory 90-day knowledge transfer period during which the outgoing and incoming contractors will operate concurrently. The Government requires that the new contractor review all existing technical documentation, data dictionaries, and system design records prior to assuming full operational responsibility. Offerors must describe in their technical approach how they will manage risk during transition from incumbent operations, including how they will handle undocumented components of the existing system. Continuity of service to Veterans during the transition period is a non-negotiable requirement, and the Government will evaluate all proposals on the basis of transition risk mitigation strategies.

Example 2:
The Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) is seeking a qualified contractor to configure and deploy a commercial off-the-shelf customer relationship management solution to support the Veterans Benefits Administration's case intake and tracking functions. The Government has determined that Salesforce Government Cloud meets the functional requirements identified in the market research phase and represents the most cost-effective approach. The contractor shall be responsible for configuring Salesforce workflows, custom objects, permission sets, and approval processes in alignment with VA business rules. No custom software development is anticipated under this effort. The contractor shall also be responsible for data migration from legacy Access databases, user acceptance testing coordination, and end-user training for approximately 400 VBA personnel across regional offices. The period of performance is a twelve-month base period with two twelve-month option periods. All work shall comply with VA Handbook 6500, FISMA requirements, and FedRAMP authorization standards applicable to the selected commercial solution.

Example 3:
The Department of Veterans Affairs, Office of Information and Technology, hereby issues this Request for Proposal for the modernization and ongoing sustainment of its legacy benefits processing systems. The scope of work encompasses system architecture redesign, application development, data migration, testing and quality assurance, training, and help desk support. Based on the complexity and mission-critical nature of this effort, the VA requires the contractor to provide a dedicated project team of no less than twenty (20) full-time personnel. The staffing complement shall include, at a minimum, a Program Manager, an Agile Release Train Engineer, five (5) software developers with demonstrated Java and cloud platform experience, three (3) database administrators, two (2) DevSecOps engineers, a cybersecurity SME, two (2) business analysts, a training lead, and requisite quality assurance personnel. All staff must be U.S. citizens and obtain a VA Suitability determination prior to commencing work. The offeror's staffing plan must demonstrate that all twenty positions will be filled within thirty (30) days of contract award, with acceptable résumés for each proposed individual submitted as part of the technical proposal. The Government reserves the right to reject proposed personnel who do not meet stated qualifications.

The Department of Veterans Affairs, Office of Information and Technology, hereby solicits proposals for the design, development, and deployment of a custom Benefits Case Management System (BCMS) to modernize the agency's legacy adjudication infrastructure. The Government seeks a contractor to deliver a cloud-native, microservices-based platform capable of processing disability compensation claims with enhanced automation and decision-support capabilities. The system shall integrate with existing VA enterprise services including the Master Veteran Index and the Electronic Health Record system via RESTful APIs. The period of performance is anticipated to be a base year of twelve months with four option years. The contractor shall provide full software development lifecycle services including requirements analysis, system architecture, iterative development, testing, security assessment and authorization support, and operations and maintenance. The solution must comply with FedRAMP Moderate authorization requirements and adhere to Section 508 accessibility standards. Offerors shall demonstrate prior experience developing large-scale case management or benefits processing systems within the federal health or benefits domain. Proposals will be evaluated on a best value tradeoff basis considering technical approach, past performance, and price. The Government anticipates a single award Indefinite Delivery Indefinite Quantity contract with a ceiling of $48 million. All development shall comply with VA Technical Reference Model guidelines and the contractor shall participate in regular sprint reviews and architecture governance forums with VA stakeholders.

Example 1:
The Department of Transportation, Federal Aviation Administration, is soliciting for acquisition support and program management assistance services for the Aviation Safety organization. The breadth of this requirement includes acquisition planning, cost estimating, source selection support, contract administration, program schedule management, risk management, independent verification and validation, and stakeholder communications. Due to the volume of active acquisitions and the complexity of ongoing program portfolios, the FAA has determined that a team of no fewer than eleven (11) full-time contractor personnel is required to execute this work satisfactorily. The required team shall include a Senior Program Manager with at least fifteen (15) years of federal acquisition experience, a Cost Estimator with ICEAA certification, two (2) Contract Specialists with FAC-C Level II or equivalent credentials, two (2) Program Analysts, a Scheduler proficient in Primavera P6, a Risk Manager, an IVV Lead, and two (2) junior analysts providing research and documentation support. The contractor shall assign named individuals to each position prior to contract award; substitution of key personnel during the first twelve (12) months of performance is prohibited without written Contracting Officer approval. Staffing levels at or above eleven personnel shall be maintained throughout the entire period of performance.

Example 2:
The Environmental Protection Agency, Office of Water, requires scientific, technical, and programmatic support services to advance water quality monitoring, data analysis, regulatory development, and outreach activities under the Clean Water Act and Safe Drinking Water Act programs. The Government has estimated the total level of effort at approximately 28,000 labor hours annually, distributed across multiple task areas requiring diverse scientific and regulatory expertise. To execute this level of effort and maintain continuity across concurrent task orders, the contractor shall maintain a dedicated team of no fewer than sixteen (16) full-time equivalent personnel. The team composition shall include environmental scientists, hydrologists, chemists, toxicologists, policy analysts, GIS specialists, technical writers, and program support staff with direct experience supporting EPA regulatory programs. The offeror's staffing plan must demonstrate that the sixteen-person team collectively possesses the competencies required to address all task areas identified in the Performance Work Statement. The Government will evaluate the staffing plan for both the depth of individual expertise and the overall team's capacity to absorb new task orders within existing resources. The contractor shall not reduce staffing below sixteen FTEs without prior written approval from the Contracting Officer's Representative and a corresponding contract modification.

Example 3:
The Department of Transportation Federal Highway Administration hereby requests proposals for an enterprise asset management platform supporting the National Bridge Inspection Program. Consistent with FHWA's technology modernization policy, this acquisition mandates a COTS solution and explicitly prohibits any custom application development. Vendors shall not include development labor categories, software engineering hours, or coding deliverables within their proposed staffing plans. The work under this contract is scoped as configuration only, covering asset taxonomy definition, inspection workflow setup, geospatial layer integration using the platform's native mapping connectors, and report template customization through the product's built-in report designer. The Contractor must demonstrate that all proposed capabilities are available in the current production release of the software and do not depend on future product updates or vendor commitments. Proposals referencing software development lifecycles, code repositories, or testing environments associated with new feature creation will be considered outside the scope of this solicitation and may result in the proposal being found technically unacceptable.

The Department of Transportation, Federal Railroad Administration, Office of Safety, hereby solicits proposals for railroad safety inspection support, data analysis, technical assistance, and outreach services in support of the FRA's national track, motive power, and equipment safety programs. The scope of this contract encompasses field inspection accompaniment and documentation support, safety data system maintenance and analysis, regulatory guidance development, training curriculum design and delivery, and stakeholder coordination with Class I, Class II, and short line railroad operators across all nine FRA regional offices. Given the geographic distribution of required activities and the concurrent demands of multiple programmatic work streams, the Government has determined that a minimum of eleven (11) full-time equivalent personnel must be dedicated to this contract at all times during active performance. The required team shall include a Program Manager with prior federal transportation safety experience, a Deputy Program Manager, three (3) Senior Safety Analysts with demonstrated knowledge of 49 CFR Parts 213, 229, and 238, two (2) Data Scientists experienced with FRA's Safety Data Portal and related rail incident databases, a Training and Curriculum Specialist, a Regulatory Affairs Specialist, and at least two (2) Field Support Coordinators capable of traveling to regional offices as required by the work plan. The offeror's Management Volume shall include an organizational chart, staffing matrix, and individual résumés for all key personnel and non-key personnel proposed under each labor category. The Government will evaluate proposed staffing for both numerical adequacy and technical depth, and proposals that do not demonstrate a minimum of eleven dedicated full-time equivalents shall be assessed as failing the staffing threshold evaluation criterion.

Example 1:
The Department of Health and Human Services, Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services, requires contractor support for its Medicare Administrative Contractor oversight and audit readiness functions. The contractor shall provide program integrity, audit management, financial analysis, IT systems support, and stakeholder communications services to enable CMS to effectively oversee a portfolio of regional contractors administering Medicare Parts A and B. The anticipated scope of work, based on historical task order volume and complexity, requires the contractor to maintain a core team of no fewer than twenty-two (22) full-time personnel. Required positions include a Program Manager, two (2) Deputy Program Managers each responsible for distinct functional areas, five (5) Program Integrity Analysts, three (3) Financial Analysts with CPA credentials, four (4) IT Specialists supporting audit management systems, two (2) Communications Specialists, a Quality Assurance Manager, two (2) Data Analysts, and supporting administrative staff. The contractor shall provide monthly staffing reports to the Contracting Officer's Representative certifying that the twenty-two-person staffing minimum has been maintained and identifying any vacancies, planned departures, or substitutions. The Government reserves the right to assess deductions from invoices for any period in which the contractor fails to maintain the minimum staffing level.

Example 2:
DEPARTMENT OF DEFENSE
DEFENSE LOGISTICS AGENCY
SOLICITATION: SPE600-24-R-0412
FOOD SERVICE MANAGEMENT

This solicitation is issued as a Women-Owned Small Business (WOSB) set-aside. In accordance with FAR 19.1505 and the authority granted under the Small Business Act, the Defense Logistics Agency Troop Support has restricted this acquisition for food service management and dining facility operations to WOSB concerns only. Market research confirmed that a reasonable expectation exists that at least two responsible WOSB firms will submit offers and that award will be made at a fair and reasonable price within the meaning of FAR 19.1505(b). The NAICS code for this procurement is 722310 (Food Service Contractors) and the applicable size standard is $47.0 million in average annual receipts. Firms must be certified as Women-Owned Small Businesses through the SBA's certify.sba.gov portal and maintain that certification throughout the contract period. The contract will cover food service operations at two military installations in the Mid-Atlantic region, with a base period of performance of one year and four one-year option periods. The Government reserves the right to cancel this set-aside if it determines that insufficient competition exists among WOSB concerns. See Section L for complete proposal preparation instructions.

Example 3:
The Department of Energy, National Nuclear Security Administration, is issuing this solicitation for nuclear security program support services at a classified facility in the Southeast United States. The scope includes physical security assessments, force-on-force exercise planning and execution, vulnerability analysis, security technology evaluation, safeguards and security training development, and regulatory compliance support. Given the classified nature of operations, the 24-hour operational tempo of the facility, and the technical complexity of nuclear security disciplines, the contractor must provide a minimum of thirteen (13) cleared personnel at all times. The required team includes a Program Director with a minimum of twenty (20) years of combined military and nuclear security experience, a Deputy Program Director, three (3) Physical Security Specialists, two (2) Force-on-Force Exercise Specialists, two (2) Safeguards and Security Analysts, a Security Technology Engineer, a Training Specialist, and two (2) Compliance Analysts. All personnel must possess active Q clearances at time of award. Offerors shall submit a staffing plan demonstrating that all thirteen positions are filled by named individuals meeting the qualification requirements set forth in Section J, Attachment 3. Personnel proposed for this effort may be subject to Government approval prior to assignment.

The offeror's Staffing Plan, submitted as Tab B of the Technical Volume, must demonstrate that all thirty full-time equivalent positions will be filled no later than the Phase-In completion date and maintained without interruption. The Government will conduct quarterly staffing audits and may assess deductions under the Quality Assurance Surveillance Plan for any period during which actual staffing falls below the contracted minimums.

NAICS Code 541330 applies with a size standard of $19.0 million. The two-in-ten rule has been applied and market research confirms the availability of two or more responsible small business concerns capable of performing this work at fair market prices. Offers submitted by large businesses will not be evaluated and shall be considered nonresponsive. By submission of an offer, the offeror represents that it is a small business concern under the size standard associated with the above NAICS code. This set-aside determination is final and no requests for waiver will be entertained.

Example 2:
ACQUISITION TYPE: Total Small Business Set-Aside. The Department of Justice, Bureau of Prisons, announces this competitive procurement restricted exclusively to small business concerns. The required services encompass medical and dental staffing support for federal correctional institutions in the Southeast Region. The applicable NAICS code is 561320 (Temporary Help Services), with a size standard of $34.0 million in average annual receipts. This set-aside is established under the authority of FAR 19.502-2 and FAR 52.219-6. By submission of a proposal, each offeror certifies that it is a small business concern under the applicable size standard. Offerors must be registered in SAM.gov with an active and current small business self-certification prior to solicitation closing. The Government has made an affirmative determination that sufficient small business sources exist for this requirement. Proposals from concerns that do not qualify as small will be deemed nonresponsive and eliminated from consideration without further evaluation. Questions relating to size eligibility should be referred to the SBA's Washington Metropolitan Area District Office.

Example 3:
TOTAL SET-ASIDE FOR SMALL BUSINESS CONCERNS. The requirement encompasses facilities operations and maintenance services for CBP field offices located within the Southwest Border region. The applicable NAICS code is 561210 (Facilities Support Services), with a small business size standard of $47.0 million in average annual receipts. Pursuant to FAR 52.219-6, only small business concerns may submit offers under this solicitation. Large businesses that submit offers will receive no evaluation consideration and will be deemed ineligible for award. The Contracting Officer has documented in the acquisition plan that a reasonable expectation exists for receiving offers from at least two responsible small business concerns capable of meeting the stated requirements at competitive prices.

Example 1:
The Department of Transportation, Federal Aviation Administration, solicits technical and price proposals for development of the Aeronautical Information Management Replacement System. Given the safety-critical nature of the system, the FAA mandates compliance with DO-178C and requires the use of traditional SDLC sequential phases throughout the program lifecycle. The contractor shall complete and obtain Government approval of all systems engineering artifacts associated with each development phase before proceeding to subsequent phases. The development sequence shall include: System Requirements Review, Software Requirements Review, Preliminary Design Review, Critical Design Review, Software Build and Integration, Software Qualification Testing, System Acceptance Testing, and Operational Readiness Review. A fixed requirements baseline shall be established following Government approval of the System and Software Requirements Specifications. The contractor shall employ rigorous phase gate controls, and no phase transition shall occur without documented Government authorization. Traceability matrices shall be maintained from requirements through test.

Example 2:
The General Services Administration, Public Buildings Service, hereby solicits offers for development of the Facilities Asset Lifecycle Tracking System. In accordance with GSA Order CIO 2100.1, the contractor shall utilize standard software lifecycle development practices as the governing development framework for this engagement. The project shall be organized into five sequential phases: Requirements Gathering, Architecture and Design, Development, Testing and Quality Assurance, and Deployment and Transition. Phase-to-phase progression is contingent upon successful completion of formal Government review and written authorization to proceed. The contractor is expressly prohibited from initiating coding activities prior to approval of the Functional Requirements Document and System Architecture Document. A fixed requirements baseline shall be established no later than 60 calendar days after contract award. Subsequent changes to baselined requirements must be processed through the formal Engineering Change Proposal process and approved in writing by the Contracting Officer.

Example 3:
The Department of Defense, Defense Logistics Agency, requires development of the Materiel Tracking and Accountability System under a waterfall development model consistent with MIL-STD-498 and DoD 5000.02 acquisition guidelines. The contractor shall conduct all software development activities in strict sequential phases including Software Requirements Analysis, Preliminary Design, Detailed Design, Coding and Unit Testing, Computer Software Component Integration and Testing, Computer Software Configuration Item Testing, and System Integration and Testing. The V-model verification and validation framework shall be applied throughout, with each left-side development phase paired with a corresponding right-side testing activity. Phase gate reviews, including the System Requirements Review, Preliminary Design Review, and Critical Design Review, shall be conducted at prescribed milestones. Entrance and exit criteria for each phase gate are defined in Attachment J-3. No phase may commence without written Government approval documenting satisfaction of all exit criteria from the preceding phase.

The Department of Transportation, Federal Aviation Administration, releases this Request for Proposal for the development of the Air Traffic Control Facility Maintenance Scheduling System. Given the safety-critical nature of this system and FAA Order 1370.92 requirements governing acquisition lifecycle management, the Government mandates use of a V-model development approach as the governing framework for all software and system engineering activities under this contract. The V-model shall be implemented such that requirements analysis, system design, architectural design, and module design on the decomposition side correspond directly to unit testing, integration testing, system testing, and acceptance testing activities on the verification side, with coding representing the apex of the V. All left-side decomposition phases must be formally completed and accepted by the FAA Contracting Officer's Representative before corresponding right-side verification activities may begin. The contractor shall establish and place under configuration management a fixed requirements baseline no later than 60 calendar days after contract award. The System Requirements Review and Preliminary Design Review constitute mandatory phase gate milestones requiring formal Government approval prior to continuation. Baseline changes initiated after the Critical Design Review shall require a written waiver from the Program Executive Officer in addition to standard Engineering Change Proposal processing. The contractor shall provide complete traceability from each requirement through design artifacts, code modules, and test cases.

Prospective vendors must be capable of providing five-day-per-week service within this funding constraint. Example 2:

The OIG seeks quotations for professional transcription and translation services in support of investigative activities across a six-month base ordering period. A single Blanket Purchase Agreement will be established with one vendor. The maximum spend authority under the BPA shall be limited to $75,000 for the base period. Calls placed against this agreement may not in aggregate exceed this ceiling, and no modification will be pursued to raise the authorized funding level. The Government estimates approximately 1,200 hours of transcription and 300 pages of certified translation will be required, though actual order volumes may vary. Vendors must confirm in their quotations that their proposed unit rates, when applied against these estimated quantities, yield a total that falls within the $75,000 maximum. The period of performance is six months. This procurement is strictly limited to the acquisition of an existing commercial solution. No software development is authorized under any circumstances. Offerors shall not propose to build, code, or engineer new application modules in response to agency-specific requirements. The Government's intent is to leverage a proven, market-available product and adapt internal business processes to align with the tool's existing capabilities. Configuration of user permissions, workflow routing, document templates, and integration with existing financial systems via standard APIs is permissible and expected. Offerors must demonstrate that the proposed platform has been deployed in at least three federal agency environments. Proposals that include statements of work referencing sprint cycles, agile development backlogs, or custom feature engineering will be evaluated unfavorably and may be deemed non-compliant with this solicitation's scope restrictions.

Example 2:
The Department of the Interior Bureau of Indian Affairs is soliciting offers for a grants management information system to replace legacy tracking tools currently in use across twelve regional offices. The Government's acquisition strategy for this requirement is based on the procurement of a commercially available, pre-built platform. No custom software development is authorized under this contract. The BIA recognizes that a COTS solution required approach may necessitate adjustment of internal grant lifecycle processes to conform to the system's standard workflows. This trade-off is acceptable to the Government and has been incorporated into the acquisition planning assumptions. The Contractor's role is limited to system configuration, user acceptance testing support, training delivery, and help desk services during a twelve-month transition period. Any proposal that includes line items for software engineers, developers, architects engaged in coding activity, or application development environments will be evaluated as non-compliant with the stated scope. Questions regarding permissible configuration activities should be submitted through the official solicitation question-and-answer process.

Example 3:
The Social Security Administration is seeking a vendor to implement a COTS document management and workflow automation solution within the Office of Disability Determinations. This is a firm-fixed-price acquisition for commercial software licensing, implementation, and ongoing support. The scope of this contract is expressly limited to the deployment and configuration of an existing commercial product. No custom software development will be permitted. Offerors may not propose to write new code, develop new application interfaces, or engineer bespoke processing logic to satisfy SSA-specific business rules. Where the commercial product includes configurable rules engines, form builders, or workflow designers, the Contractor may use those tools to tailor the solution to SSA requirements. Such activities constitute configuration and are within scope. However, any effort requiring software compilation, version branching, or source code modification is strictly prohibited and shall not be included in any proposal. SSA reserves the right to reject any proposal that conflates configuration services with software development activity.

The National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA), Goddard Space Flight Center, requires each offeror responding to this solicitation to submit a working prototype as part of the technical proposal package. The prototype submission is evaluated as a standalone technical factor and accounts for forty percent (40%) of the total technical score per Section M. Prototype submissions that fail automated threshold testing, as defined in Attachment G, will not advance to the demonstration session and will be rated Unacceptable for the prototype factor.

DEPARTMENT OF HOMELAND SECURITY
Federal Emergency Management Agency
Acquisition Division

AMENDMENT 0003 TO SOLICITATION HSFE80-24-R-0044

The purpose of this amendment is to respond to offeror questions received during the question-and-answer period and to revise certain solicitation provisions. All other terms and conditions remain unchanged.

Offeror Question 14: Does this solicitation qualify as a HUBZone set-aside? Our firm holds active HUBZone certification and we want to confirm whether we are competing in a restricted pool.

Contracting Officer Response: No. This solicitation is not a HUBZone set-aside. The requirement has been designated as a total small business set-aside under FAR 19.502-2, based on market research indicating a reasonable expectation of receiving offers from at least two responsible small business concerns at fair market prices. HUBZone certification is neither required nor provides exclusive eligibility for this competition. Any small business concern meeting the applicable NAICS size standard of $20.5 million in average annual receipts may submit an offer.

Offerors should not confuse the small business set-aside designation with a HUBZone-specific restriction. While FEMA recognizes and values the HUBZone program's mission of stimulating economic development in historically underutilized areas, the agency's set-aside decision for this particular requirement was made independently of that program. HUBZone-certified firms are welcome and encouraged to compete but do so on the same basis as all other small businesses.

Offeror Question 15: Will HUBZone price evaluation preferences apply?

Contracting Officer Response: No. Price evaluation preferences under FAR 19.1307 apply only in full and open competitions, not in small business set-asides. Because this solicitation is already restricted to small businesses, no additional HUBZone price preference will be applied during evaluation. Award will be made to the offeror whose proposal represents the best value to the Government under the evaluation criteria established in Section M.

The Department of Energy, National Nuclear Security Administration, requires that the contractor employ a waterfall methodology for the development of the Nuclear Material Accountability and Control Information System. This requirement reflects NNSA policy mandating structured, auditable development processes for systems handling Safeguards and Security information. The contractor shall execute all software development under clearly defined sequential phases: Requirements Definition, Functional Design, Detailed Design, Software Construction, Software Testing, and System Deployment. Phase gate reviews shall be scheduled and completed at the conclusion of each phase, with a formal Government Decision Authority review required before transition to the next phase. All requirements identified during the Requirements Definition phase shall be placed under configuration control as the fixed requirements baseline. Changes to this baseline require submission of a formal Request for Change, independent cost and schedule impact assessment, and written approval from the NNSA Contracting Officer's Representative and Configuration Control Board.

Example 2:
The Department of Veterans Affairs requires the contractor to employ a waterfall methodology for the development of the Veterans Benefits Management System Enhancement. All work shall proceed through sequential phases including requirements analysis, system design, implementation, testing, deployment, and maintenance. Each phase must be fully completed and formally accepted by the Contracting Officer's Representative prior to initiation of the subsequent phase. The contractor shall maintain a fixed requirements baseline established during the requirements phase, which shall not be altered without formal change control board approval and contract modification. Deliverables associated with each phase gate must be submitted, reviewed, and approved before any downstream development activities commence. The contractor shall provide a detailed Waterfall Project Plan within 30 days of contract award, identifying all phase entry and exit criteria, milestone dates, and associated deliverables. Monthly status reports shall track progress against the approved baseline schedule and document any variances exceeding five percent of planned effort.

Example 3:
The Department of Defense, Defense Logistics Agency, requires development of the Materiel Tracking and Accountability System under a waterfall development model consistent with MIL-STD-498 and DoD 5000.02 acquisition guidelines. The contractor shall conduct all software development activities in strict sequential phases including Software Requirements Analysis, Preliminary Design, Detailed Design, Coding and Unit Testing, Computer Software Component Integration and Testing, Computer Software Configuration Item Testing, and System Integration and Testing. The V-model verification and validation framework shall be applied throughout, with each left-side development phase paired with a corresponding right-side testing activity. Phase gate reviews, including the System Requirements Review, Preliminary Design Review, and Critical Design Review, shall be conducted at prescribed milestones. Entrance and exit criteria for each phase gate are defined in Attachment J-3. No phase may commence without written Government approval documenting satisfaction of all exit criteria from the preceding phase.

The Department of Homeland Security, Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), Office of Human Capital, requires professional human resources support, workforce analytics, and organizational development services to support approximately 20,000 employees across domestic and international field offices.

NAICS Code: 541612 (Human Resources Consulting Services)
Size Standard: $35.0 million average annual receipts
Set-Aside Type: Women-Owned Small Business (WOSB) — not restricted to Economically Disadvantaged Women-Owned Small Business (EDWOSB) concerns

Service requirements include, but are not limited to: position classification and compensation analysis, performance management program design, succession planning facilitation, employee engagement survey administration and analysis, and HR policy development and review. The contractor shall provide senior-level HR consultants with demonstrated federal agency experience.

To be eligible for award under this WOSB set-aside, offerors must be certified as Women-Owned Small Businesses through the SBA's certification program administered at certify.sba.gov. Third-party certifications are no longer accepted as a basis for WOSB eligibility pursuant to the SBA Final Rule effective October 15, 2020. Joint ventures must comply with all requirements set forth at 13 CFR 127.506 and must be registered as a separate legal entity in SAM.gov prior to submission.

The Government anticipates awarding a single Indefinite Delivery/Indefinite Quantity (IDIQ) contract with Time-and-Materials and Firm-Fixed-Price task order types. The estimated ceiling value is $8.5 million over a five-year ordering period.
