The contractor shall implement Agile development methodologies throughout the period of performance. All software development activities shall be conducted using Scrum framework, with two-week sprint cycles. The contractor shall conduct daily stand-up meetings, sprint planning sessions, sprint reviews, and sprint retrospectives in accordance with Scrum ceremonies. A certified Scrum Master shall be designated and responsible for facilitating all Agile ceremonies and removing impediments to team progress. The Government Product Owner shall participate in sprint planning and backlog grooming sessions to prioritize user stories and acceptance criteria. The contractor shall maintain a product backlog in a Government-approved project management tool and provide sprint velocity metrics as part of monthly progress reporting. All deliverables shall be produced incrementally, with a potentially shippable product increment demonstrated at the conclusion of each sprint. The contractor shall adhere to the Agile Manifesto principles and demonstrate continuous improvement through retrospective action items documented and tracked across sprints.

Example 2:
The Social Security Administration requires all IT development work performed under this Blanket Purchase Agreement to comply with SSA's Agile Development Standards. Offerors shall describe their Agile delivery model in the technical approach and demonstrate how their proposed methodology aligns with Scrum or Kanban frameworks. Contractors shall conduct sprint planning meetings with SSA's Product Owner to decompose epics into sprint-ready user stories with acceptance criteria no later than the iteration planning session preceding each sprint. The contractor shall update the Government's enterprise project management tool daily with sprint board status, impediments, and burndown data. Demonstration of working software to SSA stakeholders shall occur at the end of every sprint, and the Government reserves the right to redirect backlog priorities following each sprint review based on mission needs. The contractor shall implement Definition of Ready criteria to ensure user stories are sufficiently detailed prior to sprint commitment. Quarterly business reviews shall include Agile performance metrics, including average sprint velocity, defect density, and sprint goal completion percentage.

Example 3:
Under this task order issued against the NASA Solutions for Enterprise-Wide Procurement contract, the contractor shall manage and execute all software development activities in accordance with Agile methodology. The contractor shall implement a Scrum-based framework and shall operate all development teams on a uniform two-week sprint cadence synchronized with the program's release train schedule. Sprint planning shall be completed within four hours for a two-week sprint, and the contractor shall ensure that sprint goals are clearly articulated and agreed upon by the Government Product Owner prior to sprint commencement. The contractor shall maintain a Definition of Done that includes code review completion, unit test coverage thresholds, security scanning, and Product Owner acceptance. Working software shall be deployed to a staging environment at the end of each sprint for Government validation. The contractor shall produce and maintain an Agile project charter documenting team norms, Agile ceremony schedules, tooling agreements, and escalation procedures. Release planning artifacts including feature roadmaps and program increment objectives shall be reviewed and approved by the NASA Contracting Officer's Representative on a quarterly basis.

Data Rights and Deliverable Schedule. All custom software developed under this contract shall be delivered to the Government as open-source, government-purpose rights code committed to the agency's designated version control repository on a continuous basis. The concept of a formal software delivery milestone — common in contracts structured around sequential development phases — does not apply here. There is no single point at which 'the software' is delivered; rather, functional increments are continuously integrated and made available for Government inspection and use throughout the period of performance. The contractor shall not withhold code pending completion of documentation packages or formal phase reviews. Sprint review sessions, conducted bi-weekly, serve as the mechanism for Government acceptance of completed user stories and constitute the contractual delivery event for purposes of invoice submission. Documentation artifacts such as architecture decision records, user guides, and API specifications shall be maintained as living documents within the project repository rather than produced as discrete waterfall-era deliverables at predefined milestones. The Government acknowledges that this approach requires greater ongoing engagement from agency product owners and commits to providing appropriately empowered personnel to fulfill that role throughout the contract period.

All contractor work shall be conducted at the Savannah River Site, located in Aiken, SC 29808. The radiological, nuclear safety, and national security considerations associated with this facility categorically preclude the performance of any work activities from off-site locations. All contractor personnel must complete site-specific radiological worker training, obtain a DOE security clearance as applicable, and physically badge into the controlled area assigned to their scope of work each day. Given the classified nature of operations, the 24-hour operational tempo of the facility, and the technical complexity of nuclear security disciplines, the contractor must provide a minimum of thirteen (13) cleared personnel at all times. The required team includes a Program Director with a minimum of twenty (20) years of combined military and nuclear security experience, a Deputy Program Director, three (3) Physical Security Specialists, two (2) Force-on-Force Exercise Specialists, two (2) Safeguards and Security Analysts, a Security Technology Engineer, a Training Specialist, and two (2) Compliance Analysts. All personnel must possess active Q clearances at time of award. Offerors shall submit a staffing plan demonstrating that all thirteen positions are filled by named individuals meeting the qualification requirements set forth in Section J, Attachment 3. Personnel proposed for this effort may be subject to Government approval prior to assignment.

The contractor shall transfer all rights, title, and interest in developed software to the Government and publish code to GSA's public GitHub repository in accordance with the agency's open source policy.

Example 1:
The General Services Administration hereby issues this solicitation which includes a required design exercise component for all competitive range offerors. Specifically, each offeror must submit a prototype submission demonstrating their proposed citizen-facing web portal solution. The prototype submission shall be a fully navigable, hosted demonstration environment accessible via government-provided credentials. The prototype must include functional user registration, document upload, and status tracking modules. GSA evaluators will engage directly with the prototype during a structured two-hour evaluation session and will simulate end-user workflows as described in Attachment D, User Scenario Matrix. Prototype submissions are due no later than fourteen (14) days prior to the scheduled oral presentations. Offerors are advised that the prototype submission will be evaluated independently from the written technical volume and carries a weight of thirty-five percent (35%) of the total technical score. Any prototype that is unavailable, incomplete, or non-functional during the evaluation session will receive a rating of Unacceptable for that factor.

Example 2:
SOLICITATION NOTICE: The National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA), Goddard Space Flight Center, Office of Procurement, is seeking a qualified contractor to support the development and delivery of prototype sensor calibration hardware for the Earth Science Division. The period of performance for this effort is limited to eight (8) months from date of award. This is a firm-fixed-price, short-term developmental contract and does not include option periods. The contractor shall deliver a minimum of three functional prototype units, all associated technical documentation, test reports, and a lessons-learned summary. All deliverables must be submitted to the NASA Contracting Officer's Representative no later than the final month of performance. Milestone reviews are scheduled at months two, five, and seven. Due to the compressed schedule, offerors must demonstrate immediate availability of key personnel including a Systems Engineer and Hardware Integration Lead. Proposals shall be evaluated on technical capability, schedule feasibility, and price.

Example 3:
The Department of Defense, Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency, hereby solicits proposals under this Broad Agency Announcement and requires all offerors selected for Phase II negotiations to complete a mandatory prototype submission. The prototype submission shall constitute a functional hardware or software artifact demonstrating feasibility of the proposed technical approach as described in Section 3.2 of this solicitation. DARPA program managers will evaluate each prototype submission during a dedicated review session, which may include independent laboratory testing, performance benchmarking, and direct technical questioning of the offeror team. The prototype submission evaluation will assess innovation, technical merit, and readiness level advancement relative to the baseline technology described in the solicitation background. Offerors must ship hardware prototypes to the designated government laboratory no fewer than ten (10) business days prior to the evaluation session. Software prototypes must be delivered via approved media and must execute without modification in the government-provided test environment. DARPA evaluators retain the right to attempt to replicate offeror-reported performance results using independent test procedures.

Detailed prototype submission instructions, including required API documentation and environment access provisioning procedures, are provided in Section L.11.

Example 1:
The Environmental Protection Agency, Office of Acquisition Solutions, is issuing this Request for Proposals for technical support services related to water quality monitoring and sampling at Superfund sites in EPA Region 5. This acquisition will be evaluated using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process in accordance with FAR 15.101-2. Technical acceptability will be evaluated under three pass/fail criteria: Key Personnel Qualifications, Laboratory Accreditation Compliance, and Quality Assurance Plan Adequacy. Proposals must be rated Acceptable on all three criteria to be eligible for award. Technical proposals that exceed minimum requirements will receive no additional consideration. Following determination of technical acceptability, award will be made to the responsible offeror submitting the lowest evaluated total price. The Government has made a written determination that LPTA is the appropriate evaluation approach given the well-defined nature of the work and the availability of qualified contractors capable of performing at minimum standards. Questions regarding the LPTA evaluation criteria must be submitted by the deadline specified in Section L.

Example 2:
The Naval Facilities Engineering Systems Command, Southwest, hereby solicits offers for refuse collection, recycling, and solid waste management services at Naval Base San Diego. This procurement is conducted using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection methodology. Technical evaluation criteria are defined as Acceptable or Unacceptable and include: compliance with all federal, state, and local environmental regulations; demonstration of required equipment and staffing levels; and proof of required licenses and permits. Price evaluation will consider the total evaluated price across the base period and all option years. Award shall be made to the responsible offeror whose proposal is technically acceptable and offers the lowest price. The Government is not seeking best value tradeoffs and will not award to a higher-priced technically acceptable offeror under any circumstances. Offerors must thoroughly address all technical criteria to avoid an Unacceptable rating, which would preclude award regardless of pricing submitted.

Example 3:
SOLICITATION: DEPARTMENT OF ENERGY — OFFICE OF ENVIRONMENTAL MANAGEMENT — SAVANNAH RIVER OPERATIONS OFFICE. This Request for Proposals is issued for specialized radioactive waste characterization support services at the Savannah River Site in Aiken, South Carolina. The period of performance is limited to eight (8) months from date of contract award, with no option periods contemplated. This requirement is being procured as a short-duration task to support accelerated characterization activities required for near-term regulatory compliance milestones. The contractor must provide a qualified radiological engineer, a health physics technician, and appropriate monitoring equipment on-site within fifteen (15) calendar days of award. All work shall conform to applicable DOE Orders, Nuclear Regulatory Commission standards, and site-specific radiological control procedures. The contractor shall deliver a final waste characterization report summarizing sampling results, dose assessments, and waste classification recommendations no later than the penultimate month of performance. Proposals shall be evaluated using best value criteria with technical and management approach equally weighted against price.

This combined synopsis and solicitation is issued by the Department of Energy, Office of Environmental Management, Savannah River Operations Office, for the provision of industrial laundry and uniform services at the Savannah River Site in Aiken, South Carolina. The Government has determined that the use of the lowest price technically acceptable source selection methodology is appropriate for this acquisition given the well-defined, non-complex nature of the requirements. Technical proposals will be evaluated solely on a pass/fail basis against the minimum technical requirements identified in Section C, Statement of Work. To be rated technically acceptable, offerors must demonstrate the capacity to process required garment volumes, meet turnaround timelines, and comply with all applicable health and safety handling standards for potentially contaminated materials. Proposals that fail to satisfy any single mandatory requirement will be rated Unacceptable and excluded from the competition. The Government will award a contract to the responsible offeror with the lowest proposed price from among those proposals rated technically acceptable. No additional credit will be given for technical approaches that surpass minimum requirements. Offerors are encouraged to propose competitive pricing, as price is the sole discriminating factor among technically acceptable proposals under this LPTA evaluation.

Example 1:
The Department of the Interior Bureau of Indian Affairs is soliciting offers for a grants management information system to replace legacy tracking tools currently in use across twelve regional offices. The Government's acquisition strategy for this requirement is based on the procurement of a commercially available, pre-built platform. No custom software development is authorized under this contract. The BIA recognizes that a COTS solution required approach may necessitate adjustment of internal grant lifecycle processes to conform to the system's standard workflows. This trade-off is acceptable to the Government and has been incorporated into the acquisition planning assumptions. The Contractor's role is limited to system configuration, user acceptance testing support, training delivery, and help desk services during a twelve-month transition period. Any proposal that includes line items for software engineers, developers, architects engaged in coding activity, or application development environments will be evaluated as non-compliant with the stated scope. Questions regarding permissible configuration activities should be submitted through the official solicitation question-and-answer process.

Example 2:
The Department of Education, Office of Federal Student Aid (FSA), announces a solicitation for continued development and operational support of the StudentAid.gov platform and its underlying data services. This contract represents continuing work on a platform that serves over 43 million borrowers and applicants annually. The selected contractor will receive a comprehensive handoff package from the incumbent contractor, including all source code repositories, infrastructure-as-code scripts, API documentation, and outstanding feature roadmap commitments. The incoming contractor must demonstrate the capacity to onboard into the existing system rapidly, with mission-critical services such as the Free Application for Federal Student Aid (FAFSA) processing pipeline remaining fully operational throughout transition. FSA's evaluation methodology includes an oral presentation component during which offerors will be asked to describe specific technical challenges encountered when taking over legacy codebases and the resolution approaches applied. Key personnel proposed for this effort must be available to begin transition activities within two weeks of contract award.

Example 3:
The Department of Transportation, Federal Aviation Administration (FAA), requires professional services to support the Air Traffic Organization's En Route Automation Modernization (ERAM) program. This solicitation covers continuing work on operational software that is currently deployed across 20 Air Route Traffic Control Centers nationwide. Contractors must have the technical capacity to engage with a mature legacy codebase written predominantly in Ada and C++, with some components dating to original system development in the late 1990s. The FAA will facilitate a formal transition from the incumbent program support contractor; however, the incoming team must be prepared for gaps in documentation that are common in systems of this age and operational complexity. Offerors must demonstrate past performance on programs of comparable scale where they have successfully taken over an existing system with safety-critical operational requirements. All proposed personnel in key positions must hold current FAA suitability determinations or equivalent federal security clearances at time of award.

The Department of Labor, Employment and Training Administration (ETA), is conducting market research in anticipation of a forthcoming solicitation for a modernized grants management and performance reporting system. ETA administers billions of dollars in federal workforce development grants annually through a network of state workforce agencies, and the existing grants lifecycle platform requires substantial re-engineering to meet current federal data standards, improve user experience for state grantees, and enable more robust performance analytics for program administrators. The contractor will be expected to conduct stakeholder discovery sessions with state agency partners, develop and iterate on functional prototypes, build out the full production system, and support a phased migration from the legacy environment. The target architecture is a cloud-hosted, microservices-based solution deployed on a FedRAMP-authorized infrastructure provider. The system must comply with the DATA Act reporting requirements, OMB grants management standards, and applicable NIST cybersecurity frameworks. Contractors with demonstrated experience in federal grants management workflows, state-federal data exchange standards, and role-based access control implementations are encouraged to respond. The anticipated contract vehicle is a single-award Indefinite Delivery Indefinite Quantity with task orders issued for distinct phases of work. Evaluation criteria will include technical approach and understanding of the problem, corporate experience with analogous federal programs, key personnel qualifications, and a past performance assessment based on no fewer than three relevant references submitted with the proposal.

The General Services Administration, Public Buildings Service, announces this solicitation for facility operations and maintenance services for a portfolio of seventeen federally owned buildings located in the National Capital Region. The combined gross square footage of the portfolio exceeds 4.2 million square feet, encompassing office buildings, courthouses, and a federal laboratory campus. To ensure adequate coverage and rapid response to maintenance needs across this geographically dispersed portfolio, the contractor shall provide a minimum staffing complement of thirty (30) full-time personnel. Required positions include a Contract Manager, an Operations Manager, six (6) licensed journeyman electricians, four (4) HVAC mechanics holding EPA 608 Universal certification, three (3) plumbers, two (2) elevator mechanics, two (2) fire suppression system technicians, a locksmith, a roofing specialist, and sufficient general maintenance workers to support all preventive and corrective maintenance tasks. The contractor shall also provide administrative staff to support work order management and reporting. All personnel shall be physically located within the National Capital Region and available to respond to emergency calls within two (2) hours of notification. Staffing plans must demonstrate no gaps in coverage for any building within the portfolio.

Example 2:
The General Services Administration, Public Buildings Service, hereby solicits proposals for facilities management support services at the Byron G. Rogers Federal Building, 1961 Stout Street, Denver, Colorado 80294. The contractor shall provide all services on-site at the Byron G. Rogers Federal Building. This includes, but is not limited to, janitorial services, building systems monitoring, visitor management, and physical security support. All contractor employees must maintain a continuous physical presence during assigned shifts within the building. The building operates on a 24-hour, 7-day-per-week schedule, and contractor coverage must meet this requirement accordingly. Personnel files and time and attendance records will be reviewed by the COR on a biweekly basis at the building management office. Contractors are expressly forbidden from performing any contract tasks from locations other than the specified federal building. Equipment used in performance of this contract shall remain on-site at all times unless written approval for removal is obtained from the Contracting Officer. Proposals must include a site-specific staffing plan demonstrating the offeror's capacity to maintain full on-site coverage throughout the entire base period and all option years.

Example 3:
The General Services Administration, Federal Acquisition Service, Region 6, issues this combined synopsis and solicitation for janitorial and custodial services at a single federal office building in Kansas City, Missouri, containing approximately 8,400 rentable square feet. The contract will be structured as a firm-fixed-price arrangement for a base year with no options. The maximum contract value, inclusive of all labor, supplies, equipment, and supervision for the twelve-month base period, is $38,500. This figure reflects the agency's available appropriations for facility maintenance services at this location. Prospective vendors must be capable of providing five-day-per-week service within this funding constraint. The GSA contracting officer has documented that market research supports the feasibility of fulfilling the described services within the $38,500 ceiling based on comparable awards at similar-sized facilities. Quoters whose proposed annual cost exceeds thirty-eight thousand five hundred dollars ($38,500) will be eliminated from consideration. Award will be made on a lowest-price, technically acceptable basis.

The General Services Administration, Federal Acquisition Service, is issuing this Request for Proposal for comprehensive facilities management and operations support services at a multi-building federal campus located in the National Capital Region. The scope of work includes preventive and corrective maintenance, custodial services, grounds keeping, energy management, access control operations, and emergency response coordination across approximately 1.2 million square feet of occupied space. The Government has determined, based on operational demands, the geographic distribution of facilities, and the requirement for continuous seven-day-a-week coverage, that the contractor shall maintain a minimum of fourteen (14) full-time equivalent personnel assigned exclusively to this contract throughout the base period and each exercised option year. The required staffing complement shall consist of a Facilities Manager serving as the primary point of contact, an Assistant Facilities Manager, a licensed Master Electrician, a licensed Master Plumber, two (2) HVAC Technicians holding EPA Section 608 Universal Certification, a Life Safety Systems Specialist, four (4) general maintenance technicians, a Custodial Supervisor overseeing no fewer than two (2) full-time custodial staff, and an Energy Management Coordinator. All proposed personnel must undergo and successfully complete a federal background investigation prior to unescorted access to any Government facility. Offerors shall submit a comprehensive Staffing and Management Plan as Attachment C of the technical volume, clearly delineating each labor category, the named individual proposed for that role, their relevant qualifications and certifications, and a contingency staffing strategy to address unplanned vacancies without degradation of service delivery. The Government will evaluate the Staffing and Management Plan under Technical Evaluation Factor 2 and will assess whether the offeror has demonstrated the organizational capacity and bench strength necessary to sustain the full fourteen-person complement without interruption. Proposals that fail to identify and commit a minimum of fourteen qualified individuals to this requirement will be assessed a significant weakness and may be determined technically unacceptable at the discretion of the Source Selection Authority.

Customs and Border Protection
Office of Acquisition
Solicitation Number: 70B04C24R00000021
Subject: Uniform Apparel Procurement

PROCUREMENT NOTICE — HUBZONE SET-ASIDE:

This requirement is designated as a HUBZone small business set-aside. Pursuant to 15 U.S.C. § 657a and implementing regulations at 13 C.F.R. Part 126, this solicitation is restricted to offerors that are certified HUBZone small business concerns. The Contracting Officer has conducted market research and concluded that the HUBZone set-aside is appropriate given the availability of qualified HUBZone firms in the uniform manufacturing industry. FAR clause 52.219-3, Notice of HUBZone Set-Aside or Sole Source Award, is incorporated into this solicitation. The applicable NAICS code is 315990 with a small business size standard of 500 employees. Firms must hold a valid SBA HUBZone certification and ensure their SAM.gov registration reflects HUBZone eligibility before submitting an offer. Award will be made on a best-value basis. The estimated total value of this requirement is $3.1 million. Non-HUBZone offerors are not eligible to compete and will receive no further consideration upon identification as non-certified during the evaluation process.

SOLICITATION NUMBER: DOI-BIA-2024-0089

SET-ASIDE INFORMATION: This acquisition is a 100% HUBZone Small Business Set-Aside. Only offers from Historically Underutilized Business Zone (HUBZone) small business concerns are eligible for award. Offerors must be certified by the Small Business Administration as a qualified HUBZone small business concern at the time of offer submission and at the time of award. This requirement is being set aside for HUBZone small businesses pursuant to 13 C.F.R. Part 126 and FAR 19.1305. Example 3:
DEPARTMENT OF TRANSPORTATION
Federal Highway Administration
Solicitation Number: DTFH6124R00034
Program: Transportation Planning and Data Analysis

AGENCY SET-ASIDE DETERMINATION:

After conducting market research in accordance with FAR Part 10, the Contracting Officer has determined that this requirement shall be set aside exclusively for HUBZone small business concerns. This HUBZone set-aside is established under the authority of FAR 19.1305 and the Small Business Act, 15 U.S.C. § 657a. To be eligible to receive award under this solicitation, offerors must (1) be certified as a HUBZone small business concern by the SBA, (2) be registered in SAM with an active HUBZone designation, and (3) continue to qualify as a HUBZone small business through the duration of contract performance. The NAICS code for this acquisition is 541490, with a size standard of $19.0 million. Offerors must include their SBA-issued HUBZone certification number in their proposal cover letter. The Government will not consider offers from firms that do not hold an active HUBZone certification. Estimated contract value is $2.4 million over a base year plus four option years.

SECTION B – SUPPLIES OR SERVICES AND PRICES

This acquisition is conducted pursuant to Federal Acquisition Regulation (FAR) Part 19 and the Small Business Administration's socioeconomic contracting programs. The Contracting Officer has reviewed applicable set-aside thresholds and program eligibility requirements prior to issuance of this solicitation. Following market research conducted in accordance with FAR 10.002, the Contracting Officer determined that sufficient competition exists among 8(a) Business Development Program participants to warrant a sole-source award under FAR 19.808-1. Accordingly, this requirement is set aside exclusively for 8(a) certified small business concerns.

Contractors should note that although the agency previously evaluated the potential applicability of the Historically Underutilized Business Zone program for this procurement, the geographic distribution of required performance locations and the nature of the technical work to be performed rendered that program vehicle unsuitable for this particular acquisition. The HUBZone set-aside designation was considered and affirmatively declined by the Contracting Officer following consultation with the Small Business Administration Procurement Center Representative. No HUBZone preference or set-aside applies to this solicitation.

Offerors responding to this solicitation must demonstrate current, active 8(a) certification at the time of proposal submission and at the time of award. Proof of certification through the System for Award Management (SAM.gov) is mandatory. Joint ventures must comply with SBA regulations at 13 C.F.R. Part 124 regarding 8(a) joint venture agreements. Subcontracting limitations under FAR 52.219-14 apply and will be enforced throughout contract performance.

The North American Industry Classification System (NAICS) code for this acquisition is 541512, with a small business size standard of $34 million in average annual receipts. Offerors must represent their size status as of the date of this solicitation. Misrepresentation of small business status may result in criminal penalties under 15 U.S.C. § 645. Questions regarding eligibility should be directed to the Contracting Officer no later than the date specified in Section L.

DEPARTMENT OF ENERGY
NATIONAL NUCLEAR SECURITY ADMINISTRATION
SOLICITATION: DE-SOL-0015872

THIS ACQUISITION IS A TOTAL SET-ASIDE FOR SERVICE-DISABLED VETERAN-OWNED SMALL BUSINESS (SDVOSB) FIRMS. Pursuant to FAR 52.219-27 and FAR Subpart 19.14, the National Nuclear Security Administration (NNSA) is soliciting offers from qualified SDVOSB concerns for cybersecurity assessment and penetration testing services supporting NNSA facilities. The period of performance consists of a base year and four option years. NAICS code 541519 (Other Computer Related Services) applies, with a small business size standard of $34 million. To qualify under this set-aside, firms must represent that they are a Service-Disabled Veteran-Owned Small Business as defined in FAR 2.101, meaning the concern is at least 51 percent owned and controlled by one or more service-disabled veterans. U.S. CUSTOMS AND BORDER PROTECTION

SPECIAL CONTRACT REQUIREMENTS – SUBCONTRACTING AND SOCIOECONOMIC GOALS

This contract is subject to the requirements of FAR Subpart 19.7, which mandates submission of a subcontracting plan for contracts expected to exceed $750,000 when awarded to other than a small business concern. The prime contractor's approved subcontracting plan shall include separate percentage goals for subcontracting with small business, small disadvantaged business (SDB), women-owned small business, HUBZone small business, veteran-owned small business, and service-disabled veteran-owned small business concerns.

For informational purposes, firms participating in the Small Business Administration's 8(a) Business Development Program are classified as small disadvantaged businesses and may be counted toward the SDB subcontracting goal. The inclusion of 8(a) program participants as subcontractors is encouraged but not mandated. The prime contractor retains full discretion in selecting qualified subcontractors, provided that the overall subcontracting plan goals are met and all subcontractors satisfy applicable technical and financial responsibility standards. Failure to comply in good faith with an approved subcontracting plan constitutes a material breach and may result in liquidated damages as specified in FAR 52.219-16. The Department of Veterans Affairs seeks a qualified contractor to assume responsibility for the continuing work on the Veterans Benefits Management System (VBMS). The selected vendor will inherit the existing system architecture, currently maintained by the incumbent contractor whose period of performance concludes on September 30, 2024. The incoming contractor must demonstrate proven experience onboarding into a legacy codebase without service disruption. Transition activities shall include a mandatory 90-day knowledge transfer period during which the outgoing and incoming contractors will operate concurrently. The Government requires that the new contractor review all existing technical documentation, data dictionaries, and system design records prior to assuming full operational responsibility. Offerors must describe in their technical approach how they will manage risk during transition from incumbent operations, including how they will handle undocumented components of the existing system. Continuity of service to Veterans during the transition period is a non-negotiable requirement, and the Government will evaluate all proposals on the basis of transition risk mitigation strategies.

Example 2:
The Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) is seeking a qualified contractor to configure and deploy a commercial off-the-shelf customer relationship management solution to support the Veterans Benefits Administration's case intake and tracking functions. The Government has determined that Salesforce Government Cloud meets the functional requirements identified in the market research phase and represents the most cost-effective approach. The contractor shall be responsible for configuring Salesforce workflows, custom objects, permission sets, and approval processes in alignment with VA business rules. No custom software development is anticipated under this effort. The contractor shall also be responsible for data migration from legacy Access databases, user acceptance testing coordination, and end-user training for approximately 400 VBA personnel across regional offices. The period of performance is a twelve-month base period with two twelve-month option periods. All work shall comply with VA Handbook 6500, FISMA requirements, and FedRAMP authorization standards applicable to the selected commercial solution.

The scope of work encompasses system architecture redesign, application development, data migration, testing and quality assurance, training, and help desk support. Based on the complexity and mission-critical nature of this effort, the VA requires the contractor to provide a dedicated project team of no less than twenty (20) full-time personnel. The staffing complement shall include, at a minimum, a Program Manager, an Agile Release Train Engineer, five (5) software developers with demonstrated Java and cloud platform experience, three (3) database administrators, two (2) DevSecOps engineers, a cybersecurity SME, two (2) business analysts, a training lead, and requisite quality assurance personnel. All staff must be U.S. citizens and obtain a VA Suitability determination prior to commencing work. The offeror's staffing plan must demonstrate that all twenty positions will be filled within thirty (30) days of contract award, with acceptable résumés for each proposed individual submitted as part of the technical proposal. The Government reserves the right to reject proposed personnel who do not meet stated qualifications.

The Department of Energy, National Nuclear Security Administration, requires that the contractor employ a waterfall methodology for the development of the Nuclear Material Accountability and Control Information System. This requirement reflects NNSA policy mandating structured, auditable development processes for systems handling Safeguards and Security information. The contractor shall execute all software development under clearly defined sequential phases: Requirements Definition, Functional Design, Detailed Design, Software Construction, Software Testing, and System Deployment. Phase gate reviews shall be scheduled and completed at the conclusion of each phase, with a formal Government Decision Authority review required before transition to the next phase. All requirements identified during the Requirements Definition phase shall be placed under configuration control as the fixed requirements baseline. Changes to this baseline require submission of a formal Request for Change, independent cost and schedule impact assessment, and written approval from the NNSA Contracting Officer's Representative and Configuration Control Board.

Example 2:
The Department of Veterans Affairs requires the contractor to employ a waterfall methodology for the development of the Veterans Benefits Management System Enhancement. All work shall proceed through sequential phases including requirements analysis, system design, implementation, testing, deployment, and maintenance. Each phase must be fully completed and formally accepted by the Contracting Officer's Representative prior to initiation of the subsequent phase. The contractor shall maintain a fixed requirements baseline established during the requirements phase, which shall not be altered without formal change control board approval and contract modification. Deliverables associated with each phase gate must be submitted, reviewed, and approved before any downstream development activities commence. The contractor shall provide a detailed Waterfall Project Plan within 30 days of contract award, identifying all phase entry and exit criteria, milestone dates, and associated deliverables. Monthly status reports shall track progress against the approved baseline schedule and document any variances exceeding five percent of planned effort.

Example 3:
The General Services Administration, Public Buildings Service, hereby solicits offers for development of the Facilities Asset Lifecycle Tracking System. In accordance with GSA Order CIO 2100.1, the contractor shall utilize standard software lifecycle development practices as the governing development framework for this engagement. The project shall be organized into five sequential phases: Requirements Gathering, Architecture and Design, Development, Testing and Quality Assurance, and Deployment and Transition. Phase-to-phase progression is contingent upon successful completion of formal Government review and written authorization to proceed. The contractor is expressly prohibited from initiating coding activities prior to approval of the Functional Requirements Document and System Architecture Document. A fixed requirements baseline shall be established no later than 60 calendar days after contract award. Subsequent changes to baselined requirements must be processed through the formal Engineering Change Proposal process and approved in writing by the Contracting Officer.

The General Services Administration, Public Buildings Service, hereby solicits offers for development of the Facilities Asset Lifecycle Tracking System. In accordance with GSA Order CIO 2100.1, the contractor shall utilize standard software lifecycle development practices as the governing development framework for this engagement. The project shall be organized into five sequential phases: Requirements Gathering, Architecture and Design, Development, Testing and Quality Assurance, and Deployment and Transition. Phase-to-phase progression is contingent upon successful completion of formal Government review and written authorization to proceed. The contractor is expressly prohibited from initiating coding activities prior to approval of the Functional Requirements Document and System Architecture Document. A fixed requirements baseline shall be established no later than 60 calendar days after contract award. Subsequent changes to baselined requirements must be processed through the formal Engineering Change Proposal process and approved in writing by the Contracting Officer.

Example 2:
The Department of Transportation, Federal Aviation Administration, solicits technical and price proposals for development of the Aeronautical Information Management Replacement System. Given the safety-critical nature of the system, the FAA mandates compliance with DO-178C and requires the use of traditional SDLC sequential phases throughout the program lifecycle. The contractor shall complete and obtain Government approval of all systems engineering artifacts associated with each development phase before proceeding to subsequent phases. The development sequence shall include: System Requirements Review, Software Requirements Review, Preliminary Design Review, Critical Design Review, Software Build and Integration, Software Qualification Testing, System Acceptance Testing, and Operational Readiness Review. A fixed requirements baseline shall be established following Government approval of the System and Software Requirements Specifications. The contractor shall employ rigorous phase gate controls, and no phase transition shall occur without documented Government authorization. Traceability matrices shall be maintained from requirements through test.

Example 3:
The Department of Energy, National Nuclear Security Administration, requires that the contractor employ a waterfall methodology for the development of the Nuclear Material Accountability and Control Information System. This requirement reflects NNSA policy mandating structured, auditable development processes for systems handling Safeguards and Security information. The contractor shall execute all software development under clearly defined sequential phases: Requirements Definition, Functional Design, Detailed Design, Software Construction, Software Testing, and System Deployment. Phase gate reviews shall be scheduled and completed at the conclusion of each phase, with a formal Government Decision Authority review required before transition to the next phase. All requirements identified during the Requirements Definition phase shall be placed under configuration control as the fixed requirements baseline. Changes to this baseline require submission of a formal Request for Change, independent cost and schedule impact assessment, and written approval from the NNSA Contracting Officer's Representative and Configuration Control Board.

The Department of Veterans Affairs intends to award a contract for janitorial and custodial services at the Phoenix VA Medical Center. The Government will evaluate offers using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process. Technical acceptability will be determined on a pass/fail basis. Offerors must demonstrate compliance with all technical requirements outlined in the Performance Work Statement. Price will be evaluated for reasonableness and balance. The Government will award a contract to the responsible offeror whose offer conforms to the solicitation requirements and represents the lowest price technically acceptable. No tradeoffs will be made between technical merit and price. Offerors are advised that the Government does not intend to pay a premium for technical proposals that exceed the minimum requirements. Only proposals determined to be technically acceptable will be considered for award. Offerors should not propose capabilities beyond those required, as such additions will not enhance the probability of award under this LPTA evaluation methodology.

Example 3:
The Naval Facilities Engineering Systems Command, Southwest, hereby solicits offers for refuse collection, recycling, and solid waste management services at Naval Base San Diego. This procurement is conducted using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection methodology. Technical evaluation criteria are defined as Acceptable or Unacceptable and include: compliance with all federal, state, and local environmental regulations; demonstration of required equipment and staffing levels; and proof of required licenses and permits. Price evaluation will consider the total evaluated price across the base period and all option years. Award shall be made to the responsible offeror whose proposal is technically acceptable and offers the lowest price. The Government is not seeking best value tradeoffs and will not award to a higher-priced technically acceptable offeror under any circumstances. Offerors must thoroughly address all technical criteria to avoid an Unacceptable rating, which would preclude award regardless of pricing submitted.

All software development activities shall be conducted using Scrum framework, with two-week sprint cycles. The contractor shall conduct daily stand-up meetings, sprint planning sessions, sprint reviews, and sprint retrospectives in accordance with Scrum ceremonies. A certified Scrum Master shall be designated and responsible for facilitating all Agile ceremonies and removing impediments to team progress. The Government Product Owner shall participate in sprint planning and backlog grooming sessions to prioritize user stories and acceptance criteria. The contractor shall maintain a product backlog in a Government-approved project management tool and provide sprint velocity metrics as part of monthly progress reporting. All deliverables shall be produced incrementally, with a potentially shippable product increment demonstrated at the conclusion of each sprint. The contractor shall adhere to the Agile Manifesto principles and demonstrate continuous improvement through retrospective action items documented and tracked across sprints.

Example 3:

The contractor shall employ a certified Scrum Master for each squad who is accountable for coaching team members on Agile principles and ensuring adherence to Scrum practices. A product roadmap updated at minimum quarterly shall be maintained and shared with Government stakeholders to provide visibility into planned increments of capability. Sprint velocity shall be tracked and used to inform release planning and capacity projections.

To be considered economically disadvantaged, the woman or women who own and control the firm must meet the personal financial criteria established at 13 CFR 127.200, including limitations on personal net worth, adjusted gross income, and total assets. Offerors should include their SBA-issued EDWOSB certification number or third-party certifier documentation in Volume I of their offer. Awards made under this solicitation are subject to the limitations on subcontracting requirements applicable to EDWOSB set-asides as specified in FAR 52.219-30.

SECTION C – STATEMENT OF WORK

1.0 Purpose and Scope

The General Services Administration (GSA), Federal Acquisition Service, requires comprehensive logistics management and supply chain consulting services to support the consolidation of warehouse operations across six regional distribution centers. The scope of this effort encompasses process reengineering, technology integration, staff augmentation, and change management support over a 36-month period of performance. The Government has conducted an internal analysis indicating that standalone engagements of this type, when scoped for a single facility, have historically been procured for amounts below $100,000; however, the present requirement explicitly encompasses all six facilities simultaneously under a single integrated contract vehicle, resulting in a substantially different scale of effort. The multi-site nature of this work, combined with required travel, specialized tooling, and systems integration deliverables, places this acquisition well within the range of large service contracts as defined under agency acquisition policy. The total obligated funding for the base year is $1,375,000, with an overall program budget of $4,125,000 across the base and two option periods. Contractors must demonstrate experience managing programs of comparable scale and must not conflate prior experience on smaller, single-site engagements with the complexity demanded by this solicitation.

The contractor shall implement Agile development methodologies throughout the period of performance. All software development activities shall be conducted using Scrum framework, with two-week sprint cycles. The contractor shall conduct daily stand-up meetings, sprint planning sessions, sprint reviews, and sprint retrospectives in accordance with Scrum ceremonies. A certified Scrum Master shall be designated and responsible for facilitating all Agile ceremonies and removing impediments to team progress. The Government Product Owner shall participate in sprint planning and backlog grooming sessions to prioritize user stories and acceptance criteria. The contractor shall maintain a product backlog in a Government-approved project management tool and provide sprint velocity metrics as part of monthly progress reporting. All deliverables shall be produced incrementally, with a potentially shippable product increment demonstrated at the conclusion of each sprint. The contractor shall adhere to the Agile Manifesto principles and demonstrate continuous improvement through retrospective action items documented and tracked across sprints.

Example 2:
Under this task order issued against the NASA Solutions for Enterprise-Wide Procurement contract, the contractor shall manage and execute all software development activities in accordance with Agile methodology. The contractor shall implement a Scrum-based framework and shall operate all development teams on a uniform two-week sprint cadence synchronized with the program's release train schedule. Sprint planning shall be completed within four hours for a two-week sprint, and the contractor shall ensure that sprint goals are clearly articulated and agreed upon by the Government Product Owner prior to sprint commencement. The contractor shall maintain a Definition of Done that includes code review completion, unit test coverage thresholds, security scanning, and Product Owner acceptance. Working software shall be deployed to a staging environment at the end of each sprint for Government validation. The contractor shall produce and maintain an Agile project charter documenting team norms, Agile ceremony schedules, tooling agreements, and escalation procedures. Release planning artifacts including feature roadmaps and program increment objectives shall be reviewed and approved by the NASA Contracting Officer's Representative on a quarterly basis.

Example 3:
The General Services Administration Office of Information Technology requires offerors to demonstrate proven experience delivering projects using Agile development practices. The period of performance shall be executed through iterative Agile sprints of two-week duration. The contractor shall implement all five Scrum events: Sprint Planning, Daily Scrum, Sprint Review, Sprint Retrospective, and the Sprint itself, with Government personnel invited and encouraged to participate in Sprint Reviews and Retrospectives. The contractor's Agile Coach shall work with the Government Product Owner to build and maintain a prioritized product backlog, ensuring that the highest-value features are delivered earliest. Acceptance testing shall be performed within the sprint in which development occurs, consistent with Agile testing best practices and shift-left quality principles. The contractor shall track and report on Agile health metrics including team happiness index, sprint goal achievement rate, and escaped defect rate. All tools used to manage the Agile process, including the backlog and sprint boards, shall be accessible to designated Government personnel at all times.

All deliverables shall conform to the standards established in the Historic American Buildings Survey (HABS) and Historic American Engineering Record (HAER) guidelines. Interim deliverable packages for each property must be submitted within thirty (30) days of the completion of field work at that location. A consolidated final archive of all multimedia assets, accompanied by descriptive metadata, shall be delivered to the Contracting Officer's Representative no later than thirty (30) days before contract expiration. Offerors must include in their proposals a sample portfolio of comparable historic documentation work performed within the past five (5) years. This procurement is unrestricted; both large and small businesses are encouraged to submit proposals.

The period of performance for this contract is seven (7) months, beginning on or about the anticipated award date identified in this solicitation. The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency, Office of Water, has determined through a rigorous market analysis that this industry falls within the categories designated by the Small Business Administration as underrepresented by women-owned businesses, thereby meeting the threshold requirements for a WOSB set-aside designation. WOSB certification status will be verified through official SBA systems prior to any award, and the Contracting Officer will document this verification in the contract file.

In accordance with FAR 15.102, this solicitation incorporates oral presentations as a substitute for, or supplement to, written information for the Technical Approach evaluation factor. Offerors selected for oral presentations will receive written notification from the Contracting Officer specifying the date, time, location, and format requirements. The Department of Energy's Office of Environmental Management will evaluate the oral presentation under Subfactor 1A – Technical Understanding and Subfactor 1B – Staffing and Organization. Each offeror's presentation team must include the proposed Project Director, Deputy Project Director, and at least one subject matter expert for each major technical area described in the Statement of Work. Presentations shall not exceed one hundred and fifty (150) minutes inclusive of Government questions. Offerors are encouraged to use visual aids, technical demonstrations, or mock deliverable examples to enhance their presentations. The evaluation panel will score the oral presentation independently from written submissions, and both scores will be combined to produce the final Technical Approach rating used in the Best Value determination.

Example 2:
The Government reserves the right to conduct oral presentations with offerors in the competitive range. Oral presentations will be scheduled following the initial evaluation of written proposals. Each offeror shall be prepared to present and discuss their technical approach, management strategy, and key personnel qualifications before a panel of Government evaluators. The oral presentation shall not exceed ninety (90) minutes, inclusive of a question-and-answer period not to exceed thirty (30) minutes. Offerors may use visual aids such as slides or diagrams; however, all materials presented during the oral presentation must be submitted in writing to the Contracting Officer no later than 48 hours prior to the scheduled session. Information disclosed during the oral presentation will be considered part of the offeror's proposal and will be evaluated under the Technical Approach and Key Personnel factors. The Government will not compensate offerors for costs associated with preparation or participation in oral presentations. Specific scheduling instructions will be provided to offerors selected for the competitive range via written notification from the Contracting Officer.

Example 3:
Proposals submitted in response to this solicitation will undergo a multi-phase evaluation process. Phase I consists of written technical and price proposal review. Offerors achieving a combined written evaluation score that places them within the competitive range will advance to Phase II, which requires an oral presentation before the Department of Transportation Technical Review Board. The oral presentation phase is designed to supplement written proposals by allowing offerors to demonstrate technical depth, address evaluator questions regarding proposed approaches, and introduce key personnel who will execute the contract. Oral presentations will be evaluated under Factor 1 – Technical Approach and Factor 3 – Key Personnel, and scores derived from the oral presentation will be weighted equally with written proposal scores for those factors. Each presentation is allocated ninety (90) minutes, with no more than sixty (60) minutes reserved for the offeror's presentation and the remainder designated for Government questions. The offeror's Project Manager and at least two senior technical staff members named in the proposal must be physically present.

Given the scale of contamination, the complexity of multi-media remediation (soil, groundwater, and sediment), and the requirement for concurrent work across three distinct operable units, the Government has determined that a minimum of fifteen (15) qualified personnel must be engaged on this contract at all times during active remediation phases. The required team shall include a licensed Professional Engineer serving as Project Manager, a Certified Industrial Hygienist, at minimum two (2) licensed Geologists, a Health and Safety Officer, four (4) field technicians, a quality control manager, and sufficient support staff to maintain documentation, community relations, and regulatory liaison functions. The period of performance is estimated at thirty-six (36) months with two (2) twelve-month option periods. Offerors shall demonstrate in their Management Plan that the proposed team possesses the depth of expertise and numerical sufficiency to execute overlapping work streams without degradation of quality or schedule. Failure to staff to the required minimum levels at any point during performance may constitute a material breach of contract.

Example 2:
The Environmental Protection Agency, Office of Water, requires scientific, technical, and programmatic support services to advance water quality monitoring, data analysis, regulatory development, and outreach activities under the Clean Water Act and Safe Drinking Water Act programs. The Government has estimated the total level of effort at approximately 28,000 labor hours annually, distributed across multiple task areas requiring diverse scientific and regulatory expertise. To execute this level of effort and maintain continuity across concurrent task orders, the contractor shall maintain a dedicated team of no fewer than sixteen (16) full-time equivalent personnel. The team composition shall include environmental scientists, hydrologists, chemists, toxicologists, policy analysts, GIS specialists, technical writers, and program support staff with direct experience supporting EPA regulatory programs. The offeror's staffing plan must demonstrate that the sixteen-person team collectively possesses the competencies required to address all task areas identified in the Performance Work Statement. The Government will evaluate the staffing plan for both the depth of individual expertise and the overall team's capacity to absorb new task orders within existing resources. The contractor shall not reduce staffing below sixteen FTEs without prior written approval from the Contracting Officer's Representative and a corresponding contract modification.

This acquisition will be evaluated using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process in accordance with FAR 15.101-2. Technical acceptability will be evaluated under three pass/fail criteria: Key Personnel Qualifications, Laboratory Accreditation Compliance, and Quality Assurance Plan Adequacy. Proposals must be rated Acceptable on all three criteria to be eligible for award. Technical proposals that exceed minimum requirements will receive no additional consideration. Following determination of technical acceptability, award will be made to the responsible offeror submitting the lowest evaluated total price. The Government has made a written determination that LPTA is the appropriate evaluation approach given the well-defined nature of the work and the availability of qualified contractors capable of performing at minimum standards. Questions regarding the LPTA evaluation criteria must be submitted by the deadline specified in Section L.

The Government requires development of a fully custom, purpose-built data ingestion and normalization platform to support real-time telemetry processing across distributed sensor networks. The contractor shall design and implement all software components from the ground up, including custom ETL pipelines, proprietary data schemas, and bespoke API endpoints tailored to the agency's unique operational requirements. The Government conducted a thorough market survey and determined that no existing commercial solution adequately addresses the specialized security classification handling, data throughput thresholds, or inter-agency interoperability protocols mandated by this program. Vendors evaluated several off-the-shelf database engines and middleware frameworks during the preliminary assessment phase; however, those evaluations concluded that reliance on pre-packaged platforms would introduce unacceptable latency and fail to meet FedRAMP High authorization requirements within the prescribed timeline. The contractor shall not incorporate any proprietary third-party platforms as primary architectural components without prior written Government approval. The contractor shall submit all phase gate deliverables no fewer than 15 business days prior to the scheduled phase gate review to allow adequate Government review time. The Government will award a contract to the responsible offeror whose offer conforms to the solicitation requirements and represents the lowest price technically acceptable. This acquisition will be evaluated using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process in accordance with FAR 15.101-2. Technical acceptability will be evaluated under three pass/fail criteria: Key Personnel Qualifications, Laboratory Accreditation Compliance, and Quality Assurance Plan Adequacy. Proposals must be rated Acceptable on all three criteria to be eligible for award. Technical proposals that exceed minimum requirements will receive no additional consideration. Following determination of technical acceptability, award will be made to the responsible offeror submitting the lowest evaluated total price. The Government has made a written determination that LPTA is the appropriate evaluation approach given the well-defined nature of the work and the availability of qualified contractors capable of performing at minimum standards. Technical Capability will be rated either Acceptable or Unacceptable based on the offeror's demonstrated ability to meet the requirements of the Statement of Work. Price will not be evaluated for technical merit. Award will be made to the offeror submitting the lowest price among all technically acceptable proposals. Proposals rated Unacceptable under the Technical Capability factor will be eliminated from consideration regardless of price. The Government reserves the right to conduct discussions if necessary but intends to make award without discussions. All offerors must be registered in SAM.gov at time of offer submission.

The Department of Education, Office of the Chief Information Officer, is requesting proposals for desktop and laptop computer hardware refresh services under this competitive procurement. In accordance with FAR 15.101-2, this acquisition employs the lowest price technically acceptable (LPTA) evaluation method. Technical factors consist of Hardware Specifications Compliance and Delivery Capability, both evaluated on an Acceptable/Unacceptable basis. Offerors must certify that all proposed equipment meets or exceeds the minimum technical specifications listed in Attachment A. Proposals that do not meet all mandatory specifications will be rated Unacceptable and removed from consideration. Among technically acceptable proposals, the Government will select for award the offer representing the lowest total evaluated price. Offerors should not include options or features beyond those required, as additional capabilities will provide no competitive advantage under this LPTA process. All hardware must be TAA compliant and originate from approved sources.

Example 2:
The Environmental Protection Agency, Office of Acquisition Solutions, is issuing this Request for Proposals for technical support services related to water quality monitoring and sampling at Superfund sites in EPA Region 5. This acquisition will be evaluated using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process in accordance with FAR 15.101-2. Technical acceptability will be evaluated under three pass/fail criteria: Key Personnel Qualifications, Laboratory Accreditation Compliance, and Quality Assurance Plan Adequacy. Proposals must be rated Acceptable on all three criteria to be eligible for award. Technical proposals that exceed minimum requirements will receive no additional consideration. Following determination of technical acceptability, award will be made to the responsible offeror submitting the lowest evaluated total price. The Government has made a written determination that LPTA is the appropriate evaluation approach given the well-defined nature of the work and the availability of qualified contractors capable of performing at minimum standards. Questions regarding the LPTA evaluation criteria must be submitted by the deadline specified in Section L.

Example 3:
This solicitation is issued by the General Services Administration, Public Buildings Service, for grounds maintenance and landscaping services at federal facilities in the National Capital Region. Source selection will be conducted using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) methodology as prescribed in FAR 15.101-2. Evaluation factors include Technical Capability and Price. Technical Capability will be rated either Acceptable or Unacceptable based on the offeror's demonstrated ability to meet the requirements of the Statement of Work. Price will not be evaluated for technical merit. Award will be made to the offeror submitting the lowest price among all technically acceptable proposals. Proposals rated Unacceptable under the Technical Capability factor will be eliminated from consideration regardless of price. The Government reserves the right to conduct discussions if necessary but intends to make award without discussions. All offerors must be registered in SAM.gov at time of offer submission.

RFP chunk to analyze:
Background and Acquisition Strategy. The Department conducted extensive market research prior to issuing this solicitation and considered multiple source selection approaches, including streamlined procedures appropriate for commercial acquisitions and the lowest price technically acceptable process sometimes used for non-complex commoditized requirements. After careful analysis, the Contracting Officer determined that the LPTA process is not appropriate for this requirement because the services sought involve significant complexity, require professional judgment, and present meaningful performance risk if awarded solely on the basis of lowest cost. The work encompasses advanced cybersecurity engineering, continuous monitoring architecture design, and incident response capability development—domains where distinctions in technical quality produce materially different outcomes for mission assurance. Accordingly, this solicitation is conducted under FAR 15.101-1 best value tradeoff procedures. Offerors are reminded that the Government anticipates receiving proposals with varying technical strengths and is prepared to make award to a higher-priced, technically superior offeror where the record supports such a determination.

Example 2:
The contractor shall provide all personnel necessary to perform the scope of work described herein. All work under this contract shall be performed onsite at the Department of Agriculture's regional administrative offices located at 8030 Excelsior Drive, Madison, Wisconsin 53717. Remote work arrangements will not be authorized under any circumstances. Contractor employees must report to the Madison, WI facility no later than 7:30 AM on each scheduled workday. The Government will provide workspace, network access terminals, and badged entry to the facility. Contractors must comply with all Federal building security protocols, including identity verification and visitor management procedures enforced at the Madison location. This solicitation does not contemplate and will not fund any new software engineering effort. No custom development of application features, modules, dashboards, or reporting tools is included within scope. The awarded Contractor shall be responsible for configuration only, including course catalog setup, learner enrollment workflows, administrator role assignments, and integration with the Department's single sign-on environment using the platform's existing API connectors. The Government acknowledges that the selected COTS platform may not fulfill one hundred percent of desired capabilities and accepts that business processes will be adapted accordingly. Offerors should not propose feature enhancements or product roadmap commitments as part of their technical approach. Evaluation criteria will focus on the extent to which the existing, unmodified platform addresses stated requirements without reliance on future development promises.

SECTION C – STATEMENT OF WORK

4.7 Training Delivery Requirements

The contractor shall develop and deliver a comprehensive professional development curriculum targeting mid-level federal supervisors nationwide. All training modules shall be designed for asynchronous online delivery through the agency's existing Learning Management System (LMS). The curriculum must be accessible to participants regardless of their duty station location, including personnel stationed at headquarters, regional field offices, and remote telework locations throughout the continental United States, Alaska, Hawaii, and U.S. territories.

Historically, prior iterations of this training program were conducted as in-person sessions held at the Bolger Center in Potomac, Maryland, and on occasion at conference facilities in Madison, Wisconsin, which served as a regional hub for Midwest-based participants. The current solicitation represents a deliberate modernization effort, transitioning entirely away from location-dependent delivery models. The agency has determined that centralized, onsite gatherings are no longer operationally practical or cost-effective given the distributed nature of the federal workforce.

The contractor shall ensure all course materials, assessments, and instructor-facilitated components are fully functional in a virtual environment. No travel, venue coordination, or physical classroom management is within scope. Offerors should not include costs associated with facilities, lodging, or participant transportation in their price proposals, as such expenses will not be reimbursed under any circumstances.

The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, Baltimore District, is soliciting proposals for environmental remediation and site restoration services at a formerly used defense site located in the Mid-Atlantic region. Given the scale of contamination, the complexity of multi-media remediation (soil, groundwater, and sediment), and the requirement for concurrent work across three distinct operable units, the Government has determined that a minimum of fifteen (15) qualified personnel must be engaged on this contract at all times during active remediation phases. The required team shall include a licensed Professional Engineer serving as Project Manager, a Certified Industrial Hygienist, at minimum two (2) licensed Geologists, a Health and Safety Officer, four (4) field technicians, a quality control manager, and sufficient support staff to maintain documentation, community relations, and regulatory liaison functions. The period of performance is estimated at thirty-six (36) months with two (2) twelve-month option periods. Offerors shall demonstrate in their Management Plan that the proposed team possesses the depth of expertise and numerical sufficiency to execute overlapping work streams without degradation of quality or schedule. Failure to staff to the required minimum levels at any point during performance may constitute a material breach of contract.

Example 2:
The National Institutes of Health (NIH), Office of Research Services, issues this Request for Proposal for the operation and enhancement of the NIH Enterprise Directory (NED) and associated Identity Management infrastructure. This procurement represents a direct transition from incumbent contractor Booz Allen Hamilton and requires the selected offeror to assume stewardship of the existing system within 90 calendar days of contract award. The existing system integrates with more than 40 downstream NIH applications and processes identity lifecycle events for over 100,000 active users across the NIH enterprise. The incoming contractor must perform a full inventory of integration points within the first 30 days of the transition period. NIH will provide Government-Furnished Equipment and access to existing system repositories hosted in NIH's internal GitLab instance. Proposals must describe in detail the offeror's methodology for continuing work while simultaneously identifying technical debt and recommending a prioritized remediation schedule for submission to the Contracting Officer's Representative no later than Day 60 of the transition.

Example 3:
The National Aeronautics and Space Administration, Goddard Space Flight Center, is seeking a contractor to provide engineering, integration, and test support services for an upcoming Earth science mission. The nature of the work, which includes systems engineering, instrument calibration, software verification, environmental testing, launch support, and post-launch on-orbit operations, necessitates a large, multidisciplinary team. The contractor shall maintain a team of at minimum fourteen (14) engineers and scientists throughout the performance period, with additional personnel required during critical mission phases such as environmental test campaigns and launch operations. The core team shall include systems engineers, mechanical engineers, electrical engineers, software engineers, and integration and test specialists with demonstrated spaceflight heritage. The offeror shall provide a staffing matrix in the proposal that maps each of the fourteen required positions to an individual, identifies their role, qualifications, and availability by contract phase. The Government understands that mission phases will drive variable workload and reserves the right to request surge staffing above the fourteen-person baseline; however, the minimum staffing level shall not fall below fourteen individuals at any time during the base period of performance.

The Government will evaluate the depth and qualifications of the proposed team as a significant factor under the Technical Approach evaluation criterion. Proposals that fail to demonstrate a staffing complement meeting or exceeding the eighteen-person minimum will be assessed as technically unacceptable and will not receive further evaluation consideration.
