The General Services Administration (GSA) Federal Acquisition Service seeks a contractor to provide software development and maintenance support for the System for Award Management (SAM.gov). This effort requires the contractor to work directly within the existing system environment, which has been under continuous development since 2012. The incoming contractor will execute a transition from incumbent operations currently held by IBM Federal, taking full responsibility for all active development sprints, defect backlogs, and release management activities. The legacy codebase includes multiple subsystems developed across several prior contract periods, resulting in heterogeneous technology stacks that the contractor must be prepared to support concurrently. GSA anticipates that the first six months of the contract will be heavily weighted toward stabilization and documentation of the existing system before new feature development proceeds at full velocity. The Government's Quality Assurance Surveillance Plan includes specific metrics for transition risk during this stabilization phase.

Example 2:
The General Services Administration Office of Technology Policy is issuing this Request for Proposal for a cloud-based contract writing and acquisition management platform. This procurement is strictly limited to the acquisition of an existing commercial solution. No software development is authorized under any circumstances. Offerors shall not propose to build, code, or engineer new application modules in response to agency-specific requirements. The Government's intent is to leverage a proven, market-available product and adapt internal business processes to align with the tool's existing capabilities. Configuration of user permissions, workflow routing, document templates, and integration with existing financial systems via standard APIs is permissible and expected. Offerors must demonstrate that the proposed platform has been deployed in at least three federal agency environments. Proposals that include statements of work referencing sprint cycles, agile development backlogs, or custom feature engineering will be evaluated unfavorably and may be deemed non-compliant with this solicitation's scope restrictions.

Example 3:
The General Services Administration, Public Buildings Service, hereby solicits offers for development of the Facilities Asset Lifecycle Tracking System. In accordance with GSA Order CIO 2100.1, the contractor shall utilize standard software lifecycle development practices as the governing development framework for this engagement. The project shall be organized into five sequential phases: Requirements Gathering, Architecture and Design, Development, Testing and Quality Assurance, and Deployment and Transition. Phase-to-phase progression is contingent upon successful completion of formal Government review and written authorization to proceed. The contractor is expressly prohibited from initiating coding activities prior to approval of the Functional Requirements Document and System Architecture Document. A fixed requirements baseline shall be established no later than 60 calendar days after contract award. Subsequent changes to baselined requirements must be processed through the formal Engineering Change Proposal process and approved in writing by the Contracting Officer.

The General Services Administration, Technology Transformation Services, hereby solicits proposals for the design and development of a centralized grants lifecycle management platform intended to serve multiple Federal agencies participating in the Cross-Agency Grants Modernization Program. The platform shall support end-to-end grants management functions including applicant registration, eligibility determination, award processing, performance reporting, and audit trail generation. The contractor shall conduct user research with program officers and grant recipients across participating agencies to inform iterative design decisions, producing wireframes, interaction models, and validated prototypes prior to full development sprints. The solution must be built on FedRAMP-authorized cloud infrastructure and conform to the U.S. Web Design System accessibility and visual standards, meeting WCAG 2.1 AA compliance at minimum. Custom development shall encompass a modular microservices architecture enabling individual agencies to configure workflows without requiring platform-wide code changes. The contractor shall provide a dedicated team encompassing engineering, design, product management, and quality assurance disciplines, operating under collaborative planning processes with GSA product owners. Contract structure will be a Time and Materials task order with a not-to-exceed ceiling, issued under an existing multiple-award contract vehicle. The base period of performance is eighteen months with one twelve-month option period. The Government anticipates conducting a technical evaluation based on written technical approach, relevant past performance, and proposed staffing qualifications. All data processed by the platform shall remain within FedRAMP-authorized boundaries and shall be encrypted at rest and in transit using FIPS 140-2 validated cryptographic modules.

OFFICE OF ADMINISTRATION
DIVISION OF PROCUREMENT AND CONTRACTS

SOLICITATION NO. HUD-24-R-00088
REQUESTED SERVICES: LANGUAGE INTERPRETATION AND TRANSLATION SERVICES

Small Business Size Standard: $19.0 million

Offerors shall develop and submit a working prototype of their proposed traffic management interface within thirty (30) calendar days of solicitation release. Government evaluators will conduct hands-on testing of each submitted prototype during a structured demonstration session scheduled at DOT headquarters in Washington, D.C. Scoring criteria for the design challenge component account for forty percent (40%) of the overall technical evaluation score. Prototypes that fail to meet minimum functionality thresholds as defined in Attachment C will be deemed technically unacceptable and removed from further consideration. All prototype submissions must be delivered via secure government portal no later than 5:00 PM Eastern Time on the date specified in the solicitation timeline.

The Department of Homeland Security, Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency, requires offerors to participate in a structured design challenge as a condition of this competitive acquisition. The design challenge will task each offeror's technical team with developing a threat detection use case scenario using a simulated network environment provided by the Government. Offerors will have four (4) hours to architect a detection logic framework, implement rules within the provided SIEM platform, and produce a written summary of their methodology. The design challenge is intended to evaluate offeror capability in applied threat intelligence, rule engineering, and documentation quality under realistic operational conditions. Government evaluators will assess outputs using a standardized rubric detailed in Section M, Evaluation Criteria. The design challenge will be conducted at a CISA facility; specific location and scheduling details will be provided to competitive range offerors via the Contracting Officer. Teams may consist of no more than four (4) individuals. All work product generated during the exercise becomes the property of the United States Government upon submission.

The General Services Administration hereby issues this solicitation which includes a required design exercise component for all competitive range offerors. Specifically, each offeror must submit a prototype submission demonstrating their proposed citizen-facing web portal solution. The prototype submission shall be a fully navigable, hosted demonstration environment accessible via government-provided credentials. The prototype must include functional user registration, document upload, and status tracking modules. GSA evaluators will engage directly with the prototype during a structured two-hour evaluation session and will simulate end-user workflows as described in Attachment D, User Scenario Matrix. Prototype submissions are due no later than fourteen (14) days prior to the scheduled oral presentations. Offerors are advised that the prototype submission will be evaluated independently from the written technical volume and carries a weight of thirty-five percent (35%) of the total technical score. Any prototype that is unavailable, incomplete, or non-functional during the evaluation session will receive a rating of Unacceptable for that factor.

The Social Security Administration, Office of Facilities and Supply Management, is soliciting proposals for courier and document delivery services between SSA regional offices located throughout the Mid-Atlantic region. This solicitation is issued under the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection method as outlined in FAR 15.101-2. Proposals will be evaluated on two factors: Technical Acceptability and Price. Technical Acceptability will be rated Acceptable or Unacceptable based on whether the offeror demonstrates sufficient vehicle fleet capacity, staffing plans, and route coverage to satisfy the requirements in the Performance Work Statement. No additional credit will be given for exceeding minimum requirements. Price will be evaluated based on the total price across the base year and four option years. Award will be made to the technically acceptable offeror with the lowest evaluated price. Offerors are reminded that submission of a technically acceptable proposal at the lowest price constitutes the sole basis for award determination under this LPTA competition.

Example 2:
The Environmental Protection Agency, Office of Acquisition Solutions, is issuing this Request for Proposals for technical support services related to water quality monitoring and sampling at Superfund sites in EPA Region 5. This acquisition will be evaluated using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process in accordance with FAR 15.101-2. Technical acceptability will be evaluated under three pass/fail criteria: Key Personnel Qualifications, Laboratory Accreditation Compliance, and Quality Assurance Plan Adequacy. Proposals must be rated Acceptable on all three criteria to be eligible for award. Technical proposals that exceed minimum requirements will receive no additional consideration. Following determination of technical acceptability, award will be made to the responsible offeror submitting the lowest evaluated total price. The Government has made a written determination that LPTA is the appropriate evaluation approach given the well-defined nature of the work and the availability of qualified contractors capable of performing at minimum standards. Questions regarding the LPTA evaluation criteria must be submitted by the deadline specified in Section L.

Example 3:
This solicitation is issued by the General Services Administration, Public Buildings Service, for grounds maintenance and landscaping services at federal facilities in the National Capital Region. Source selection will be conducted using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) methodology as prescribed in FAR 15.101-2. Evaluation factors include Technical Capability and Price. Technical Capability will be rated either Acceptable or Unacceptable based on the offeror's demonstrated ability to meet the requirements of the Statement of Work. Price will not be evaluated for technical merit. Award will be made to the offeror submitting the lowest price among all technically acceptable proposals. Proposals rated Unacceptable under the Technical Capability factor will be eliminated from consideration regardless of price. The Government reserves the right to conduct discussions if necessary but intends to make award without discussions. All offerors must be registered in SAM.gov at time of offer submission.

Given the clearly defined and standardized nature of the required services, the contracting officer has determined that source selection will proceed using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable methodology as authorized under FAR 15.101-2. Proposals will be evaluated under two factors: Technical Acceptability and Price. The General Services Administration Office of Information Technology requires offerors to demonstrate proven experience delivering projects using Agile development practices. The period of performance shall be executed through iterative Agile sprints of two-week duration. The contractor shall implement all five Scrum events: Sprint Planning, Daily Scrum, Sprint Review, Sprint Retrospective, and the Sprint itself, with Government personnel invited and encouraged to participate in Sprint Reviews and Retrospectives. The contractor's Agile Coach shall work with the Government Product Owner to build and maintain a prioritized product backlog, ensuring that the highest-value features are delivered earliest. Acceptance testing shall be performed within the sprint in which development occurs, consistent with Agile testing best practices and shift-left quality principles. The contractor shall track and report on Agile health metrics including team happiness index, sprint goal achievement rate, and escaped defect rate. All tools used to manage the Agile process, including the backlog and sprint boards, shall be accessible to designated Government personnel at all times.

Example 2:
The Department of Education, Office of the Chief Information Officer, requires the contractor to lead and execute a modernization initiative using Agile Scrum practices in alignment with the agency's Technology Modernization Playbook. The contractor shall staff an Agile delivery team that includes at minimum a Scrum Master, Product Manager, UX Researcher, Front-End and Back-End Developers, and a Quality Assurance Engineer. All work shall be organized into a continuously refined product backlog with epics, features, and user stories sized and prioritized by the Government Product Owner in collaboration with the contractor. Sprint cycles shall be two weeks in duration, and the contractor shall present a sprint demo to agency stakeholders at each sprint review, with attendance tracked and documented. The Scrum Master shall facilitate sprint retrospectives and shall maintain a retrospective action log accessible to the Government. Agile metrics shall be reported in the contractor's monthly status report, including planned versus completed story points, sprint goal achievement rate, and the number of impediments raised and resolved. The contractor shall provide Agile coaching support to Government personnel as a secondary deliverable to build internal agency capacity.

Example 3:
This solicitation for Healthcare Information Technology Modernization Support Services includes an oral presentation requirement as a formal element of the evaluation process. The Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) will invite offerors in the competitive range to participate in structured oral presentations following the initial written proposal review. Oral presentations will be assessed under Technical Factor 2 – Implementation Methodology and Technical Factor 4 – Relevant Experience. Each offeror will have seventy-five (75) minutes to present, followed by a twenty-five (25) minute question-and-answer session conducted by the HHS Evaluation Committee. The offeror's proposed Program Manager, Lead Systems Architect, and at least one clinical informatics specialist must be present in person. Remote participation is not authorized except under emergency circumstances approved in advance by the Contracting Officer. Offerors should structure their oral presentation to directly address the evaluation criteria outlined in Section M and are advised that new information introduced during the oral presentation that was not contained in the written proposal will be evaluated as a clarification and not as a revision or amendment to the offer.

Presentations shall be a maximum of seventy-five (75) minutes in duration, which includes a mandatory fifteen (15) minute question-and-answer session conducted by the Technical Evaluation Panel. The proposed Contract Program Manager must attend and lead the presentation. Supporting staff may participate but shall not exceed four (4) individuals total. The Government may record oral presentations for evaluation documentation purposes, and offerors will be notified accordingly.

Example 3:
The Government reserves the right to conduct oral presentations with offerors in the competitive range. Oral presentations will be scheduled following the initial evaluation of written proposals. The oral presentation shall not exceed ninety (90) minutes, inclusive of a question-and-answer period not to exceed thirty (30) minutes. Offerors may use visual aids such as slides or diagrams; however, all materials presented during the oral presentation must be submitted in writing to the Contracting Officer no later than 48 hours prior to the scheduled session. Information disclosed during the oral presentation will be considered part of the offeror's proposal and will be evaluated under the Technical Approach and Key Personnel factors. The Government will not compensate offerors for costs associated with preparation or participation in oral presentations. Specific scheduling instructions will be provided to offerors selected for the competitive range via written notification from the Contracting Officer.

The Government will evaluate all proposals strictly on the basis of written submissions. Requests by offerors to supplement their proposals through verbal or in-person means will not be accommodated. This procurement is conducted using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection methodology. Technical evaluation criteria are defined as Acceptable or Unacceptable and include: compliance with all federal, state, and local environmental regulations; demonstration of required equipment and staffing levels; and proof of required licenses and permits. Price evaluation will consider the total evaluated price across the base period and all option years. Award shall be made to the responsible offeror whose proposal is technically acceptable and offers the lowest price. The Government is not seeking best value tradeoffs and will not award to a higher-priced technically acceptable offeror under any circumstances. Offerors must thoroughly address all technical criteria to avoid an Unacceptable rating, which would preclude award regardless of pricing submitted.

Example 2:
The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, Omaha District, is seeking proposals for facility maintenance and repair services at Camp Ashland, Nebraska. The source selection methodology for this procurement is Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA). Offerors must submit a Technical Volume and a Price Volume. The Technical Volume will be evaluated to determine whether the offeror's proposed approach meets the minimum requirements of the Performance Work Statement. Ratings will be limited to Acceptable or Unacceptable. An offeror receiving an Unacceptable technical rating will not be eligible for award. Price will be evaluated for completeness, reasonableness, and balance. Award will be made to the offeror with the lowest evaluated price whose technical proposal has been rated Acceptable. The use of LPTA in this acquisition is consistent with the determination that the requirement is well-defined and risk of unsuccessful performance is minimal. The Government does not anticipate the need for discussions.

Example 3:
The Environmental Protection Agency, Office of Acquisition Solutions, is issuing this Request for Proposals for technical support services related to water quality monitoring and sampling at Superfund sites in EPA Region 5. This acquisition will be evaluated using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process in accordance with FAR 15.101-2. Technical acceptability will be evaluated under three pass/fail criteria: Key Personnel Qualifications, Laboratory Accreditation Compliance, and Quality Assurance Plan Adequacy. Proposals must be rated Acceptable on all three criteria to be eligible for award. Technical proposals that exceed minimum requirements will receive no additional consideration. Following determination of technical acceptability, award will be made to the responsible offeror submitting the lowest evaluated total price. The Government has made a written determination that LPTA is the appropriate evaluation approach given the well-defined nature of the work and the availability of qualified contractors capable of performing at minimum standards. Questions regarding the LPTA evaluation criteria must be submitted by the deadline specified in Section L.

Example 1:
The Department of Transportation Federal Highway Administration hereby requests proposals for an enterprise asset management platform supporting the National Bridge Inspection Program. Consistent with FHWA's technology modernization policy, this acquisition mandates a COTS solution and explicitly prohibits any custom application development. Vendors shall not include development labor categories, software engineering hours, or coding deliverables within their proposed staffing plans. The work under this contract is scoped as configuration only, covering asset taxonomy definition, inspection workflow setup, geospatial layer integration using the platform's native mapping connectors, and report template customization through the product's built-in report designer. The Contractor must demonstrate that all proposed capabilities are available in the current production release of the software and do not depend on future product updates or vendor commitments. Proposals referencing software development lifecycles, code repositories, or testing environments associated with new feature creation will be considered outside the scope of this solicitation and may result in the proposal being found technically unacceptable.

Example 2:
The Department of Justice, Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives, solicits proposals for the Federal Firearms Licensee Inspection Management System. The contractor shall develop the system in accordance with ATF's Information Systems Security and Development Policy, which requires use of a standard software lifecycle development approach for all major applications. The development effort shall be organized into the following sequential phases: Initiation and Planning, Requirements Analysis, System Design, Development and Unit Testing, Integration Testing, User Acceptance Testing, Security Assessment and Authorization, and Production Deployment. Each phase shall conclude with a formal milestone review and production of Government-accepted phase deliverables before the subsequent phase is initiated. A fixed requirements baseline shall be documented and approved in writing no later than 45 days following contract award. The contractor shall not begin any system design activities until the Requirements Traceability Matrix and System Requirements Specification have been formally approved by the Government.

Example 3:
The Department of Transportation, Federal Aviation Administration, solicits technical and price proposals for development of the Aeronautical Information Management Replacement System. Given the safety-critical nature of the system, the FAA mandates compliance with DO-178C and requires the use of traditional SDLC sequential phases throughout the program lifecycle. The contractor shall complete and obtain Government approval of all systems engineering artifacts associated with each development phase before proceeding to subsequent phases. The development sequence shall include: System Requirements Review, Software Requirements Review, Preliminary Design Review, Critical Design Review, Software Build and Integration, Software Qualification Testing, System Acceptance Testing, and Operational Readiness Review. A fixed requirements baseline shall be established following Government approval of the System and Software Requirements Specifications. The contractor shall employ rigorous phase gate controls, and no phase transition shall occur without documented Government authorization. Traceability matrices shall be maintained from requirements through test.

Integration connectors used to link the platform with DHS legacy systems must be pre-built and commercially supported. Engineering of new connectors or middleware specifically for this contract is outside the permissible scope of work.

Example 2:
The Department of Homeland Security (DHS) Science and Technology Directorate is soliciting proposals for the implementation of a cloud-based project portfolio management capability to support research program tracking across the Directorate. Following a formal analysis of alternatives, the Government has selected a commercial solution based on Salesforce Project Management modules as the technical baseline. The primary work under this contract is configuring and deploying the selected commercial off-the-shelf platform to meet DHS-specific reporting, resource allocation, and milestone tracking requirements. The contractor shall configure dashboards, automated notifications, role-based access controls, and integration connectors to existing financial management systems. Offerors shall describe their approach to Salesforce configuration without reliance on custom Apex code development except where configuration-only approaches are technically infeasible and documented as such. The Government anticipates a total level of effort of approximately 2,800 hours across the base year, with options for ongoing platform administration and enhancement.

Example 3:
The Department of Homeland Security anticipates award of a single Indefinite Delivery Indefinite Quantity (IDIQ) contract to support cybersecurity operations at multiple field offices across the continental United States. The successful offeror shall provide no fewer than twelve (12) full-time equivalent personnel, including a Program Manager, Deputy Program Manager, four (4) Senior Cybersecurity Analysts, three (3) Network Engineers, and at least two (2) Vulnerability Assessment Specialists. All personnel must maintain active Secret clearances at time of contract award. The contractor shall ensure continuous coverage during core business hours (0700–1800 local time) and shall provide on-call support during off-hours for critical incident response. The Government anticipates that the breadth of work, spanning threat monitoring, penetration testing, compliance reporting, and stakeholder coordination, will necessitate the full complement of staff described herein throughout the base period and all option years. Offerors whose proposed staffing plans reflect fewer than twelve personnel will be deemed technically unacceptable and removed from further consideration. The offeror shall submit a staffing plan as Volume III of the technical proposal, clearly identifying each labor category, the individual proposed, and their respective roles and responsibilities in support of this requirement.

Offerors shall provide a written technical proposal not to exceed 30 pages and shall include at least two past performance references for federal cybersecurity software development efforts of similar scope and complexity completed within the past five years.

The period of performance shall be five (5) months from the date of award. The Government requires that the new contractor review all existing technical documentation, data dictionaries, and system design records prior to assuming full operational responsibility. Offerors must describe in their technical approach how they will manage risk during transition from incumbent operations, including how they will handle undocumented components of the existing system. Continuity of service to Veterans during the transition period is a non-negotiable requirement, and the Government will evaluate all proposals on the basis of transition risk mitigation strategies.

The Department of Homeland Security (DHS) Science and Technology Directorate is soliciting proposals for the implementation of a cloud-based project portfolio management capability to support research program tracking across the Directorate. Following a formal analysis of alternatives, the Government has selected a commercial solution based on Salesforce Project Management modules as the technical baseline. The primary work under this contract is configuring and deploying the selected commercial off-the-shelf platform to meet DHS-specific reporting, resource allocation, and milestone tracking requirements. The contractor shall configure dashboards, automated notifications, role-based access controls, and integration connectors to existing financial management systems. Offerors shall describe their approach to Salesforce configuration without reliance on custom Apex code development except where configuration-only approaches are technically infeasible and documented as such. The Government anticipates a total level of effort of approximately 2,800 hours across the base year, with options for ongoing platform administration and enhancement.

Example 2:
The Department of Homeland Security, Office of the Chief Information Officer, hereby solicits proposals for an enterprise identity and access management solution. This requirement is for a COTS solution required to meet the agency's zero-trust architecture objectives. The Government will not fund, authorize, or accept delivery of any custom-developed software components. Offerors are advised that the scope of this engagement is configuration only, encompassing identity provider setup, multi-factor authentication policy definition, role-based access control mapping, and directory synchronization. All proposed software must be commercially available and actively maintained by the Offeror as a standard product offering. Licensing terms must reflect commercial market rates, and the product must receive regular security patches and version updates as part of the vendor's standard release cycle. Integration connectors used to link the platform with DHS legacy systems must be pre-built and commercially supported. Engineering of new connectors or middleware specifically for this contract is outside the permissible scope of work.

Example 3:
The Department of Homeland Security, Office of the Chief Information Officer, solicits proposals for the modernization of the Personnel Security Adjudication Platform. The Government mandates use of a traditional SDLC approach consistent with DHS policy directives governing enterprise system development. The contractor shall execute the project through clearly delineated sequential phases: Concept of Operations, Requirements Definition, Preliminary Design, Critical Design, Code and Unit Test, Integration and Test, System Acceptance Test, and Operational Deployment. A formal phase gate review shall be conducted at the conclusion of each phase, with Government approval required before work proceeds. Requirements established at the conclusion of the Requirements Definition phase shall constitute the fixed requirements baseline and shall be placed under configuration management control. No development work shall begin prior to Government approval of the System Requirements Specification document.

RFP chunk to analyze:
The Department of Homeland Security (DHS), Office of the Chief Human Capital Officer, is soliciting proposals for the implementation and configuration of a commercial off-the-shelf human resources information system to consolidate workforce management functions currently distributed across three legacy platforms. DHS has completed an extensive market research effort and determined that Workday Government Cloud satisfies the functional, security, and integration requirements identified in the agency's HR modernization roadmap. The contractor shall be responsible for configuring Workday modules covering talent acquisition, onboarding, performance management, and learning administration in accordance with DHS-specific business rules and organizational hierarchies. No proprietary or custom software development shall be performed under this contract. Contractor responsibilities include leading configuration workshops with DHS HR stakeholders, developing and executing a data migration strategy for approximately 240,000 employee records, and coordinating with the DHS Security Operations Center to ensure alignment with FISMA Moderate baseline controls and applicable FedRAMP authorization documentation for the selected commercial solution. The contractor shall also deliver end-user training curricula, system administrator guides, and a post-go-live hypercare support period of no less than sixty days. Offerors shall demonstrate a minimum of five years of experience configuring and deploying Workday or comparable commercial HR platforms within large federal or state government environments. The base period of performance is eighteen months with two twelve-month option periods.

Example 1:
The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, Omaha District, is seeking proposals for facility maintenance and repair services at Camp Ashland, Nebraska. The source selection methodology for this procurement is Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA). Offerors must submit a Technical Volume and a Price Volume. The Technical Volume will be evaluated to determine whether the offeror's proposed approach meets the minimum requirements of the Performance Work Statement. Ratings will be limited to Acceptable or Unacceptable. An offeror receiving an Unacceptable technical rating will not be eligible for award. Price will be evaluated for completeness, reasonableness, and balance. Award will be made to the offeror with the lowest evaluated price whose technical proposal has been rated Acceptable. The use of LPTA in this acquisition is consistent with the determination that the requirement is well-defined and risk of unsuccessful performance is minimal. The Government does not anticipate the need for discussions.

Example 2:
The Environmental Protection Agency, Office of Acquisition Solutions, is issuing this Request for Proposals for technical support services related to water quality monitoring and sampling at Superfund sites in EPA Region 5. This acquisition will be evaluated using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process in accordance with FAR 15.101-2. Technical acceptability will be evaluated under three pass/fail criteria: Key Personnel Qualifications, Laboratory Accreditation Compliance, and Quality Assurance Plan Adequacy. Proposals must be rated Acceptable on all three criteria to be eligible for award. Technical proposals that exceed minimum requirements will receive no additional consideration. Following determination of technical acceptability, award will be made to the responsible offeror submitting the lowest evaluated total price. The Government has made a written determination that LPTA is the appropriate evaluation approach given the well-defined nature of the work and the availability of qualified contractors capable of performing at minimum standards. Questions regarding the LPTA evaluation criteria must be submitted by the deadline specified in Section L.

Example 3:
This solicitation is issued by the General Services Administration, Public Buildings Service, for grounds maintenance and landscaping services at federal facilities in the National Capital Region. Source selection will be conducted using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) methodology as prescribed in FAR 15.101-2. Evaluation factors include Technical Capability and Price. Technical Capability will be rated either Acceptable or Unacceptable based on the offeror's demonstrated ability to meet the requirements of the Statement of Work. Price will not be evaluated for technical merit. Award will be made to the offeror submitting the lowest price among all technically acceptable proposals. Proposals rated Unacceptable under the Technical Capability factor will be eliminated from consideration regardless of price. The Government reserves the right to conduct discussions if necessary but intends to make award without discussions. All offerors must be registered in SAM.gov at time of offer submission.

---

RFP chunk to analyze:
The Department of the Army, Installation Management Command, is soliciting offers for base operations support services at Fort Drum, New York, encompassing facility maintenance, snow removal, and minor construction repair tasks. The Government has determined that the use of the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process is appropriate for this acquisition given the well-defined, non-complex nature of the requirements. Evaluation will be conducted under two factors: Technical Acceptability and Price. Technical Acceptability will be assessed on a pass/fail basis against the requirements specified in the Performance Work Statement. Offerors must achieve an Acceptable rating under the Technical Acceptability factor to be eligible for award consideration. Price proposals will be evaluated solely for reasonableness; no price-technical tradeoffs will be conducted. Award will be made to the responsible offeror whose proposal is deemed technically acceptable and offers the lowest total evaluated price. The Government expressly advises offerors that exceeding minimum technical requirements will not improve the likelihood of award. Proposals that fail to meet all mandatory technical requirements will be rated Unacceptable and removed from further consideration. Offerors are encouraged to submit streamlined technical proposals that directly address compliance with each requirement without elaboration beyond what is necessary to demonstrate acceptability.

Example 1:
The Department of Veterans Affairs, Office of Information and Technology, hereby issues this Request for Proposal for the modernization and ongoing sustainment of its legacy benefits processing systems. The scope of work encompasses system architecture redesign, application development, data migration, testing and quality assurance, training, and help desk support. Based on the complexity and mission-critical nature of this effort, the VA requires the contractor to provide a dedicated project team of no less than twenty (20) full-time personnel. The staffing complement shall include, at a minimum, a Program Manager, an Agile Release Train Engineer, five (5) software developers with demonstrated Java and cloud platform experience, three (3) database administrators, two (2) DevSecOps engineers, a cybersecurity SME, two (2) business analysts, a training lead, and requisite quality assurance personnel. All staff must be U.S. citizens and obtain a VA Suitability determination prior to commencing work. The offeror's staffing plan must demonstrate that all twenty positions will be filled within thirty (30) days of contract award, with acceptable résumés for each proposed individual submitted as part of the technical proposal. The Government reserves the right to reject proposed personnel who do not meet stated qualifications.

Example 2:
The Environmental Protection Agency, Office of Water, requires scientific, technical, and programmatic support services to advance water quality monitoring, data analysis, regulatory development, and outreach activities under the Clean Water Act and Safe Drinking Water Act programs. The Government has estimated the total level of effort at approximately 28,000 labor hours annually, distributed across multiple task areas requiring diverse scientific and regulatory expertise. To execute this level of effort and maintain continuity across concurrent task orders, the contractor shall maintain a dedicated team of no fewer than sixteen (16) full-time equivalent personnel. The team composition shall include environmental scientists, hydrologists, chemists, toxicologists, policy analysts, GIS specialists, technical writers, and program support staff with direct experience supporting EPA regulatory programs. The offeror's staffing plan must demonstrate that the sixteen-person team collectively possesses the competencies required to address all task areas identified in the Performance Work Statement. The Government will evaluate the staffing plan for both the depth of individual expertise and the overall team's capacity to absorb new task orders within existing resources. The contractor shall not reduce staffing below sixteen FTEs without prior written approval from the Contracting Officer's Representative and a corresponding contract modification.

Example 3:
The Department of Justice, Executive Office for Immigration Review, requires comprehensive information technology support services to sustain and enhance court management systems used by immigration judges across more than seventy (70) immigration courts nationwide. The contractor must deploy a standing team of not fewer than eighteen (18) IT professionals to execute the full scope of work, which includes help desk operations, application maintenance and development, network administration, data center management, cybersecurity monitoring, and business intelligence reporting. The required staffing configuration shall include a Program Manager with PMP certification, a Deputy Program Manager, a Help Desk Manager overseeing a staff of at least four (4) Tier I and Tier II support specialists, three (3) application developers, two (2) database administrators, a network engineer, a cybersecurity analyst, a data warehouse specialist, and a configuration management specialist. The contractor shall maintain this minimum staffing level throughout all base and option periods. Proposed personnel must successfully complete a DOJ background investigation prior to being granted access to DOJ networks and systems. The staffing plan, including résumés for all eighteen key and non-key personnel, shall be submitted with Volume II of the technical proposal.

RFP chunk to analyze:
The U.S. Department of Labor, Employment and Training Administration, hereby solicits proposals for workforce development program evaluation and performance management technical assistance services to be delivered to state workforce agencies, local workforce development boards, and American Job Center operators nationwide. The scope of work encompasses program performance data analysis, outcome evaluation design and execution, technical assistance delivery through on-site visits and virtual engagements, development of evaluation toolkits and resource materials, policy analysis, and support for ETA's evidence-building agenda as required under the Foundations for Evidence-Based Policymaking Act. The complexity of this requirement, the geographic reach of the beneficiary agencies spanning all fifty states and territories, and the diversity of programmatic expertise required across Workforce Innovation and Opportunity Act title programs necessitate a contractor team of substantial size and capability. The Department has determined that no fewer than thirteen (13) full-time personnel must be dedicated to this contract at all times. The required team shall include a Project Director with senior leadership experience in federal workforce programs, a Research and Evaluation Director holding a doctoral degree in a relevant social science discipline, three (3) Senior Program Analysts with WIOA title-specific expertise, two (2) Statisticians or Quantitative Analysts proficient in administrative data linkage and outcome modeling, two (2) Technical Assistance Specialists experienced in adult learning and capacity building, a Communications and Dissemination Specialist, a Data Systems Analyst, and at least two (2) Program Analysts providing general support across task areas. Offerors must demonstrate in their Management and Staffing Plan that all thirteen positions will be filled at contract award and sustained throughout the five-year ordering period. Proposals reflecting fewer than thirteen dedicated full-time equivalents will not be considered for award.

Example 1:
The Department of Energy, National Nuclear Security Administration, is issuing this solicitation for nuclear security program support services at a classified facility in the Southeast United States. The scope includes physical security assessments, force-on-force exercise planning and execution, vulnerability analysis, security technology evaluation, safeguards and security training development, and regulatory compliance support. Given the classified nature of operations, the 24-hour operational tempo of the facility, and the technical complexity of nuclear security disciplines, the contractor must provide a minimum of thirteen (13) cleared personnel at all times. The required team includes a Program Director with a minimum of twenty (20) years of combined military and nuclear security experience, a Deputy Program Director, three (3) Physical Security Specialists, two (2) Force-on-Force Exercise Specialists, two (2) Safeguards and Security Analysts, a Security Technology Engineer, a Training Specialist, and two (2) Compliance Analysts. All personnel must possess active Q clearances at time of award. Offerors shall submit a staffing plan demonstrating that all thirteen positions are filled by named individuals meeting the qualification requirements set forth in Section J, Attachment 3. Personnel proposed for this effort may be subject to Government approval prior to assignment.

Example 2:
The Department of Energy Office of Environmental Management hereby solicits proposals for a project controls and earned value management system to support cleanup program oversight at multiple national laboratory sites. This acquisition is structured as a commercial item purchase under FAR Subpart 12.3 and requires a COTS solution required to be delivered without modification to core application logic. No software development is permitted under this contract. Offerors who propose to build custom modules, develop proprietary integrations requiring new code, or enhance existing product functionality through engineering effort will be found non-compliant. The permissible scope of work is configuration only and includes definition of work breakdown structures, cost account mapping, schedule baseline setup, earned value calculation method selection using product-native options, and report layout customization within the platform's standard reporting environment. The DOE contracting officer's representative will serve as the primary configuration authority and must approve all system settings prior to go-live. A configuration management plan shall be submitted within thirty days of contract award.

Example 3:
The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, Baltimore District, is soliciting proposals for environmental remediation and site restoration services at a formerly used defense site located in the Mid-Atlantic region. Given the scale of contamination, the complexity of multi-media remediation (soil, groundwater, and sediment), and the requirement for concurrent work across three distinct operable units, the Government has determined that a minimum of fifteen (15) qualified personnel must be engaged on this contract at all times during active remediation phases. The required team shall include a licensed Professional Engineer serving as Project Manager, a Certified Industrial Hygienist, at minimum two (2) licensed Geologists, a Health and Safety Officer, four (4) field technicians, a quality control manager, and sufficient support staff to maintain documentation, community relations, and regulatory liaison functions. The period of performance is estimated at thirty-six (36) months with two (2) twelve-month option periods. Offerors shall demonstrate in their Management Plan that the proposed team possesses the depth of expertise and numerical sufficiency to execute overlapping work streams without degradation of quality or schedule. Failure to staff to the required minimum levels at any point during performance may constitute a material breach of contract.

The Department of Energy, Office of Environmental Management, is releasing this Request for Proposal for deactivation and decommissioning support services at a legacy nuclear research facility slated for eventual transfer to the host state for unrestricted use. Work activities will encompass radiological characterization surveys, hazardous material abatement, structural demolition planning, waste packaging and manifesting, regulatory agency interface, and final status surveys in accordance with NRC and DOE Order requirements. The Government has determined, based on independent government estimates and lessons learned from comparable D&D projects, that effective execution of concurrent characterization, abatement, and waste disposition activities requires a dedicated project workforce of no fewer than twenty-two (22) personnel during active decommissioning phases. The required staffing baseline shall include a Project Manager holding a current Professional Engineer license, a Deputy Project Manager, a Radiation Protection Manager certified under 10 CFR Part 835, three (3) Health Physics Technicians, a Certified Industrial Hygienist, two (2) Asbestos Supervisors licensed in the host state, a Waste Shipping Coordinator with current DOT hazmat certification, four (4) Decontamination and Decommissioning Technicians, two (2) Structural Engineers, a Quality Assurance Manager, a Document Control Specialist, and sufficient field support personnel to maintain safe operations across all active work zones simultaneously. The offeror's Integrated Management Plan shall include an organizational chart, a staffing ramp-up schedule by contract month, and a narrative explanation of how the contractor will sustain the minimum complement of twenty-two personnel across a base period of thirty months and two option periods of twelve months each.

Example 1:
SOLICITATION NUMBER: HHS-2024-EDWOSB-0042

This acquisition is a 100% set-aside for Economically Disadvantaged Women-Owned Small Business (EDWOSB) concerns under the authority of 15 U.S.C. 637(m). The Department of Health and Human Services, Office of Acquisition Services, intends to award a firm-fixed-price contract for the provision of professional health information technology consulting services in support of the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services modernization initiative. Only offerors who are certified Economically Disadvantaged Women-Owned Small Business concerns at the time of offer submission and at the time of award are eligible to receive this contract. Offerors must be registered in the System for Award Management (SAM.gov) and must have a current EDWOSB certification through the SBA certification program or a third-party certifier approved by the Small Business Administration. The applicable NAICS code is 541512 with a small business size standard of $34 million in average annual receipts. Any offer received from a concern that does not qualify as an EDWOSB at the time of offer will be considered nonresponsive and will not be evaluated. Questions regarding EDWOSB eligibility requirements should be directed to the contracting officer prior to the closing date of this solicitation.

Example 2:
DEPARTMENT OF HOMELAND SECURITY
U.S. CUSTOMS AND BORDER PROTECTION
Solicitation No.: 70B04C24R00000023

NOTICE: This procurement action is set aside exclusively for Economically Disadvantaged Women-Owned Small Business (EDWOSB) concerns as defined under 13 CFR 127.200 and implemented through FAR 19.1505(b). U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) seeks to acquire translation and interpretation services in more than 40 languages to support field operations at U.S. ports of entry along the southern and northern borders. The applicable NAICS code is 541930 – Translation and Interpretation Services, with a small business size standard of $19 million. This set-aside has been authorized following market research that revealed sufficient EDWOSB competition within the relevant industry to warrant restriction of the solicitation. Offerors must be certified as Economically Disadvantaged Women-Owned Small Businesses through a certifier recognized by the SBA and must maintain active certification throughout the entire period of performance, which includes a base year and up to four option years. Offerors are advised that misrepresentation of EDWOSB status constitutes a violation of 15 U.S.C. 645(d) and may result in suspension, debarment, and civil or criminal penalties. Certification status will be verified by the contracting officer through SAM.gov and the SBA's certification database prior to award.

Example 3:
DEPARTMENT OF EDUCATION
OFFICE OF ACQUISITIONS
Solicitation Number: ED-ODS-24-C-0087

The U.S. Department of Education, Office of the Deputy Secretary, is issuing this Request for Proposals as an Economically Disadvantaged Women-Owned Small Business (EDWOSB) set-aside pursuant to FAR 19.1505 and the SBA's regulations at 13 CFR Part 127. The Department requires program evaluation and performance measurement services to assess the effectiveness of Title I grant programs across selected local educational agencies. This acquisition falls under NAICS code 541611 – Administrative Management and General Management Consulting Services, with a size standard of $24.5 million. To be eligible for this award, an offeror must qualify as an Economically Disadvantaged Women-Owned Small Business concern and must hold a current, valid EDWOSB certification issued by the SBA or a third-party certifier recognized by the SBA. Mere self-certification as a WOSB or EDWOSB is no longer sufficient; offerors must provide a third-party or SBA-issued certification as required under the SBA's final rule effective October 15, 2020. Proposals received from firms that do not meet the EDWOSB eligibility criteria will be rejected without evaluation. The anticipated period of performance is three years from the date of award, inclusive of options.

FEDERAL EMERGENCY MANAGEMENT AGENCY

The anticipated period of performance consists of a 12-month base period with two 12-month options. The National Aeronautics and Space Administration, Goddard Space Flight Center, is seeking a contractor to provide engineering, integration, and test support services for an upcoming Earth science mission. The nature of the work, which includes systems engineering, instrument calibration, software verification, environmental testing, launch support, and post-launch on-orbit operations, necessitates a large, multidisciplinary team. The contractor shall maintain a team of at minimum fourteen (14) engineers and scientists throughout the performance period, with additional personnel required during critical mission phases such as environmental test campaigns and launch operations. The core team shall include systems engineers, mechanical engineers, electrical engineers, software engineers, and integration and test specialists with demonstrated spaceflight heritage. The offeror shall provide a staffing matrix in the proposal that maps each of the fourteen required positions to an individual, identifies their role, qualifications, and availability by contract phase. The Government understands that mission phases will drive variable workload and reserves the right to request surge staffing above the fourteen-person baseline; however, the minimum staffing level shall not fall below fourteen individuals at any time during the base period of performance.

Example 2:
The National Aeronautics and Space Administration, Glenn Research Center, located in Cleveland, Ohio, requires support services for wind tunnel operations and test article preparation under this solicitation. All work under this contract shall be performed at NASA Glenn Research Center, 21000 Brookpark Road, Cleveland, OH 44135. The unique nature of the test infrastructure, including the Icing Research Tunnel, the Supersonic Wind Tunnel, and associated diagnostic equipment, mandates that all contractor technicians, engineers, and support staff perform their assigned duties exclusively on the Center's premises. Physical manipulation of test hardware, sensor calibration, data acquisition systems operation, and real-time monitoring of test conditions require continuous on-site presence. Remote access to NASA's restricted research networks is not authorized for contractor personnel under this effort. All deliverables, including test reports, technical drawings, and calibration records, must be generated using government-furnished workstations within the assigned laboratory spaces at Glenn Research Center. The contractor shall maintain a minimum staffing level as specified in the Performance Work Statement at the Glenn facility every scheduled workday throughout the period of performance.

All personnel must possess active Q clearances at time of award. The contractor shall organize personnel into Agile squads operating in two-week sprints, with each squad responsible for a defined set of product backlog items. All Agile ceremonies, including sprint planning, daily standups, sprint reviews, and retrospectives, shall be conducted in person or via Government-approved collaboration tools. The contractor shall employ a certified Scrum Master for each squad who is accountable for coaching team members on Agile principles and ensuring adherence to Scrum practices. Sprint planning shall be completed within four hours for a two-week sprint, and the contractor shall ensure that sprint goals are clearly articulated and agreed upon by the Government Product Owner prior to sprint commencement. The contractor shall maintain a Definition of Done that includes code review completion, unit test coverage thresholds, security scanning, and Product Owner acceptance. Working software shall be deployed to a staging environment at the end of each sprint for Government validation. The contractor shall produce and maintain an Agile project charter documenting team norms, Agile ceremony schedules, tooling agreements, and escalation procedures. Release planning artifacts including feature roadmaps and program increment objectives shall be reviewed and approved by the NASA Contracting Officer's Representative on a quarterly basis.

Contractors responding to this solicitation must understand that this effort constitutes continuing work on a system first placed into production in 2016. SSA does not anticipate a full redesign during the base period; rather, the contractor is expected to maintain, enhance, and incrementally modernize the existing system in accordance with the Technology Modernization Plan attached as Exhibit C. The Government will facilitate a structured transition from incumbent contractor Leidos, providing access to all repository assets, architectural documentation, and operations runbooks. Proposals must include a staffing plan that accounts for immediate knowledge absorption from outgoing personnel during a 120-day transition period.

Example 3:
The Department of Veterans Affairs seeks a qualified contractor to assume responsibility for the continuing work on the Veterans Benefits Management System (VBMS). The selected vendor will inherit the existing system architecture, currently maintained by the incumbent contractor whose period of performance concludes on September 30, 2024. The incoming contractor must demonstrate proven experience onboarding into a legacy codebase without service disruption. Transition activities shall include a mandatory 90-day knowledge transfer period during which the outgoing and incoming contractors will operate concurrently. The Government requires that the new contractor review all existing technical documentation, data dictionaries, and system design records prior to assuming full operational responsibility. Offerors must describe in their technical approach how they will manage risk during transition from incumbent operations, including how they will handle undocumented components of the existing system. Continuity of service to Veterans during the transition period is a non-negotiable requirement, and the Government will evaluate all proposals on the basis of transition risk mitigation strategies.

Example 2:
The Department of Transportation invites qualified vendors to participate in a mandatory design challenge as part of this solicitation process. Offerors shall develop and submit a working prototype of their proposed traffic management interface within thirty (30) calendar days of solicitation release. The prototype must demonstrate core functionality including real-time data ingestion, alert escalation workflows, and dashboard visualization. Government evaluators will conduct hands-on testing of each submitted prototype during a structured demonstration session scheduled at DOT headquarters in Washington, D.C. Scoring criteria for the design challenge component account for forty percent (40%) of the overall technical evaluation score. Prototypes that fail to meet minimum functionality thresholds as defined in Attachment C will be deemed technically unacceptable and removed from further consideration. Offerors are encouraged to review the Technical Evaluation Criteria in Section M prior to initiating prototype development. All prototype submissions must be delivered via secure government portal no later than 5:00 PM Eastern Time on the date specified in the solicitation timeline.

Example 3:

Each offeror will be given access to a standardized environmental monitoring dataset and tasked with producing a data quality analysis report and visualization dashboard within a three-hour period. The hands-on exercise will be conducted at EPA's Research Triangle Park facility. Offerors must bring all necessary hardware and licensed software; EPA will provide only network connectivity and access to the standardized dataset via a secure file transfer mechanism. Evaluators will assess the quality, accuracy, and clarity of the analysis report and dashboard outputs using criteria specified in Section M.4. The hands-on exercise score will be combined with written technical proposal scores to produce a composite technical rating. Offerors are advised that strong written proposals will not compensate for poor performance on the hands-on exercise, as the Agency views demonstrated capability as equally important to proposed methodology. All outputs produced during the exercise must be submitted to the Contracting Officer's Representative before participants depart the facility.

The National Aeronautics and Space Administration, Goddard Space Flight Center, is seeking a contractor to provide engineering, integration, and test support services for an upcoming Earth science mission. The nature of the work, which includes systems engineering, instrument calibration, software verification, environmental testing, launch support, and post-launch on-orbit operations, necessitates a large, multidisciplinary team. The contractor shall maintain a team of at minimum fourteen (14) engineers and scientists throughout the performance period, with additional personnel required during critical mission phases such as environmental test campaigns and launch operations. The core team shall include systems engineers, mechanical engineers, electrical engineers, software engineers, and integration and test specialists with demonstrated spaceflight heritage. The offeror shall provide a staffing matrix in the proposal that maps each of the fourteen required positions to an individual, identifies their role, qualifications, and availability by contract phase. The Government understands that mission phases will drive variable workload and reserves the right to request surge staffing above the fourteen-person baseline; however, the minimum staffing level shall not fall below fourteen individuals at any time during the base period of performance.

Example 2:
The Department of Transportation, Federal Aviation Administration, is soliciting for acquisition support and program management assistance services for the Aviation Safety organization. The breadth of this requirement includes acquisition planning, cost estimating, source selection support, contract administration, program schedule management, risk management, independent verification and validation, and stakeholder communications. Due to the volume of active acquisitions and the complexity of ongoing program portfolios, the FAA has determined that a team of no fewer than eleven (11) full-time contractor personnel is required to execute this work satisfactorily. The required team shall include a Senior Program Manager with at least fifteen (15) years of federal acquisition experience, a Cost Estimator with ICEAA certification, two (2) Contract Specialists with FAC-C Level II or equivalent credentials, two (2) Program Analysts, a Scheduler proficient in Primavera P6, a Risk Manager, an IVV Lead, and two (2) junior analysts providing research and documentation support. The contractor shall assign named individuals to each position prior to contract award; substitution of key personnel during the first twelve (12) months of performance is prohibited without written Contracting Officer approval. Staffing levels at or above eleven personnel shall be maintained throughout the entire period of performance.

Example 3:
The Department of Energy, National Nuclear Security Administration, is issuing this solicitation for nuclear security program support services at a classified facility in the Southeast United States. The scope includes physical security assessments, force-on-force exercise planning and execution, vulnerability analysis, security technology evaluation, safeguards and security training development, and regulatory compliance support. Given the classified nature of operations, the 24-hour operational tempo of the facility, and the technical complexity of nuclear security disciplines, the contractor must provide a minimum of thirteen (13) cleared personnel at all times. The required team includes a Program Director with a minimum of twenty (20) years of combined military and nuclear security experience, a Deputy Program Director, three (3) Physical Security Specialists, two (2) Force-on-Force Exercise Specialists, two (2) Safeguards and Security Analysts, a Security Technology Engineer, a Training Specialist, and two (2) Compliance Analysts. All personnel must possess active Q clearances at time of award. Offerors shall submit a staffing plan demonstrating that all thirteen positions are filled by named individuals meeting the qualification requirements set forth in Section J, Attachment 3. Personnel proposed for this effort may be subject to Government approval prior to assignment.

U.S. SMALL BUSINESS ADMINISTRATION
OFFICE OF PROCUREMENT
SOLICITATION NO. SBAHQ-24-Q-0027
ADMINISTRATIVE SUPPORT SERVICES

SET-ASIDE: This acquisition is set aside 100% for Women-Owned Small Business (WOSB) concerns as authorized under 15 U.S.C. 637(m) and implemented through FAR 19.15. The SBA has determined through market research that this requirement falls within an industry identified by the SBA as underrepresented or substantially underrepresented by women-owned businesses, thus satisfying the industry designation requirement for a WOSB set-aside. BACKGROUND AND ACQUISITION HISTORY

The Department of Transportation (DOT) has a longstanding commitment to maximizing small business participation in its contracting portfolio. The agency annually achieves or exceeds governmentwide small business prime contracting and subcontracting goals through deliberate acquisition planning. For this procurement, the Office of the Senior Procurement Executive reviewed the requirement and determined that the magnitude of the effort, estimated at approximately $180 million over a five-year ordering period, exceeds the threshold at which a small business set-aside would be viable without fragmenting the requirement in a manner inconsistent with sound acquisition practice. Accordingly, this solicitation is issued on an unrestricted basis. It should not be inferred that the agency has reduced its commitment to small business development; rather, the agency has structured this acquisition to require meaningful small business subcontracting at not less than 35 percent of total contract value. This subcontracting floor reflects input from the agency's OSDBU and market research conducted over a twelve-month period. Offerors that are themselves small business concerns are reminded that the subcontracting plan requirement under FAR 52.219-9 applies only to other-than-small businesses receiving contracts exceeding $750,000. Small business primes are exempt from the formal plan requirement but are still encouraged to document their intended use of small business suppliers and teammates.

As part of the evaluation methodology for this Information Technology Services procurement, the General Services Administration (GSA) will incorporate oral presentations into the source selection process. After an initial review of written proposals, offerors receiving a rating of Acceptable or higher under the Technical Factor will be invited to participate in an oral presentation before the Technical Evaluation Team. The oral presentation allows the Government to assess offeror capabilities beyond what is conveyed in written form and to evaluate proposed personnel directly. Each offeror will be allotted ninety (90) minutes total: sixty (60) minutes for the presentation and thirty (30) minutes for Government-directed questions. Key personnel named in Section H must be present and actively participate. Substitutions of key personnel for the oral presentation will not be permitted without prior written approval and may negatively impact the evaluation. Offerors should note that the oral presentation is evaluated separately under Factor 2 – Management Approach and scored on an Adjectival basis consistent with the criteria described in Section M.

Example 2:
Proposals submitted in response to this solicitation will undergo a multi-phase evaluation process. Phase I consists of written technical and price proposal review. Offerors achieving a combined written evaluation score that places them within the competitive range will advance to Phase II, which requires an oral presentation before the Department of Transportation Technical Review Board. The oral presentation phase is designed to supplement written proposals by allowing offerors to demonstrate technical depth, address evaluator questions regarding proposed approaches, and introduce key personnel who will execute the contract. Oral presentations will be evaluated under Factor 1 – Technical Approach and Factor 3 – Key Personnel, and scores derived from the oral presentation will be weighted equally with written proposal scores for those factors. Each presentation is allocated ninety (90) minutes, with no more than sixty (60) minutes reserved for the offeror's presentation and the remainder designated for Government questions. The offeror's Project Manager and at least two senior technical staff members named in the proposal must be physically present.

Example 3:
Offerors in the competitive range shall be required to participate in an oral presentation as part of the evaluation process. The oral presentation will be conducted at the Department of Veterans Affairs Network Contracting Office and will be scheduled at the Government's discretion. The purpose of the oral presentation is to assess each offeror's understanding of the Performance Work Statement requirements, proposed staffing model, and quality control methodology. Presentations shall be a maximum of seventy-five (75) minutes in duration, which includes a mandatory fifteen (15) minute question-and-answer session conducted by the Technical Evaluation Panel. The proposed Contract Program Manager must attend and lead the presentation. Supporting staff may participate but shall not exceed four (4) individuals total. All slide materials or visual aids must be provided to the Contracting Officer no later than three (3) business days prior to the presentation. The Government may record oral presentations for evaluation documentation purposes, and offerors will be notified accordingly.

Phase I consists of the evaluation of written technical and price proposals. Offerors achieving a rating of Acceptable or higher under all non-price factors during Phase I will advance to Phase II, which requires the delivery of a formal oral presentation to the Evaluation Advisory Group (EAG). The oral presentation component is designed to assess offeror capability in real time and to validate claims made within the written technical volume. Each oral presentation shall be allocated a maximum of sixty (60) minutes, inclusive of a mandatory fifteen (15) minute question-and-answer segment. Offerors must designate a presentation lead who holds the proposed Program Manager role as identified in the staffing plan. Phase II oral presentation scores will be evaluated under Factor 2, Technical Approach, with equal weight applied to written and oral components in computing the composite technical score. Offerors are strongly encouraged to use the oral presentation to highlight innovative methodologies, demonstrate subject matter expertise, and articulate their understanding of GSA's acquisition modernization priorities as described in the Performance Work Statement.

Example 1:
Under this task order issued against the NASA Solutions for Enterprise-Wide Procurement contract, the contractor shall manage and execute all software development activities in accordance with Agile methodology. The contractor shall implement a Scrum-based framework and shall operate all development teams on a uniform two-week sprint cadence synchronized with the program's release train schedule. Sprint planning shall be completed within four hours for a two-week sprint, and the contractor shall ensure that sprint goals are clearly articulated and agreed upon by the Government Product Owner prior to sprint commencement. The contractor shall maintain a Definition of Done that includes code review completion, unit test coverage thresholds, security scanning, and Product Owner acceptance. Working software shall be deployed to a staging environment at the end of each sprint for Government validation. The contractor shall produce and maintain an Agile project charter documenting team norms, Agile ceremony schedules, tooling agreements, and escalation procedures. Release planning artifacts including feature roadmaps and program increment objectives shall be reviewed and approved by the NASA Contracting Officer's Representative on a quarterly basis.

Example 2:
Pursuant to the Department of Defense's software acquisition pathway as defined in DoDI 5000.87, the contractor shall deliver capabilities using Agile software development methodology with continuous integration and continuous delivery pipelines. The contractor shall organize personnel into Agile squads operating in two-week sprints, with each squad responsible for a defined set of product backlog items. All Agile ceremonies, including sprint planning, daily standups, sprint reviews, and retrospectives, shall be conducted in person or via Government-approved collaboration tools. The contractor shall employ a certified Scrum Master for each squad who is accountable for coaching team members on Agile principles and ensuring adherence to Scrum practices. A product roadmap updated at minimum quarterly shall be maintained and shared with Government stakeholders to provide visibility into planned increments of capability. The use of Kanban boards or equivalent visual management tools is required to provide real-time transparency into work-in-progress, blocked items, and completed work. Sprint velocity shall be tracked and used to inform release planning and capacity projections.

Example 3:
This Performance Work Statement requires the contractor to execute all software design, development, and testing activities using Agile Scrum methodology in strict compliance with the agency's DevSecOps and Agile Transformation Initiative. The contractor shall establish and maintain Agile teams with a recommended team size of five to nine members, each including a dedicated Scrum Master and supported by a Government-designated Product Owner. User stories shall be written with clearly defined acceptance criteria using the standard 'As a [user], I want [functionality], so that [benefit]' format and shall be estimated using Planning Poker or equivalent relative sizing techniques. The contractor shall operate on a consistent two-week sprint cadence and shall not alter sprint scope once the sprint has commenced without documented consent from the Contracting Officer's Representative. Sprint retrospective outputs shall be shared with the Government and incorporated into the team's continuous improvement plan. Quarterly roadmaps reflecting planned feature delivery shall be maintained and updated following each sprint review.

SECTION C – STATEMENT OF WORK

5.4 Coordination and Stakeholder Engagement

The Contractor shall coordinate closely with agency program offices to ensure deliverables align with mission requirements. Historical precedent under predecessor contracts involved extensive on-site presence at the client agency; however, this follow-on acquisition has been restructured to reflect lessons learned and changing operational needs. On-site presence is no longer a contract requirement. Stakeholder engagement shall be accomplished primarily through scheduled video conferences, collaborative document platforms, and asynchronous communication tools approved by the Government. The Contractor's project manager shall be available during core business hours (9:00 AM – 3:00 PM Eastern) regardless of the physical location from which they are working. This requirement is intended to ensure responsiveness and does not mandate any particular geographic location or facility. Where in-person workshops are deemed necessary by the COR, the Contractor shall coordinate logistics in advance, and such events shall be treated as discrete, scheduled activities rather than ongoing performance obligations. The Government will provide at least fifteen (15) business days' notice prior to any requested in-person engagement. Offerors operating with fully distributed teams are considered equally competitive under this solicitation.
