The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, Louisville District, is soliciting offers under a total Economically Disadvantaged Women-Owned Small Business (EDWOSB) set-aside for architectural and engineering services related to the design and rehabilitation of flood control infrastructure in the Ohio River watershed. This procurement is conducted in accordance with the Brooks Act (40 U.S.C. 1101 et seq.) and the Small Business Act's EDWOSB provisions at 15 U.S.C. 637(m). The NAICS code applicable to this requirement is 541330 – Engineering Services, with a size standard of $25.5 million. Only EDWOSB-certified firms may submit qualifications packages in response to this announcement. To qualify, the concern must be at least 51 percent unconditionally and directly owned and controlled by one or more women who are economically disadvantaged as defined by 13 CFR 127.200. Firms must demonstrate active EDWOSB certification through the SBA's certification platform (certify.sba.gov) or an approved third-party certifier. Failure to maintain EDWOSB certification through the date of award shall result in the offeror being deemed ineligible. Subcontracting to non-EDWOSB firms is permitted in accordance with applicable FAR provisions, provided the EDWOSB prime contractor performs the required percentage of work.

FSA requires the contractor to employ a waterfall development lifecycle consistent with USDA's Departmental Regulation 3300-001 governing enterprise application development. The contractor shall organize all development activities into the following sequential phases: Requirements Elicitation and Validation, System Architecture and Design, Database Design, Application Development, Unit and Integration Testing, System Testing, User Acceptance Testing, and Production Implementation. Formal phase gate reviews shall be conducted at the completion of each phase, and the Government's written approval to proceed is a prerequisite for initiation of any subsequent phase. A fixed requirements baseline shall be established upon Government acceptance of the Approved System Requirements Document. Modifications to the approved baseline are subject to the FSA Change Management process, require documented business justification, and must receive written Contracting Officer authorization before implementation.

Example 2:
The Department of Veterans Affairs requires the contractor to employ a waterfall methodology for the development of the Veterans Benefits Management System Enhancement. All work shall proceed through sequential phases including requirements analysis, system design, implementation, testing, deployment, and maintenance. Each phase must be fully completed and formally accepted by the Contracting Officer's Representative prior to initiation of the subsequent phase. The contractor shall maintain a fixed requirements baseline established during the requirements phase, which shall not be altered without formal change control board approval and contract modification. Deliverables associated with each phase gate must be submitted, reviewed, and approved before any downstream development activities commence. The contractor shall provide a detailed Waterfall Project Plan within 30 days of contract award, identifying all phase entry and exit criteria, milestone dates, and associated deliverables. Monthly status reports shall track progress against the approved baseline schedule and document any variances exceeding five percent of planned effort.

Phase-to-phase progression is contingent upon successful completion of formal Government review and written authorization to proceed. The contractor is expressly prohibited from initiating coding activities prior to approval of the Functional Requirements Document and System Architecture Document. A fixed requirements baseline shall be established no later than 60 calendar days after contract award. Subsequent changes to baselined requirements must be processed through the formal Engineering Change Proposal process and approved in writing by the Contracting Officer.

The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, Omaha District, is seeking proposals for facility maintenance and repair services at Camp Ashland, Nebraska. The source selection methodology for this procurement is Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA). Offerors must submit a Technical Volume and a Price Volume. The Technical Volume will be evaluated to determine whether the offeror's proposed approach meets the minimum requirements of the Performance Work Statement. Ratings will be limited to Acceptable or Unacceptable. An offeror receiving an Unacceptable technical rating will not be eligible for award. Price will be evaluated for completeness, reasonableness, and balance. Award will be made to the offeror with the lowest evaluated price whose technical proposal has been rated Acceptable. The use of LPTA in this acquisition is consistent with the determination that the requirement is well-defined and risk of unsuccessful performance is minimal. The Government does not anticipate the need for discussions.

Example 2:
The Environmental Protection Agency, Office of Acquisition Solutions, is issuing this Request for Proposals for technical support services related to water quality monitoring and sampling at Superfund sites in EPA Region 5. This acquisition will be evaluated using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process in accordance with FAR 15.101-2. Technical acceptability will be evaluated under three pass/fail criteria: Key Personnel Qualifications, Laboratory Accreditation Compliance, and Quality Assurance Plan Adequacy. Proposals must be rated Acceptable on all three criteria to be eligible for award. Technical proposals that exceed minimum requirements will receive no additional consideration. Following determination of technical acceptability, award will be made to the responsible offeror submitting the lowest evaluated total price. The Government has made a written determination that LPTA is the appropriate evaluation approach given the well-defined nature of the work and the availability of qualified contractors capable of performing at minimum standards. Questions regarding the LPTA evaluation criteria must be submitted by the deadline specified in Section L.

Example 3:
The Department of Veterans Affairs intends to award a contract for janitorial and custodial services at the Phoenix VA Medical Center. The Government will evaluate offers using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process. Technical acceptability will be determined on a pass/fail basis. Offerors must demonstrate compliance with all technical requirements outlined in the Performance Work Statement. Price will be evaluated for reasonableness and balance. The Government will award a contract to the responsible offeror whose offer conforms to the solicitation requirements and represents the lowest price technically acceptable. No tradeoffs will be made between technical merit and price. Offerors are advised that the Government does not intend to pay a premium for technical proposals that exceed the minimum requirements. Only proposals determined to be technically acceptable will be considered for award. Offerors should not propose capabilities beyond those required, as such additions will not enhance the probability of award under this LPTA evaluation methodology.

This procurement is strictly limited to the acquisition of an existing commercial solution. No software development is authorized under any circumstances. Offerors shall not propose to build, code, or engineer new application modules in response to agency-specific requirements. The Government's intent is to leverage a proven, market-available product and adapt internal business processes to align with the tool's existing capabilities. Configuration of user permissions, workflow routing, document templates, and integration with existing financial systems via standard APIs is permissible and expected. Offerors must demonstrate that the proposed platform has been deployed in at least three federal agency environments. Proposals that include statements of work referencing sprint cycles, agile development backlogs, or custom feature engineering will be evaluated unfavorably and may be deemed non-compliant with this solicitation's scope restrictions.

Example 2:
The Department of Homeland Security, Office of the Chief Information Officer, hereby solicits proposals for an enterprise identity and access management solution. This requirement is for a COTS solution required to meet the agency's zero-trust architecture objectives. The Government will not fund, authorize, or accept delivery of any custom-developed software components. Offerors are advised that the scope of this engagement is configuration only, encompassing identity provider setup, multi-factor authentication policy definition, role-based access control mapping, and directory synchronization. All proposed software must be commercially available and actively maintained by the Offeror as a standard product offering. Licensing terms must reflect commercial market rates, and the product must receive regular security patches and version updates as part of the vendor's standard release cycle. Integration connectors used to link the platform with DHS legacy systems must be pre-built and commercially supported. Engineering of new connectors or middleware specifically for this contract is outside the permissible scope of work.

The legacy codebase includes multiple subsystems developed across several prior contract periods, resulting in heterogeneous technology stacks that the contractor must be prepared to support concurrently. GSA anticipates that the first six months of the contract will be heavily weighted toward stabilization and documentation of the existing system before new feature development proceeds at full velocity. The Government's Quality Assurance Surveillance Plan includes specific metrics for transition risk during this stabilization phase.

This HUBZone set-aside is established under the authority of FAR 19.1305 and the Small Business Act, 15 U.S.C. § 657a. The contractor shall implement Agile development methodologies throughout the period of performance. All software development activities shall be conducted using Scrum framework, with two-week sprint cycles. The contractor shall conduct daily stand-up meetings, sprint planning sessions, sprint reviews, and sprint retrospectives in accordance with Scrum ceremonies. A certified Scrum Master shall be designated and responsible for facilitating all Agile ceremonies and removing impediments to team progress. The Government Product Owner shall participate in sprint planning and backlog grooming sessions to prioritize user stories and acceptance criteria. The contractor shall maintain a product backlog in a Government-approved project management tool and provide sprint velocity metrics as part of monthly progress reporting. All deliverables shall be produced incrementally, with a potentially shippable product increment demonstrated at the conclusion of each sprint. The contractor shall adhere to the Agile Manifesto principles and demonstrate continuous improvement through retrospective action items documented and tracked across sprints.

Example 2:
The General Services Administration Office of Information Technology requires offerors to demonstrate proven experience delivering projects using Agile development practices. The period of performance shall be executed through iterative Agile sprints of two-week duration. The contractor shall implement all five Scrum events: Sprint Planning, Daily Scrum, Sprint Review, Sprint Retrospective, and the Sprint itself, with Government personnel invited and encouraged to participate in Sprint Reviews and Retrospectives. The contractor's Agile Coach shall work with the Government Product Owner to build and maintain a prioritized product backlog, ensuring that the highest-value features are delivered earliest. Acceptance testing shall be performed within the sprint in which development occurs, consistent with Agile testing best practices and shift-left quality principles. The contractor shall track and report on Agile health metrics including team happiness index, sprint goal achievement rate, and escaped defect rate. All tools used to manage the Agile process, including the backlog and sprint boards, shall be accessible to designated Government personnel at all times.

Example 3:
The Social Security Administration requires all IT development work performed under this Blanket Purchase Agreement to comply with SSA's Agile Development Standards. Offerors shall describe their Agile delivery model in the technical approach and demonstrate how their proposed methodology aligns with Scrum or Kanban frameworks. Contractors shall conduct sprint planning meetings with SSA's Product Owner to decompose epics into sprint-ready user stories with acceptance criteria no later than the iteration planning session preceding each sprint. The contractor shall update the Government's enterprise project management tool daily with sprint board status, impediments, and burndown data. Demonstration of working software to SSA stakeholders shall occur at the end of every sprint, and the Government reserves the right to redirect backlog priorities following each sprint review based on mission needs. The contractor shall implement Definition of Ready criteria to ensure user stories are sufficiently detailed prior to sprint commitment. Quarterly business reviews shall include Agile performance metrics, including average sprint velocity, defect density, and sprint goal completion percentage.

The contractor shall establish and maintain cross-functional Scrum teams, with each team comprised of a designated Scrum Master, Product Owner proxy, software engineers, quality assurance personnel, and user experience designers as appropriate to the task. Sprint cycles shall be uniform at two weeks in duration across all active development teams. The contractor shall conduct all standard Scrum ceremonies, including sprint planning, daily stand-ups, sprint reviews, and sprint retrospectives, and shall provide meeting notes and action items to the Contracting Officer's Representative within two business days of each ceremony. The Government-designated Product Owner shall hold final authority over product backlog prioritization and acceptance of user stories during sprint review sessions. User stories shall be written in a standard format, include clearly defined acceptance criteria, and shall be estimated using story points via planning poker or equivalent consensus-based technique. The contractor shall track and report sprint velocity, sprint burndown, and release burnup metrics using a Government-approved Agile project management platform such as Jira or Azure DevOps. At the conclusion of each sprint, the contractor shall deliver a potentially shippable product increment and conduct a live demonstration for Government stakeholders. Continuous integration and continuous delivery pipelines shall support frequent and reliable software releases consistent with Agile delivery expectations. The contractor shall document retrospective outcomes and demonstrate measurable process improvements across successive sprints as evidence of a maturing Agile practice.

The Government has determined that use of the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process is appropriate for this procurement. Technical evaluation will consist of two pass/fail subfactors: Staffing Plan Adequacy and Menu Compliance with BOP Nutritional Standards. Offerors must achieve a rating of Acceptable on both subfactors to remain in the competitive range. Price will be evaluated for all contract line items including the base year and four option periods. The Government will award a firm-fixed-price contract to the responsible offeror receiving an Acceptable technical rating and proposing the lowest total evaluated price. The Government expressly declines to conduct a best value tradeoff under this acquisition. All proposals must include completed representations and certifications and signed Standard Form 1449 to be considered responsive.

Example 2:
The Environmental Protection Agency, Office of Acquisition Solutions, is issuing this Request for Proposals for technical support services related to water quality monitoring and sampling at Superfund sites in EPA Region 5. This acquisition will be evaluated using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process in accordance with FAR 15.101-2. Technical acceptability will be evaluated under three pass/fail criteria: Key Personnel Qualifications, Laboratory Accreditation Compliance, and Quality Assurance Plan Adequacy. Proposals must be rated Acceptable on all three criteria to be eligible for award. Technical proposals that exceed minimum requirements will receive no additional consideration. Following determination of technical acceptability, award will be made to the responsible offeror submitting the lowest evaluated total price. The Government has made a written determination that LPTA is the appropriate evaluation approach given the well-defined nature of the work and the availability of qualified contractors capable of performing at minimum standards. Questions regarding the LPTA evaluation criteria must be submitted by the deadline specified in Section L.

Example 3:
This solicitation is issued by the General Services Administration, Public Buildings Service, for grounds maintenance and landscaping services at federal facilities in the National Capital Region. Source selection will be conducted using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) methodology as prescribed in FAR 15.101-2. Evaluation factors include Technical Capability and Price. Technical Capability will be rated either Acceptable or Unacceptable based on the offeror's demonstrated ability to meet the requirements of the Statement of Work. Price will not be evaluated for technical merit. Award will be made to the offeror submitting the lowest price among all technically acceptable proposals. Proposals rated Unacceptable under the Technical Capability factor will be eliminated from consideration regardless of price. The Government reserves the right to conduct discussions if necessary but intends to make award without discussions. All offerors must be registered in SAM.gov at time of offer submission.

This methodology has been selected because the Government's requirements are clearly defined, the risk of unsuccessful performance is minimal, and paying a premium for technical approaches exceeding the minimum requirements would not be in the best interest of the Government. Offerors are advised that the LPTA process does not permit tradeoffs between price and technical merit, and that proposals demonstrating capabilities beyond the stated minimums will not be evaluated more favorably than those meeting only the minimum requirements.

Offerors are advised that the scope of this engagement is configuration only, encompassing identity provider setup, multi-factor authentication policy definition, role-based access control mapping, and directory synchronization. In accordance with FAR 15.102, this solicitation incorporates oral presentations as a substitute for, or supplement to, written information for the Technical Approach evaluation factor. Offerors selected for oral presentations will receive written notification from the Contracting Officer specifying the date, time, location, and format requirements. The Department of Energy's Office of Environmental Management will evaluate the oral presentation under Subfactor 1A – Technical Understanding and Subfactor 1B – Staffing and Organization. Each offeror's presentation team must include the proposed Project Director, Deputy Project Director, and at least one subject matter expert for each major technical area described in the Statement of Work. Presentations shall not exceed one hundred and fifty (150) minutes inclusive of Government questions. Offerors are encouraged to use visual aids, technical demonstrations, or mock deliverable examples to enhance their presentations. The evaluation panel will score the oral presentation independently from written submissions, and both scores will be combined to produce the final Technical Approach rating used in the Best Value determination.

Example 2:
Offerors in the competitive range shall be required to participate in an oral presentation as part of the evaluation process. The oral presentation will be conducted at the Department of Veterans Affairs Network Contracting Office and will be scheduled at the Government's discretion. The purpose of the oral presentation is to assess each offeror's understanding of the Performance Work Statement requirements, proposed staffing model, and quality control methodology. Presentations shall be a maximum of seventy-five (75) minutes in duration, which includes a mandatory fifteen (15) minute question-and-answer session conducted by the Technical Evaluation Panel. The proposed Contract Program Manager must attend and lead the presentation. Supporting staff may participate but shall not exceed four (4) individuals total. All slide materials or visual aids must be provided to the Contracting Officer no later than three (3) business days prior to the presentation. The Government may record oral presentations for evaluation documentation purposes, and offerors will be notified accordingly.

Example 3:
The Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) intends to conduct oral presentations with offerors as part of its evaluation of proposals submitted in response to this solicitation for Environmental Monitoring and Assessment Support Services. Oral presentations will be conducted following the initial screening of written proposals. Only those offerors determined to be technically acceptable after written review will be scheduled for an oral presentation. The oral presentation will focus primarily on the offeror's technical methodology, data management approach, and the qualifications of proposed scientific personnel. Each session is limited to one hundred and twenty (120) minutes, including a thirty (30) minute question period. The proposed Principal Investigator must personally attend and lead the oral presentation. Additional team members may participate at the offeror's discretion but are subject to a five-person maximum. Materials presented must not introduce new technical approaches not previously described in the written proposal, and any inconsistencies identified will be evaluated unfavorably.

As part of this competitive acquisition, the Department of Energy (DOE), Office of Environmental Management, will incorporate an oral presentation phase into the overall proposal evaluation process. The purpose of the oral presentation is to allow offerors an opportunity to demonstrate their understanding of site-specific technical and safety requirements, discuss their management infrastructure, and present their approach to regulatory compliance and environmental remediation performance objectives. Evaluators will assess the oral presentation under the Technical Approach and Safety Management Program evaluation factors. Scores derived from the oral presentation will be documented in the evaluation record and factored into the overall best value determination alongside written proposal scores and past performance assessments.

The Division has standardized on Microsoft SharePoint Online as its enterprise collaboration platform under the Army's existing M365 enterprise agreement and requires a contractor to configure and deploy a structured knowledge repository supporting engineering project documentation, lessons learned capture, and cross-district information sharing. The contractor shall design and implement a SharePoint site collection hierarchy, configure content types, managed metadata term stores, search schema, and retention policies consistent with Army Records Management requirements. Training shall be provided for up to 60 knowledge managers across six district offices. Final deliverables include a fully operational configured SharePoint environment and comprehensive administrator and end-user documentation.

The Government intends to leverage the existing Microsoft 365 enterprise licensing agreement and does not require the development of proprietary software. Contractors shall provide SharePoint configuration services including taxonomy design, metadata schema development, site collection architecture, and integration with existing Azure Active Directory identity management. The scope explicitly excludes custom application development. Deliverables include a configured SharePoint environment, governance documentation, administrator training, and a phased migration plan for approximately 12,000 legacy content items. Offerors must demonstrate a minimum of three years of experience deploying SharePoint as a commercial solution within federal government environments and shall provide three relevant past performance references from federal civilian agencies.

Example 3:
The Department of Transportation, Federal Aviation Administration, solicits technical and price proposals for development of the Aeronautical Information Management Replacement System. Given the safety-critical nature of the system, the FAA mandates compliance with DO-178C and requires the use of traditional SDLC sequential phases throughout the program lifecycle. The contractor shall complete and obtain Government approval of all systems engineering artifacts associated with each development phase before proceeding to subsequent phases. The development sequence shall include: System Requirements Review, Software Requirements Review, Preliminary Design Review, Critical Design Review, Software Build and Integration, Software Qualification Testing, System Acceptance Testing, and Operational Readiness Review. A fixed requirements baseline shall be established following Government approval of the System and Software Requirements Specifications. The contractor shall employ rigorous phase gate controls, and no phase transition shall occur without documented Government authorization. Traceability matrices shall be maintained from requirements through test.

The contractor shall execute all software development under clearly defined sequential phases: Requirements Definition, Functional Design, Detailed Design, Software Construction, Software Testing, and System Deployment. Phase gate reviews shall be scheduled and completed at the conclusion of each phase, with a formal Government Decision Authority review required before transition to the next phase. All requirements identified during the Requirements Definition phase shall be placed under configuration control as the fixed requirements baseline. Changes to this baseline require submission of a formal Request for Change, independent cost and schedule impact assessment, and written approval from the NNSA Contracting Officer's Representative and Configuration Control Board.

Requirements established at the conclusion of the Requirements Definition phase shall constitute the fixed requirements baseline and shall be placed under configuration management control. In accordance with ATF information technology governance directives and OMB Circular A-130, the contractor shall govern all development activities using standard software lifecycle development methodology. The Government has established that the statutory and regulatory compliance requirements embedded within this system, combined with the need for formal documentation supporting future legal defensibility, necessitate sequential phase execution with comprehensive documentation artifacts at each stage. The program shall be organized into six sequential phases: Stakeholder Requirements Definition, System Architecture and Design, Detailed Software Design, Software Construction and Unit Verification, Integration and System Test, and Operational Acceptance and Deployment. A phase gate control process shall govern all phase transitions; the contractor shall prepare and submit a Phase Completion Report for each phase, and no subsequent phase activities shall commence until the Contracting Officer's Representative issues a written Authorization to Proceed. Changes to the approved requirements baseline shall be subject to the ATF Change Control Board review process and shall be incorporated only upon issuance of a bilateral contract modification. The contractor shall maintain a requirements traceability matrix and update it at each phase gate to demonstrate complete and verifiable linkage between approved requirements and associated design, code, and test artifacts.

In accordance with FAR 15.102, this solicitation incorporates oral presentations as a substitute for, or supplement to, written information for the Technical Approach evaluation factor. Offerors selected for oral presentations will receive written notification from the Contracting Officer specifying the date, time, location, and format requirements. The Department of Energy's Office of Environmental Management will evaluate the oral presentation under Subfactor 1A – Technical Understanding and Subfactor 1B – Staffing and Organization. Each offeror's presentation team must include the proposed Project Director, Deputy Project Director, and at least one subject matter expert for each major technical area described in the Statement of Work. Presentations shall not exceed one hundred and fifty (150) minutes inclusive of Government questions. Offerors are encouraged to use visual aids, technical demonstrations, or mock deliverable examples to enhance their presentations. The evaluation panel will score the oral presentation independently from written submissions, and both scores will be combined to produce the final Technical Approach rating used in the Best Value determination.

Example 2:
Section M – Evaluation Criteria: The Department of Homeland Security will evaluate all responsive proposals using a Best Value Tradeoff methodology. Following written proposal evaluation, offerors deemed within the competitive range will be invited to deliver an oral presentation to the Source Selection Evaluation Board (SSEB). The oral presentation is intended to allow offerors to clarify and elaborate upon their written technical submissions and to demonstrate the depth of understanding of the program requirements. Presentations shall be limited to sixty (60) minutes with an additional twenty (20) minutes reserved for Government questions. The lead proposed Program Manager and at least two key technical personnel identified in the proposal must participate in person. Remote participation via video teleconference will be permitted only upon written approval by the Contracting Officer. Scores assigned during the oral presentation phase will be integrated with written proposal scores to produce a final technical rating for each offeror.

Example 3:
As part of the evaluation methodology for this Information Technology Services procurement, the General Services Administration (GSA) will incorporate oral presentations into the source selection process. After an initial review of written proposals, offerors receiving a rating of Acceptable or higher under the Technical Factor will be invited to participate in an oral presentation before the Technical Evaluation Team. The oral presentation allows the Government to assess offeror capabilities beyond what is conveyed in written form and to evaluate proposed personnel directly. Each offeror will be allotted ninety (90) minutes total: sixty (60) minutes for the presentation and thirty (30) minutes for Government-directed questions. Key personnel named in Section H must be present and actively participate. Substitutions of key personnel for the oral presentation will not be permitted without prior written approval and may negatively impact the evaluation. Offerors should note that the oral presentation is evaluated separately under Factor 2 – Management Approach and scored on an Adjectival basis consistent with the criteria described in Section M.

Example 1:
The Department of Justice, Bureau of Prisons, is soliciting offers for food service management and inmate meal preparation at the Federal Correctional Institution located in Sheridan, Oregon. The Government has determined that use of the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process is appropriate for this procurement. Technical evaluation will consist of two pass/fail subfactors: Staffing Plan Adequacy and Menu Compliance with BOP Nutritional Standards. Offerors must achieve a rating of Acceptable on both subfactors to remain in the competitive range. Price will be evaluated for all contract line items including the base year and four option periods. The Government will award a firm-fixed-price contract to the responsible offeror receiving an Acceptable technical rating and proposing the lowest total evaluated price. The Government expressly declines to conduct a best value tradeoff under this acquisition. All proposals must include completed representations and certifications and signed Standard Form 1449 to be considered responsive.

Example 2:
The Social Security Administration, Office of Facilities and Supply Management, is soliciting proposals for courier and document delivery services between SSA regional offices located throughout the Mid-Atlantic region. This solicitation is issued under the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection method as outlined in FAR 15.101-2. Proposals will be evaluated on two factors: Technical Acceptability and Price. Technical Acceptability will be rated Acceptable or Unacceptable based on whether the offeror demonstrates sufficient vehicle fleet capacity, staffing plans, and route coverage to satisfy the requirements in the Performance Work Statement. No additional credit will be given for exceeding minimum requirements. Price will be evaluated based on the total price across the base year and four option years. Award will be made to the technically acceptable offeror with the lowest evaluated price. Offerors are reminded that submission of a technically acceptable proposal at the lowest price constitutes the sole basis for award determination under this LPTA competition.

Example 3:
The Environmental Protection Agency, Office of Acquisition Solutions, is issuing this Request for Proposals for technical support services related to water quality monitoring and sampling at Superfund sites in EPA Region 5. This acquisition will be evaluated using the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable (LPTA) source selection process in accordance with FAR 15.101-2. Technical acceptability will be evaluated under three pass/fail criteria: Key Personnel Qualifications, Laboratory Accreditation Compliance, and Quality Assurance Plan Adequacy. Proposals must be rated Acceptable on all three criteria to be eligible for award. Technical proposals that exceed minimum requirements will receive no additional consideration. Following determination of technical acceptability, award will be made to the responsible offeror submitting the lowest evaluated total price. The Government has made a written determination that LPTA is the appropriate evaluation approach given the well-defined nature of the work and the availability of qualified contractors capable of performing at minimum standards. Questions regarding the LPTA evaluation criteria must be submitted by the deadline specified in Section L.

This solicitation is structured as a competitive acquisition utilizing the Lowest Price Technically Acceptable, or LPTA, source selection approach. The Government has determined that LPTA is the appropriate methodology given that the requirements are clearly definable and the risk of performance failure can be adequately mitigated through the technical acceptability determination. Offers will be evaluated on two factors: Technical and Price. Under the Technical factor, the Government will assess whether the offeror's proposed staffing plan, management approach, and menu capabilities conform to all mandatory requirements specified in the Statement of Work. Evaluations will yield only Acceptable or Unacceptable ratings; adjectival ratings and color-coded assessments will not be employed. Proposals failing to address any element of the Technical factor in sufficient detail to permit an affirmative acceptability determination will be rated Unacceptable and excluded from further consideration. Price proposals submitted by technically acceptable offerors will be reviewed for reasonableness in accordance with FAR 15.404-1. The contract will be awarded to the responsible, technically acceptable offeror offering the lowest evaluated price. The Government will not pay a premium for technical merit above and beyond established minimum thresholds. Questions regarding this solicitation must be submitted in writing no later than ten calendar days prior to the proposal due date.

DEPARTMENT OF THE INTERIOR
BUREAU OF LAND MANAGEMENT
Solicitation Number: L24PX02094

The Bureau of Land Management (BLM), National Operations Center, is conducting this competitive acquisition as a total set-aside for Economically Disadvantaged Women-Owned Small Business (EDWOSB) concerns pursuant to the authority granted under the Small Business Act, 15 U.S.C. 637(m), and FAR Subpart 19.15. The BLM requires geospatial information systems (GIS) mapping and cartographic services to support public lands survey and resource management planning activities across BLM-administered lands in eleven western states. NAICS code 541370 – Surveying and Mapping (except Geophysical) Services applies, with a size standard of $19 million. In accordance with FAR 52.219-30, Notice of Set-Aside for, or Sole Source Award to, Economically Disadvantaged Women-Owned Small Business Concerns, only EDWOSB-certified firms are eligible to receive an award under this solicitation. To be eligible, the offeror must represent that it is an EDWOSB concern meeting all requirements of 13 CFR Part 127 and must provide documentation confirming current SBA certification or third-party certifier approval. The contracting officer will not consider offers from firms that are certified only as Women-Owned Small Businesses (WOSB) without the economically disadvantaged designation. Offerors are encouraged to review the eligibility criteria at certify.sba.gov prior to submitting a proposal.

Example 3:
ENVIRONMENTAL PROTECTION AGENCY
OFFICE OF WATER
SOLICITATION NUMBER: EP-W-24-017
WATER QUALITY ASSESSMENT AND TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE

This acquisition is conducted as a Women-Owned Small Business (WOSB) set-aside under the authority of 15 U.S.C. 637(m) and FAR Subpart 19.15. The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency, Office of Water, has determined through a rigorous market analysis that this industry falls within the categories designated by the Small Business Administration as underrepresented by women-owned businesses, thereby meeting the threshold requirements for a WOSB set-aside designation. Only firms that hold valid, current certification as a Women-Owned Small Business through the SBA's WOSB Federal Contract Program are eligible to compete. NAICS Code 541380 (Testing Laboratories and Services) applies; the size standard is $19.0 million. The EPA requires technical support services to assist states and tribes in conducting Clean Water Act Section 305(b) water quality assessments and Section 303(d) impaired waters listings. Deliverables include field sampling protocols, water quality data analysis, geospatial mapping products, and written technical reports. The contract vehicle will be a Time-and-Materials Indefinite Delivery/Indefinite Quantity contract with a ceiling of $12 million over five years. Minimum order: $100,000. WOSB certification status will be verified through official SBA systems prior to any award, and the Contracting Officer will document this verification in the contract file.

DeBakey VA Medical Center, 2002 Holcombe Blvd). No off-site work is authorized under this contract. Contractor employees must badge in and out daily using the facility's access control system, and all deliverables must be produced using government-furnished equipment located on the premises. The nature of the work, which includes direct patient record management and clinical data entry support, requires constant physical presence within secured medical facility zones. The scope includes physical security assessments, force-on-force exercise planning and execution, vulnerability analysis, security technology evaluation, safeguards and security training development, and regulatory compliance support. Given the classified nature of operations, the 24-hour operational tempo of the facility, and the technical complexity of nuclear security disciplines, the contractor must provide a minimum of thirteen (13) cleared personnel at all times. The required team includes a Program Director with a minimum of twenty (20) years of combined military and nuclear security experience, a Deputy Program Director, three (3) Physical Security Specialists, two (2) Force-on-Force Exercise Specialists, two (2) Safeguards and Security Analysts, a Security Technology Engineer, a Training Specialist, and two (2) Compliance Analysts. All personnel must possess active Q clearances at time of award. Offerors shall submit a staffing plan demonstrating that all thirteen positions are filled by named individuals meeting the qualification requirements set forth in Section J, Attachment 3. Personnel proposed for this effort may be subject to Government approval prior to assignment.
